title: Searching the Forensic Page
content:
The Advanced Search pane enables you to customize a search for files, hosts, users, and network traffic, based on any of the available metadata parameters. Under Advanced Search you can also customize the displayed fields in the inventory list on any Forensic page. For more information on the fields displayed by default in the Forensic page, see Default Forensic Display .

Customizing the Display

You can modify the inventory list on the forensic pages to display additional columns. To add or remove columns, select/clear the fields in the Display Fields menu, then click Search to refresh to data.
You can also sort the display by each field by clicking the arrows next to the column title. By default, the items are not sorted by any specific order.

Search Fields

You can add any field that appears in Display Fields as a search parameter in the Search Fields window (even if the field is not selected) . To add a parameter, in the Display Fields menu, click by the field name.
To search the Forensic page:
From the dropdown menu, select a logical operator for each search parameter.
Enter a value for the search parameter.
To remove a parameter from the search, click .
Click Search . The inventory list is filtered according search parameters.
The inventory list is filtered according search parameters.
Save the search by clicking Save Search , then entering a search name. Once the search is saved you can:
Once the search is saved you can:
Add it to to Favorite Searches, by clicking .
Delete it, by clicking X .
The Advanced Search page includes a Relative Time filter used to search for results within a defined number of minutes from a current time. This filter is very useful for conducting a real-time incident response and ensures that only the most relevant results are shown. To apply a Relative Time search, use the Last Seen and First Seen fields.

Exporting Data

When viewing the results of a search in the Forensics page, you have the option to export the search results as an excel file.
To export the results of a search:
Navigate to the Forensic page.
Select the tab you want to export data from.
Edit your search filters to show the results you want to export.
Click the Export button at the top right-hand side of the page.
Note: If your search returned a large number of results, the system displays only the first 10k rows of results and only these are exported to the excel file.

title: Setting Scan Concurrency
content:
Use Scan Throttle settings to control the number of concurrent scans during scan cycles in order to scan environments more efficiently.
The following settings are available under Settings > Advanced :
Max Concurrent Scanned Hosts: The maximum number of hosts the Cynet server can attempt to scan at a time. This setting throttles the scanner and prevents flooding the network with scan attempts. The default setting is 200 hosts.
Scanned Hosts Timeout: The timeout value for responses from the PsExec process when using the Remote Procedure Calls (RPC) distribution type. The default setting is 80 seconds.
Concurrent Remediation Actions: The maximum number of remediation actions the server can attempt at a time. The default setting is 300 actions.

title: Excluding Hosts from Scans
content:
You can exclude individual IP addresses, IP address ranges, or Hostnames from scanning, even if they are configured in a Group. Alternatively, from the Scanner page, you can remove the EPS from the host (consider this option if the host you want to exclude does not meet the criteria below).
Available in Cynet 360 on-premise only. Not available in hosts in which the EPS was deployed using MSI.
Navigate to Settings > Configuration > Exclusion Settings .
Enter an IP address or an IP address range into the text box. Acceptable IP and IP Range formats are: Parameter Acceptable Format Explicit IP address 192.168.0.1 IP address range 192.168.0.1-192.168.0.255 IP address with subnet prefix 192.168.0.1/24 Host name johnsmith-lp
Click Add . When a valid IP Range or CIDR range is entered, the number of addresses within that range is displayed.
When a valid IP Range or CIDR range is entered, the number of addresses within that range is displayed.
To remove an IP address or IP address range from the exclusion list, select the item, then click Delete .

title: Sending Alerts by Mail
content:
You can configure Cynet 360 to send alerts by email to specified recipients. This can help you to keep track of alerts and events that require a response, without going into the Cynet 360 console. You can filter which alerts are sent by the severity level of the alert.
To send alerts by email:
Navigate to Settings > Alerts > Local Email Settings.
Enter the email addresses, separated by a comma, to which Cynet 360 must forward alerts.
Enter the IP address of the SMTP server from which to send the emails. If you do not specify an SMPT server, the emails are sent from the Cynet SOC.
If you specified an SMTP server, you can enable SSL encryption between Cynet and SMTP servers. To do so, select the Require SSL check box.
Enter an email address that will appear as the sender.
Navigate to Email Alert Filter Settings at the bottom of the page.
Select the minimum alert severity level for sending it by email. Alerts with severity level lower than specified are not sent.
Click Save . Alert emailing is configured.
Alert emailing is configured.

title: Remediation Action Behavior
content:
When you perform a remediation action , its behavior depends on the location from which you initiated the action, and whether you execute the action on an endpoint or server:
If you perform a remediation action on an endpoint, it is executed on all the relevant endpoints, depending on the remediated entity. The action is applied depending on the page from which you executed the action: From the Alert page: The remediation action is performed on the host that triggered the alert. For example, a file remediation action is executed on a file saved on a single endpoint. From the Forensic page: The remediation action is performed on all hosts that contain the entity in that is remediated. For example, a file remediation action is executed on a file, on all endpoints that contain the file.
From the Alert page: The remediation action is performed on the host that triggered the alert. For example, a file remediation action is executed on a file saved on a single endpoint.
From the Forensic page: The remediation action is performed on all hosts that contain the entity in that is remediated. For example, a file remediation action is executed on a file, on all endpoints that contain the file.
If you perform a remediation action on an execution server, it is executed once per remediated entity.

title: Creating Policies
content:
A policy is a saved search of forensic data, that Cynet 360 performs automatically every five minutes. You can set the policy to trigger an alert if a new result is found, thus creating a custom alert. It is not recommended to save more than 100 policies.
To create a policy:
Navigate to Forensic > Advanced Search .
In Search Fields , enter search criteria and click Save Policy . A dialog box opens.
Enter a Policy Name and Policy Risk Number between 1-1000.
Select Open alert on policy match .
To create an alert for a new occurrence of an event based on your saved policy, select Reopen alert on new occurrence .
From the dropdown list, select the Alert Severity .
Click OK . The saved policy appears under Saved Policies. You can apply the saved policy to a new search by selecting it from the Saved Policies list.
To delete a saved policy, click X next to the policy name.
Objects that match the policy criteria appear in the object Details page as a triggered indicator. The risk value you entered when creating the policy is factored into the new risk level for the object.
If the saved policy is configured to create an alert when the policy is matched, the alert is displayed in the Alerts page. The name of the alert is the policy name you entered.
If you close a Policy Alert and an identical alert is triggered again, the alert does not reopen, unless it is a file alert, with High or Critical severity. Click here for more information about alert status.

title: Agent Uninstall Password
content:
An anti-tampering feature protects the Cynet agent from being uninstalled without authorization. When enabled, the user is required to enter a password when removing the Cynet agent directly from the endpoint. By default, an uninstall password is enabled on all protected endpoints. You can change the initial password or disable this feature at any time.
This feature is only available in Windows endpoints, in which the agent was deployed using MSI.
You can set an uninstall password per Group or for the entire site.
An uninstall password set for a Group overrides an uninstall password set for the site.
To change an agent uninstall password for the entire site:
Navigate to Settings > Advanced and scroll down to the EPS Uninstall Password section. A notice is displayed, notifying you how many endpoints have an updated MSI installer, which is required for this feature.
Enter a new uninstall password.
Enter the password again for conformation.
Click Save .
To change an agent uninstall password for a single group:
Navigate to Settings > Groups and edit the specific group.
Under Agent Settings , click Manage .
Toggle Set an Agent Uninstall Password to On .
Enter an uninstall password.
Click Ok .

title: Monitoring Critical Endpoints and Groups
content:
Cynet 360 enables you to closely monitor critical endpoints and Groups and trigger alerts if these aren't scanned.
If an endpoint in the list of critical endpoints is not scanned in the set period, Cynet 360 generates an alert. For SaaS , the set period in 1.5 hours. For on-premise customers, the set time interval for scanning a critical endpoint is 1.5X(the scan interval set for the Group). For example, in a Group that is set to be scanned every 20 minutes, an alert is generated if a critical host is not scanned for 30 minutes.
To add an endpoint to the list of critical endpoints, navigate to Settings > Alerts > Unscanned Host Alert Settings .
To trigger alerts for un-scanned Groups, navigate to Settings > Groups and select the check box:

title: EPP Alerts
content:
Endpoint Protection Platform (EPP) alerts allow Cynet to monitor, detect, and prevent attacks, exploits and many other risks to endpoints in your organization.
This video explains the alerts related to the Antivirus mechanism.
This video explains the alerts related to the EPP mechanism.

EPP Alert List

The following table lists the Cynet 360 alerts that can be triggered when the EPP category is turned on in the Group settings. You can also see additional explanation about these alerts the videos above, as well as in the following link: EPP Alerts . The information provided in the console for several of the alerts includes information about the associated MITRE ATT&CK tactic and technique. These alerts are marked with the ATT&CK icon.

title: How to Integrate Cynet 360 with N-able
content:
Integrate Cynet 360 with N-able and add security to your RMM platform. Use N-able to check if the Cynet agent is installed and running, as well as viewing Cynet alerts as tickets in the MSP Manager.
This integration guide includes two sections:
Adding Cynet to the N-able monitoring dashboard. This includes monitoring two Cynet processes: CynetEPS and CynetMS.
Configuring alert processing from Cynet to N-able MSP Manager.

Monitoring The Cynet Agent

To add Cynet to the N-able Monitoring Dashboard:
Log in to your N-able account.
Navigate to Devices > Settings > Monitoring Templates > Manage Templates . A dialog box opens.
A dialog box opens.
Click Add.
For Template Name, enter CynetEPS .
Enter the frequency in which you want N-able to check the CynetEPS process.
Under Checks and Tasks , click Add . The Script Check dialog box opens.
The Script Check dialog box opens.
Select the script Running Process .
Select the behavior Pass if process is running .
For Name of Process, enter CynetEPS.exe .
Enter a number of seconds until the script times out.
Click OK . The script is added to the list of checks.
The script is added to the list of checks.
Click OK . The process CynetEPS is added to the Monitoring Dashboard.
The process CynetEPS is added to the Monitoring Dashboard.
Repeat steps 3-12 for the process CynetMS.exe.
Repeat steps 3-12 for the process CynetMS.exe.
To apply the monitoring templates to a device:
Navigate to your monitored servers/workstations and right-click the device on which to apply the monitoring template.
Select Monitoring Templates > Apply Monitoring Template and select the Cynet EPS and Cynet MS templates you created.
Click Apply .
Enter your password to confirm the action.

Configuring Incoming Email Processing

Using Incoming Email Processing you can automatically create a ticket in your N-able MSP Manager from a Cynet Alert. To configure this feature, you must also configure Cynet to send alerts to a specific email address.
To configure Incoming Email Processing:
Log in to your N-able account.
Navigate to Settings > Tickets & Workflows > Ticket Mailboxes .
Click Add IMAP Mailbox . For automatic ticket creation, N-able creates a mailbox. Alerts are sent to this mailbox, then automatically converted to a ticket.
Enter an email and server for the mailbox. The full email address appears below. You can configure additional settings below. Note for Global users: Every mailbox is associate with a single account. If you manage multiple accounts/sites, you must create a different mailbox for each Cynet site.
You can configure additional settings below.
Note for Global users: Every mailbox is associate with a single account. If you manage multiple accounts/sites, you must create a different mailbox for each Cynet site.
Click .
To configure Cynet 360 to send alerts to by email:
In the Cynet UI, Navigate to Settings > Alerts > Local Email Alert Recipients.
For Local Email Alert Recipient , enter the email address of the IMAP mailbox you created.
Click Save. Alerts are sent to the N-able mailbox for ticket creation.
Alerts are sent to the N-able mailbox for ticket creation.

title: How to Integrate Cynet 360 with AWS S3
content:
You can integrate Cynet 360 with AWS Simple Storage Service (S3) to store alert information and audit logs.
To store information in AWS S3:
Configure the S3 settings.
Configure the specific bucket in which data will be stored.
Enable the Cynet server to send alerts and audit logs to S3.
You can send alerts and audit logs to multiple AWS S3 instances and buckets.
You must create an AWS S3 instance before setting up the integration with Cynet 360.

Configuring AWS S3

To configure integration with a S3:
Navigate to Settings > Configuration . The screen displays existing S3 integrations.
To create a new S3 integration, In the AWS S3 Settings row, click Create .
Enter a name for the S3 integration.
Enter the access and secret keys for AWS S3 in which you want to store the data.
Select the region in which the S3 instance is located.
If you want to forward the data a third-party service that is integrated with AWS S3, enter the third-party Service URL (optional).
Click Save .
The S3 integration is set up. You can edit or delete this instance at any time by clicking Edit or Delete in the instance row.
You must now specify the S3 bucket in which to store data.
To specify an S3 bucket:
In the AWS S3 Bucket Settings row, click Create.
Enter a name for the S3 bucket integration.
Enter the bucket name. The bucket name must be between 3 and 63 characters long. Bucket names can consist only of lowercase letters, numbers, dots (.), and hyphens (-). Bucket names must begin and end with a letter or number.
Enter the path to the location within the bucket in which to store the data. The path must contain at least three characters.
Enter an expiration time (in days) for objects in the bucket. The expiration time specifies the number of days after which objects are deleted from the S3 bucket. The default value is zero (never delete).
Specify whether to encrypt the data. By default, encryption algorithm AES256 is applied. If you are using a third-party service, refer to its documentation to find out if encryption is required.
Specify whether to compress the data before storing it in the S3 bucket. By default, data is compressed .
Click Save .
The S3 bucket integration is set up. You can edit or delete this bucket at any time by clicking Edit or Delete in the instance row.

Enabling Cynet 360 to Send Data

To enable the Cynet server to send audit logs to S3:
Navigate to Settings > Advanced .
Select the Enable Sending Audit Records to AWS S3 check box. Two fields appear.
Two fields appear.
From the dropdown lists, select the S3 and S3 bucket to which the Cynet server must send the audit records. You can add additional S3 and S3 buckets by clicking the + on the right.
Click Save.
Sending audit logs is enabled. You can disable sending records at any time by clearing the check box.
To enable the Cynet server to send alerts to S3:
Navigate to Settings > Alerts > Alerts Compliance Settings .
Select the Enable Sending Alerts to AWS S3 check box. Two field appear.
Two field appear.
From the dropdown lists, select the S3 and S3 bucket to which the Cynet server must send the audit records. You can add additional S3 and S3 buckets by clicking the + on the right.
Click Save.
Sending alerts is enabled. You can disable sending alerts at any time by clearing the check box.

Enter the access and secret keys for AWS S3 in which you want to store the data.
Select the region in which the S3 instance is located.
If you want to forward the data a third-party service that is integrated with AWS S3, enter the third-party Service URL (optional).
Click Save .
The S3 integration is set up. You can edit or delete this instance at any time by clicking Edit or Delete in the instance row.
You must now specify the S3 bucket in which to store data.

Specify whether to compress the data before storing it in the S3 bucket. By default, data is compressed .
Click Save .
The S3 bucket integration is set up. You can edit or delete this bucket at any time by clicking Edit or Delete in the instance row.

title: Network Detection
content:
To unveil network attacks and help you investigate suspicious activity, Cynet 360 collects forensic data regarding network activity and correlates it with information collected from the endpoints in your network.
There are several places to view network data in the Cynet 360 console:
Data on network-related process actions (such as Connection Established ).
Socket and domain data from all protected endpoints.
The Traffic tab in the Host Forensic page, that details all the domains an endpoint communicated with.
Cynet 360 triggers alerts for suspicious network activity observed on your network. Enable/disable these alerts using the Network Attack Detection check box in the Group Settings page.

title: Exclusion Profiles
content:
An Exclusion Profile enables you to specify trusted items (processes, directories, files or extensions) that are excluded from detection during a scan. Exclusion profiles can be applied to a wide set of parameters and are useful for third-party applications running on your environment.
Process: All process activities are excluded.
Directory: All activities within the directory and its sub-directories are excluded (read, write, etc.).
File: All activities in the file are excluded (read, write, etc.).
Extension: All activities in files with this extension are excluded read, write etc.).
Note: System processes should never be added to an exclusion profile. For more information on system processes, click here .
The Exclusions screen displays the list of saved exclusion profiles.
To create an exclusion profile:
Navigate to Setting > Allowlist & Exclusions > Exclusions. Note that you can exclude folders and files from here as well.
Click New .
Enter a profile name.
Under Exclusion Rules : From the Platform Type dropdown, select the operating system on which to apply the rule. From the Type dropdown, select an item type: Process Path, Process Command Line, Directory, File, Extension, IP/IP Range, or Domain. Enter the path to the process/directory/file, or the extension, IP/IP range, or domain, and click + . You can enter the full path, or a partial path using wildcards (wildcards are not supported in the first character). Supported wildcards include asterisk ( * ) and question mark ( ? ). A process can also be excluded by its command line. When excluding a process, you can also apply the exclusion rule to any child process it spawns by selecting the Propagate button.
From the Platform Type dropdown, select the operating system on which to apply the rule.
From the Type dropdown, select an item type: Process Path, Process Command Line, Directory, File, Extension, IP/IP Range, or Domain.
Enter the path to the process/directory/file, or the extension, IP/IP range, or domain, and click + . You can enter the full path, or a partial path using wildcards (wildcards are not supported in the first character). Supported wildcards include asterisk ( * ) and question mark ( ? ). A process can also be excluded by its command line. When excluding a process, you can also apply the exclusion rule to any child process it spawns by selecting the Propagate button.
A process can also be excluded by its command line.
When excluding a process, you can also apply the exclusion rule to any child process it spawns by selecting the Propagate button.
Select the Groups to which the rule must apply. If you do not select a Group, the rule will apply to all Groups. You can select multiple Groups from the list.
Select the endpoints to which the rule must apply. If you do not select an endpoint, the rule will apply to all endpoints. You can select multiple endpoints from the list.
Enter a profile description.
Click Save.
The profile is added to the list of exclusion profiles.
Note: The value in an exclusion rule is case sensitive.

Exporting Exclusion Profiles

To export Exclusion profiles:
Navigate to Settings > Allowlist & Exclusions > Exclusions .
Click on the three dots menu icon at the right-hand side of the screen.
Click Export . The JSON file is downloaded.
Exporting includes all Exclusion profiles that exist on the current site. It includes all the values contained in the following fields:
Profile Name
Profile Description
Platform type
Type
Value
Propagate
Note: When exporting profiles, details of the Hosts or Groups to which a profile was applied are not included.
To import an Exclusion Profile:
Navigate to Settings > Allowlist & Exclusions > Exclusions .
Click on the three dots menu icon at the right-hand side of the screen.
Click Import .
Drag and drop a file onto the import box, or click Browse to select a file from your computer.
Choose whether to keep existing profiles with the same name, or replace existing profiles with the same name.
Click Continue . The profiles are imported and applied to all groups.
Note: Since details of the Hosts or Groups to which a profile was applied are not included when exporting profiles, when importing, the profiles will be applied to all Groups.

Exclusion Example

Some applications, such as Zoom, are commonly used in organizations and it is recommended to create exclusion profiles for them. To create an exclusion profile for Zoom:
If you decide to use an additional antivirus together with Cynet 360, you must create an exclusion profile for it. E.g., an exclusion profile for the ESET directory to prevent alerts:

Exclusion Profiles in Global View

Global operators can create exclusion profiles that apply to all environments in the Global Manager, or to specific sites.
The Global tab displays the list of exclusion profiles that apply to all sites. To create an exclusion profile that applies to all environments, click Create .
To create an exclusion profile that applies to all environments, click Create .
The Site tab displays the list of exclusion profiles that apply to a specific site. To create an exclusion profile for a specific site, navigate to the individual site dashboard.
To create an exclusion profile for a specific site, navigate to the individual site dashboard.

title: How to Configure Logstash for Log Management
content:
To enable data transfer from certain data sources to Cynet Log Management, you must install Logstash and configure the data source. Cynet provides a Logstash Installer for Windows and Linux, which you can download from the console.
MSSPs that use Groups to manage different customers, can configure a separate Logstash server for each Group. By adding a Group tag to the config file, events appear in CLM with the corresponding Group name, allowing you to easily correlate each event with its source. See the details in the Configuration Changes section below.

Requirements for the Logstash Server

Windows Server 2016 and above/ Linux Debian
4GB RAM
At least 16GB disk space
TCP or UDP access from the data source to the Logstash server over the required port.
English-language operating system.

Versions of Logstash Installed Prior to May 2023

Some CLM customers are using older versions of Logstash that do not get automatic updates. These updates are important, so please make sure that you have upgraded to the latest version, as indicated in the requirements above.

Please Note:

If you are not sure which version you have, there is no harm in doing the manual update anyway.
This upgrade only needs to be done once, and then you will get automatic updates.
Also note:
You will need to back up config prior to upgrading.
You should read the README file afterwards, as it contains important instructions with new ways of doing config.
Please contact Cynet Support for additional assistance.
Firewall Configuration

Logstash Installation

Use these instructions to obtain the Cynet installation package.
From the downloaded folder, open LogstashInstaller, and select Windows or Linux.
Follow the instructions in README file.

Post-Installation

Verify that the installation was successful:
A new cynet_logstash service is created and running.
All files exist in the newly created folder c:\cynet-logstash.
Windows installer only: The Installer creates and maintains Windows Host Firewall rules to allow incoming traffic on the Logstash ports, including custom ports you may have added.
It is recommended to use the default ports and avoid editing the configuration. if you must, edit the the config.json file, and make sure the json format is valid.
If you made changes to the configuration, you must restart the cynet_logstash service.
Cynet may, occasionally, update the configuration remotely. This does not require any action on your behalf. If you made any configuration changes, they will be preserved during the update.
[Optional:] To associate the incoming data from the Logstash server to a specific Group, a dd 'group' to the root level of config.json: "group": "My Group"

Troubleshooting

Turn off Windows Host Firewall on the Logstash server as it may block incoming traffic (Tools such as Wireshark may still show incoming traffic). If turning off Windows Host Firewall is not possible, create firewall rules to allow incoming traffic on the relevant port.
Using common networking tools such as Ping or Telnet, ensure network connectivity between the data source and Logstash server over the specific IP address and port for your data source.
Ensure network connectivity between the Logstash server and the Cynet SaaS service endpoint (e.g., communication could be blocked by organizational firewall).
Verify that the destination of log forwarding from the data source is correctly configured to the IP address of the Logstash server. Remember that IP address may change (e.g. when the Logstash server restarts).
Ensure that the port configured in the data source is the same as the port in the Logstash config's UDP or TCP port.
Verify that messages are being sent to the target port in the correct format by capture messages using Wireshark. When using the CEF input plugin, make sure the message contain the CEF: signature.
Modifying the Logstash config file must be performed carefully and only as needed. Save a backup of last working config file.
Modifying the Logstash config file must be performed carefully and only as needed. Save a backup of last working config file.
If the Logstash service is not running, check the logstash-plain.log log file for errors.
If the Logstash service is not running, check the logstash-plain.log log file for errors.
If the Logstash server reaches 100% CPU, it may indicate that the Logstash service is stuck in a crash loop. This is often due to config file parsing errors. You can confirm this by checking the logstash-plain.log log file for the SIGINT message: Or search for the keyword ConfigurationError. Revert back to last working config file.
If the Logstash server reaches 100% CPU, it may indicate that the Logstash service is stuck in a crash loop. This is often due to config file parsing errors. You can confirm this by checking the logstash-plain.log log file for the SIGINT message:
Or search for the keyword ConfigurationError. Revert back to last working config file.

sections.text: Some CLM customers are using older versions of Logstash that do not get automatic updates. These updates are important, so please make sure that you have upgraded to the latest version, as indicated in the requirements above.

sections.heading: Please Note:
sections.text:
If you are not sure which version you have, there is no harm in doing the manual update anyway.
This upgrade only needs to be done once, and then you will get automatic updates.
Also note:
You will need to back up config prior to upgrading.
You should read the README file afterwards, as it contains important instructions with new ways of doing config.
Please contact Cynet Support for additional assistance.
Firewall Configuration

sections.heading: Logstash Installation
sections.text:
Use these instructions to obtain the Cynet installation package.
From the downloaded folder, open LogstashInstaller, and select Windows or Linux.
Follow the instructions in README file.

sections.heading: Post-Installation
sections.text:
Verify that the installation was successful:
A new cynet_logstash service is created and running.
All files exist in the newly created folder c:\cynet-logstash.
Windows installer only: The Installer creates and maintains Windows Host Firewall rules to allow incoming traffic on the Logstash ports, including custom ports you may have added.
It is recommended to use the default ports and avoid editing the configuration. if you must, edit the the config.json file, and make sure the json format is valid.
If you made changes to the configuration, you must restart the cynet_logstash service.
Cynet may, occasionally, update the configuration remotely. This does not require any action on your behalf. If you made any configuration changes, they will be preserved during the update.
[Optional:] To associate the incoming data from the Logstash server to a specific Group, a dd 'group' to the root level of config.json: "group": "My Group"

sections.heading: Troubleshooting
sections.text:
Turn off Windows Host Firewall on the Logstash server as it may block incoming traffic (Tools such as Wireshark may still show incoming traffic). If turning off Windows Host Firewall is not possible, create firewall rules to allow incoming traffic on the relevant port.
Using common networking tools such as Ping or Telnet, ensure network connectivity between the data source and Logstash server over the specific IP address and port for your data source.
Ensure network connectivity between the Logstash server and the Cynet SaaS service endpoint (e.g., communication could be blocked by organizational firewall).
Verify that the destination of log forwarding from the data source is correctly configured to the IP address of the Logstash server. Remember that IP address may change (e.g. when the Logstash server restarts).
Ensure that the port configured in the data source is the same as the port in the Logstash config's UDP or TCP port.
Verify that messages are being sent to the target port in the correct format by capture messages using Wireshark. When using the CEF input plugin, make sure the message contain the CEF: signature.
Modifying the Logstash config file must be performed carefully and only as needed. Save a backup of last working config file.
Modifying the Logstash config file must be performed carefully and only as needed. Save a backup of last working config file.
If the Logstash service is not running, check the logstash-plain.log log file for errors.
If the Logstash service is not running, check the logstash-plain.log log file for errors.
If the Logstash server reaches 100% CPU, it may indicate that the Logstash service is stuck in a crash loop. This is often due to config file parsing errors. You can confirm this by checking the logstash-plain.log log file for the SIGINT message: Or search for the keyword ConfigurationError. Revert back to last working config file.
If the Logstash server reaches 100% CPU, it may indicate that the Logstash service is stuck in a crash loop. This is often due to config file parsing errors. You can confirm this by checking the logstash-plain.log log file for the SIGINT message:
Or search for the keyword ConfigurationError. Revert back to last working config file.

title: How to Configure Webhooks for Log Management
content:
Use webhooks to send logs from most SaaS applications to the Cynet 360 Log management.
To configure a webhook (e.g. in Zoom):
Navigate to Zoom Marketplace.
Select app type Webhook only.
For App Name, enter Cynet, and fill-in the rest of the required fields.
In the left navigation pane, click Feature .
Click Add new event subscription .
Enter the details as they appear below, then click Add Events .
Select the events you want to send to Cynet's Log Management, then click Done .
Click Save . For more information, click here .

title: VMRay - Send to Analysis
content:
The Send to VMRay remediatrion action enables you to send any file, directly from the Cynet 360 console, to VMRay for analysis. If the conclusion of the VMRay analysis is that the file is malicious, an alert is triggered.
This integration is available for on-premise customers, who are VMRay users.
To send a file to VMRay for analysis:
Contact Cynet Support. You will be required to provide to following details: VMRay API key (to generate one, see below). Cynet username and Password Port for communication with the server (preferably port 5555) The Cynet team will contact you to install the required component on your server, then add the remediation action Send to VMRay to you Cynet 360 console.
VMRay API key (to generate one, see below).
Cynet username and Password
Port for communication with the server (preferably port 5555) The Cynet team will contact you to install the required component on your server, then add the remediation action Send to VMRay to you Cynet 360 console.
From a File alert, or after selecting a file in the Forensic page, click .
In the Action pane on the right, click the Remediation tab and select Send to VMRay . The file is sent to VMRay for analysis. In the Actions page you can view the results of the analysis: Malicious, suspicious, or legitimate. If the conclusion of the analysis is that the file is malicious, an alert is triggered.
The file is sent to VMRay for analysis. In the Actions page you can view the results of the analysis: Malicious, suspicious, or legitimate. If the conclusion of the analysis is that the file is malicious, an alert is triggered.
To generate a VMRay API key:
Navigate to https://cloud.vmray.com and enter your VMRay credentials.
From the top action bar, select Analysis Settings .
Click API KEYS .
Enter a name for the new key, then click Save . The generated API key is displayed. To view it again, click Show Key.
The generated API key is displayed. To view it again, click Show Key.

title: Map View
content:
The Map view in Cynet 360 displays the locations of your organization's hosts on a map.
To configure the Map view:
Navigate to Settings > Maps .
On the map, click the country where your organization's hosts are located. The Edit Coordinate dialog box opens:
The Edit Coordinate dialog box opens:
Enter a region name.
Enter the IP range of the hosts, and then click Save . Note that each host that sends a heartbeat is displayed on the map.

title: Investigating Files
content:
The Forensic page Files tab provides visibility into all scanned files in the organization and enables you to obtain file-specific information. To customize or search the File list, see Searching the Forensic Page .
Select the file you want to investigate.
The File page provides information that Cynet 360 collected about the selected file. At the top of the page, a timeline describes the lifecycle of the file. Below, you can see how the file is related to other entities in the organization. All the displayed objects are clickable.
The lower section of the File page displays multiple tabs providing detailed information about this file.
A tab does not appear in the File page if there is no relevant data to be displayed for that category.
Metadata about the file, such as file size, path, publisher and hashes.
The Details tab includes a file status, which you can change manually. This status affects alerts generated by the Threat Intelligence mechanism only.
Alerts associated with this file. The relevant remediation action tools are associated with each alert.
Every instance of a file throughout the environment. If a specific file is executed on two hosts, there are two entries in the Occurrences tab.
To view additional information on each process occurrence, click + .
Each occurrence includes details on how the file ran during the instance, including the running user, command-line, and the path from which the file was run.
Hosts on which the file is saved.
Users that opened the file.
Information collected during the static analysis of the file.
Information collected and generated from a sandboxed execution of the file in the Cynet SSE.
Network traffic generated to and from this file.
Domains queried by this process to initiate network traffic.
DLL files loaded by this process.

title: How to Investigate an Alert
content:
This article explains how to approach and respond when you see a new alert in Cynet 360.

What is an alert?

An alert is a recorded event that occurred on an endpoint in your environment and matched one of Cynet's detection rules. Several detection rules can correspond to a single alert type, which indicates a specific malicious activity. See the following link for an overview of Cynet's most common alerts to customers .
The investigation steps are displayed in the flow and detailed below:

Investigation

Break the alert down to the entities that were affected (file/network/user/host) and investigate the relevant telemetry data in the Forensic page. Cynet collects extensive forensic information to help you investigate the incident. There are several important details to check:
The forensic timeline: In addition to the involved entities and alerts, you should check when the user performing the action was created. It is highly suspicious if the user was created just before the event occurred.
Process tree: The main objective is to understand what caused the incident in question and what are the resulting events. Deepen your investigation by exploring the process. For more information, see Investigating Processes . Can the parent process shed some light on the legitimacy of the activity?
From the File/Process/Host/User page, check if other alerts were triggered for this entity. It is important to understand if this event is part of a larger attack or if this is a single event.
The file hash in VirusTotal (if the event involves a file)
You can use the information in the alert description to get a better understanding of its urgency. Desired/Actual EPS prevention details the actions the EPS performed to eliminate the threat or would have perform if Prevention mode was enabled.
For a detailed understanding and training about Cynet alerts, see the following videos.
This video explains the alerts related to the Antivirus mechanism.
This video explains the alerts related to the EPP mechanism.
This video explains the alerts related to the NDR mechanism.

Conclusions and Actions

By now you have concluded whether the event is malicious or safe. Each conclusion requires a different course of action. If you are still not sure about the nature of this event, The CyOps team is available 24/7 to assist.
If the alert was triggered following legitimate activity, you should prevent it from being triggered again by allowlisting or excluding the file/process. Too many false-positive alerts may obscure real alerts that require your attention.
Exclusions are used for processes and paths that generate a variety of alerts and that their activity is legitimate in its entirety . The Cynet agent does not scan excluded entities at all, so this option must be used with caution. Exclusions are useful for other security tools.
Allowlisting is configured per alert and is useful, for example, for an installation file of a known program. The allowlisting profile for this file would be applied to File Dumped on Disk or Attempt to Run alerts. The Cynet agent will still trigger an alert if the executable does something out of the ordinary, like attempt to access the memory of another process.
If the alert was triggered by malicious activity, take action immediately to eliminate the threat and reduce the damage to your environment. There are many remediation actions available in Cynet 360. For more information, see Remediating Alerts . Cynet 360 also includes the ability to create custom remediation actions , which allow you to run any script in response to a specific alert.
You can also configure auto-remediation rules that will be performed automatically when an alert is triggered. E.g., you can set an auto-remediation to rule to delete the file whenever a File Dumped on Disc alert is triggered.
Remediation actions are important for keeping your environment protected, but are performed in hindsight. Activate Prevention mode to stop malicious activity before it occurs.

Automated Investigation and Response

Cynet's Playbooks enable you to automate a cyber breach response. Playbooks define the investigation steps to be automatically taken for any alert type and can include remediation actions based on findings. A Playbook can be automatically triggered by an alert or other condition to uncover and remediate the full security incident associated with an alert.
Once you have remediated the alert, you can change its status to Closed.

title: Investigating Processes
content:
Cynet 360 collects data about individual process occurrences, making it easy to investigate and dig deep into suspicious activity. This article explains what information is displayed in a Process Occurrence page, and gives you optional directions of inquiry to help you gather additional details.
All the information in the Process page refers to a specific occurrence of the process, as opposed to the File page, which refers to the process in general.
The process occurrence page includes the following tabs:
Information about a specific occurrence of the process. Note the following important details:
Command line parameters
Running User and Owner - see also in Users tab
Parent Occurrence ID (the process that spawned this process)
Running User - the privileges with which the process ran
Owner - the owner of the file on the disk

Action Parameters

The following table lists some of the displayed fields for File Monitor actions. To view this information, click + in the action row.
Field
Description
To investigate what happened in the time between occurrences that do not have an ancestry relationship, you can navigate to the Host forensic page > File Monitor tab. This will provide visibility to additional events that occurred, which could help you complete the puzzle.

title: Automated Protection - Playbooks
content:
Playbooks are available to SaaS customers only.
Playbooks enable you to automate a cyberbreach response. By creating a Playbook , you can transform your security operations to scalable, automated workflows. The investigation and remediation flow can include 3rd party integrations, forensic data and automation capabilities.
Playbooks define the investigation steps to be automatically taken for any alert type and can include remediation actions based on findings. A Playbook can be automatically triggered by an alert or other condition to uncover and remediate the full security incident associated with an alert. In future versions, you will be able to initiate a playbook manually.
Every playbook workflow is graphically represented in the Cynet Incident View , showing investigation steps, findings and remediation actions along with a detailed timeline.
Cynet provides pre-built Automated Response Playbooks , as well as an intuitive drag-and-drop playbook builder that enables security analysts to quickly and easily build custom playbooks based on their company’s particular needs.
The operation mode of playbooks is determined by EDR settings in the Group Settings page: Disable : Playbooks are turned off and will not perform an autonomous investigation when an alert is triggered Detect : Playbooks are turned on and will perform an autonomous investigation when an alert is triggered. Playbook actions will remain pending until you approve them. Prevent : Playbooks are turned on and will perform an autonomous investigation when an alert is triggered. Playbook actions will be executed

Limitations

For every alert type you can activate only one playbook at a time. Alternatively, you can create more than one branch within a playbook. Different branches of a playbook run in parallel.
You can not run a playbook on an alert that was already triggered. A new playbook will run when a new alert is triggered.

title: Playbook Modules
content:
The following article explains the various modules available in the Playbook Canvas .
All modules are based on dynamic parameters: Parameters the value of which is calculated at runtime, individually for every playbook run.
All operations include the field Display Name, which is the name of the step as it appears on the Canvas and in the Incident View. Enter a descriptive name so you can easily follow the flow of the playbook.
Most operations include the field Result Name, to help you recognize the results of the specific step further down in the investigation (will appear as dynamic parameters under the Display Name).
File Action: Select from the list of actions that can be performed by a process. For information on the different actions, see Investigating Processes .
Search Params: Define the file attributes that you want to investigate. AND is applied between parameters.
Type: Select the entity for which to get the associated alerts: File SHA256, IP or domain, or host name
Entity value: after selecting type, specify the exact value.
File SHA256
Process Occurrence ID
Process Occurrence ID
Iterate On: Define the array on which to perform the next investigation steps.
Param Name: The parameter within the collected result that you want to continue investigating
Operator
Value
The JSON string (typically the response of an API call)
The items you want to retrieve from the JSON
Type: Select the type of object that is the result of the investigation
Resource: Enter the parameter to display as the investigation finding
Reason: Enter a conclusion for your investigation, i.e. why the findings are malicious. You can also include dynamic parameters.
Host Name: Enter the name of the endpoint on which the action is performed.
File, user, and network actions require additional input about the entity.
Recipient Name: The address of the email recipient
Subject: The subject of the email that is sent
Message: The body of the email that is sent
Alert ID
Comment
Alert ID
Depends on the parameters passed from the parent playbook.

title: How to Send Syslog to IBM Security QRadar SIEM
content:
When setting up syslog transfer between Cynet 360 and IBM Security QRadar SIEM you must perform the following steps:
Configure Cynet 360 to send syslog to QRadar. See instructions here .
Verify that QRadar is receiving the logs.
Create a new Log Source in QRadar, and map log properties and events so that Cynet 360 logs are interpreted correctly.
Map events and configure Cynet 360 as a Log Source.

To verify that QRadar is receiving logs:

Log into QRadar as root over SSH. If the syslog destination is different appliance, such as an event collector, use SSH to the event collector.
Run the following commands: tcpdump -nnAs0 -i eth0 host <host_IP> and port 514 <host_IP> is the IPv4 address or hostname of the Cynet 360 server sending the logs to QRadar Replace eth0 with the name of the QRadar network interface.
<host_IP> is the IPv4 address or hostname of the Cynet 360 server sending the logs to QRadar
Replace eth0 with the name of the QRadar network interface.
Logs are displayed in the SSH session.

To create a new Log Source and map properties:

Log in to the IBM QRadar UI and navigate to the Admin menu.
Select DSM Editor , then click Create new .
For Log Source Type enter CynetServer, then click select . The Log Source CynetServer is created.
In the newly created log source, navigate to the Properties Tab and click + . You must create a new Custom Property Definition for every field you want to map from the log.
Click Create New .
Enter a name for the Custom Property Definition and click Save . See a table of properties below. A new Custom Property Definition is created.
Select the Custom Property Definition you created.
Select Expression Type CEF and enter the corresponding expression from the log.
Repeat steps 6-8 for each property in the following table: Custom Property Name Expression Value Example Header Custom Properties cynet_cefversion $cefversion$ CEF:0 cynet_vendor $vendor$ Cynet cynet_product $product$ Cynet 360 cynet_version $version$ 4.2.11.10895 cynet_id $id$ NA cynet_name $name$ Process Monitoring cynet_severity $severity$ 8 External Custom Properties Event ID externalId 147 cynet_ clientId clientId 100500 Log message example: CEF:0|Cynet|Cynet 360|4.2.11.10895|0|Process Monitoring|8| externalId=147 clientId=100500
Navigate to the Event Mapping Tab and click + .

To configure Cynet 360 as a Log Source:

From Admin > Data Sources and select Log Sources .
Select the following parameters: Log source type: Universal CEF Protocol type: Syslog
Enter the following Log Source Parameters: Name: Cynet360 Description: Cynet360 server Enabled: Yes Group: select the desired group Target event collector: select the log collector that receives the logs Log source identifier: Cynet server IP address
Click Deploy Changes . Once the configuration is deployed you can view the parsing results in the Log Activity tab.

When setting up syslog transfer between Cynet 360 and IBM Security QRadar SIEM you must perform the following steps:
Configure Cynet 360 to send syslog to QRadar. See instructions here .
Verify that QRadar is receiving the logs.
Create a new Log Source in QRadar, and map log properties and events so that Cynet 360 logs are interpreted correctly.
Map events and configure Cynet 360 as a Log Source.

sections.heading: To verify that QRadar is receiving logs:
sections.text:
Log into QRadar as root over SSH. If the syslog destination is different appliance, such as an event collector, use SSH to the event collector.
Run the following commands: tcpdump -nnAs0 -i eth0 host <host_IP> and port 514 <host_IP> is the IPv4 address or hostname of the Cynet 360 server sending the logs to QRadar Replace eth0 with the name of the QRadar network interface.
<host_IP> is the IPv4 address or hostname of the Cynet 360 server sending the logs to QRadar
Replace eth0 with the name of the QRadar network interface.
Logs are displayed in the SSH session.

Select DSM Editor , then click Create new .
For Log Source Type enter CynetServer, then click select . The Log Source CynetServer is created.
In the newly created log source, navigate to the Properties Tab and click + . You must create a new Custom Property Definition for every field you want to map from the log.
Click Create New .
Enter a name for the Custom Property Definition and click Save . See a table of properties below. A new Custom Property Definition is created.
Select the Custom Property Definition you created.
Select Expression Type CEF and enter the corresponding expression from the log.
Repeat steps 6-8 for each property in the following table: Custom Property Name Expression Value Example Header Custom Properties cynet_cefversion $cefversion$ CEF:0 cynet_vendor $vendor$ Cynet cynet_product $product$ Cynet 360 cynet_version $version$ 4.2.11.10895 cynet_id $id$ NA cynet_name $name$ Process Monitoring cynet_severity $severity$ 8 External Custom Properties Event ID externalId 147 cynet_ clientId clientId 100500 Log message example: CEF:0|Cynet|Cynet 360|4.2.11.10895|0|Process Monitoring|8| externalId=147 clientId=100500
Navigate to the Event Mapping Tab and click + .

From Admin > Data Sources and select Log Sources .
Select the following parameters: Log source type: Universal CEF Protocol type: Syslog
Enter the following Log Source Parameters: Name: Cynet360 Description: Cynet360 server Enabled: Yes Group: select the desired group Target event collector: select the log collector that receives the logs Log source identifier: Cynet server IP address

title: Cynet 360 Alert Flow
content:
The following article describes the chain of events following an incident that occurs on an endpoint protected by the Cynet agent.
Customers who purchased Cynet 360 via MSP/MSSP: All interaction with CyOps is via your MSP/MSSP.
When Cynet recognizes a potentially malicious action, an alert is triggered in the Cynet 360 console. The alert type and severity are determined by the various detection rules, which are constantly created and updated to uncover new threats. By default, only critical and high severity alerts, that are in status Open, are displayed in the Alert page. You can easily change the display to include other severities or statuses.
Critical alert: A security incident that indicated malicious activity and requires immediate mitigation. CyOps contacts the customer within one hour.
High severity alert: Any cyber security event that requires the attention of the customer’s security personnel.
You can configure Cynet 360 to send alerts by email to specified recipients. This can help you to keep track of alerts and events that require a response, without going into the Cynet 360 console. You can filter which alerts are sent by the severity level of the alert. To update the list of email recipients, contact your technical account manager. Different mechanisms determine if an alert is identical or similar to another, recently triggered, alert. Identical alerts are aggregated to appear only once in the Cynet 360 console and corresponding email.
The CyOps team monitors critical and high severity alerts only. The CyOps team contacts a customer ASAP via telephone only if there is reason to believe that a triggered alert or discovered artifacts in your environment require an Incident Response. Any follow-up communication will be performed via the ticketing system.
Cases in which the CyOps team contacts customers via email are:
Advise and assist with alert allowlisting
Penetration tests performed by the customer
Reply with file verdict to Send to SOC Analysis action, performed from the console
Address cases opened via the ticketing system
When alert management is complete, it is the responsibility of the customer to close the alert.

title: Long-Term Storage for CLM
content:
Cynet offers Centralized Log Management customers infinite, cold storage of data for compliance purposes.
During the basic 14-day retention period or extended 90-day retention period, CLM data is available to customers in the Cynet 360 console. However, during the cold storage period, the data is no longer available in the console and customers cannot access it. During the cold storage period the data is stored in an AWS S3 bucket, and Cynet Support can retrieve data for a customer who require a small data set from a specific period.

title: How to Integrate Cynet 360 with Datto Autotask (PSA)
content:
Integrating Cynet with Datto Autotask enables you to sync alert management in Cynet 360 with ticket management in Datto Autotask. A new ticket is created when an alert is triggered and closed when you change the alert status to Closed or Ignore. Closing an Autotask ticket closes the corresponding alert in Cynet 360. Updating an alert (with the same alert ID) updates the existing Autotask ticket, unless it was already closed, in which case the closed ticket will change to new. If the ticket was deleted in Autotask, updating the alert in Cynet 360 will open a new ticket in Autotask.
Currently, you can configure the Datto Autotask integration per site only.
Alerts are sent to Datto Autotask from the time you completed setting up the integration and onwards. Cynet does not send previously triggered alerts.
The Cynet Datto integration requires configuration in both systems:
Configuring the API user in Datto Autotask.
Configuring the Integration in Cynet 360.
Configuring the Integration in Datto Autotask: Creating a new extension callout. Creating a new workflow automation.
Creating a new extension callout.
Creating a new workflow automation.

Configuring the API User in Datto Autotask

In the Datto Autotask console, navigate to Admin > Extensions & Integrations > Other Extension & Tools > Integration Center .
Click + Add API User .
Enter the desired details, ensuring the Security Level is set to API User (system) . Save the Username (Key) and Password (Secret) in a safe place, for use when configuring the integration in Cynet 360.
Click Save & Close .
Continue to configuring the integration in Cynet 360.

Configuring the Integration in Cynet 360

Navigate to Settings > Integrations and click the Datto icon.
Enter the details of your Datto Autotask account: Autotask Zone Number: Your Autotask’s WSDL/Zone identifier. This corresponds to the wwX number you see in the URL. For example, if you see https://ww2.autotask.net you should enter webservices2 | if you see https://ww3.autotask.net/ you should enter webservices3 , etc. Account ID: The account ID number which corresponds to the account associated with the API user in Datto. Username (Key) and Password (Secret) : Enter the Username (Key) and Password (Secret) you saved when you configured the API user in Datto. API Tracking Identifier: Select Cynet Security .
Autotask Zone Number: Your Autotask’s WSDL/Zone identifier. This corresponds to the wwX number you see in the URL. For example, if you see https://ww2.autotask.net you should enter webservices2 | if you see https://ww3.autotask.net/ you should enter webservices3 , etc.
Account ID: The account ID number which corresponds to the account associated with the API user in Datto.
Username (Key) and Password (Secret) : Enter the Username (Key) and Password (Secret) you saved when you configured the API user in Datto.
API Tracking Identifier: Select Cynet Security .
Enter the statuses to assign to Autotask tickets following changes in alert status: Status for a newly created ticket Status for a ticket that is closed following a change in alert status to Closed/Ignore. You will need to enter this value again later in Datto Autotask.
Status for a newly created ticket
Status for a ticket that is closed following a change in alert status to Closed/Ignore. You will need to enter this value again later in Datto Autotask.
Click Save . The Cynet Listener URL is displayed. Copy and save this URL in a safe location, you will need it in the next step.

Configuring the Integration in Datto Autotask

Extension callouts and workflow rules enable the data transfer between Datto Autotask and Cynet 360. This ensures that closing an alert/ticket in one system will be reflected in the other system.
To create a new extension callout:
In the Autotask console, navigate to Admin > Extensions & Integrations > Other Extensions & Tools > Extension Callout (Tickets) and click New Extension Callout . The Extension Callout window opens.
Enter a name for the new extension callout.
Enter the Cynet Listener URL that you saved after configuring the integration in Cynet 360.
Click Save & Close . A new extension callout is created.
To create a new workflow rule:
In the Autotask console, navigate to Admin > Automation > Workflow Rules > Workflow Rules and click New . The New Workflow Rule window opens.
Enter a name for the new workflow rule.
Select the following values: Under EVENTS, select Edited by > Anyone . Under CONDITIONS: Select Ticket Title > Contains and enter the value: Cynet Alert . Select Status > Equals to and enter same status you configured in Cynet for a resolved ticket (step 3 when you configured the integration in Cynet 360). Select Account Name > Equals and, from the list, select the relevant Autotask account. Under ACTIONS > Then Execute Extension Callout , select the extension callout you created in the previous step.
Under EVENTS, select Edited by > Anyone .
Under CONDITIONS: Select Ticket Title > Contains and enter the value: Cynet Alert . Select Status > Equals to and enter same status you configured in Cynet for a resolved ticket (step 3 when you configured the integration in Cynet 360). Select Account Name > Equals and, from the list, select the relevant Autotask account.
Select Ticket Title > Contains and enter the value: Cynet Alert .
Select Status > Equals to and enter same status you configured in Cynet for a resolved ticket (step 3 when you configured the integration in Cynet 360).
Select Account Name > Equals and, from the list, select the relevant Autotask account.
Under ACTIONS > Then Execute Extension Callout , select the extension callout you created in the previous step.
Click Save & Close . A new workflow rule is created.

Verifying the Integration

Once the configuration is complete in both Cynet and Datto, you can verify the integration is working by clicking on the 3 dots next to the integration and clicking Send Test Ticket .

title: Viewing CLM Data
content:
The data collected from different data sources is displayed in an embedded Kibana. With Kibana, you can easily deep dive into the logged events, using a free search and various filters, or create your own dashboard. To read more about how to use Kibana, click here .
To view information collected by the Cynet CLM:
Navigate to Forensic > Analytics .
Click on the Menu icon.
Click on Dashboards .
Select a dashboard from the list to view.

Data Mapping

Cynet CLM maps incoming data to fields defined by Elastic Common Schema. All fields are mapped according to this standard, therefore, they do not appear in their original format. You can not add custom fields. All fields are added by Cynet.
* Optional fields
* Optional fields

content:
The Forensic page Users tab displays a list of all user accounts that have been logged into Cynet-protected hosts, as well as their metadata and enables you to obtain user-specific information. To customize or search the User list, see Searching the Forensic Page .

At the top of the page is a timeline that includes the user's recorded events. Below, you can see how the file is related to other entities in the organization. All the displayed objects are clickable.
The lower section of the User page displays multiple tabs providing detailed information about this user.
A tab does not appear in the User page if there is no relevant data to be displayed for that category.

title: Palo Alto - Block IP
content:
Use the Palo Alto Block IP remediation action to block an external IP address in your organization. This procedure was verified on Palo Alto firewall versions 9 and above.
In the current Beta version, the SOAR remediation actions are provided as scripts, per customer request. For more information, contact Cynet.
You must store your Palo Alto credentials in the Security Vault before creating a new Custom Remediation action. In the newly created vault object:
For name and key enter: Palo Alto Account
Activate the vault object and enable agent remediation
Add the following variables: Name: Credentials Key: Credentials Value: Your Palo Alto credentials in the format: "<IP>, <USER>, <PASSWORD>" (include the quotation marks)
Name: Credentials
Key: Credentials
Value: Your Palo Alto credentials in the format: "<IP>, <USER>, <PASSWORD>" (include the quotation marks)
Now you must create a new custom remediation action to run on an execution server connected to Palo Alto using the following parameters:
You must enter all the arguments, in the order in which they appear

title: How to Create a Playbook - Example #1
content:
This article describes the full process of creating a playbook to automate the investigation and response to the following event: A Cynet 360 alert that detected a malicious process on an endpoint. As an analyst, I want to know if this process opened any connections to remote IPs/domains. If so, block those connections on the endpoint.
Preliminary event: Cynet 360 triggers an alert regarding a malicious process (e.g., Malicious Process Command, Process Monitoring, etc.).
Since the event that initiates this investigation is an alert, select Alert from the dropdown list. Selecting Alert exposes a list of alert attributes that can be used as dynamic parameters in later steps of the playbook. Dynamic parameters are parameters that their value is calculated at runtime, and are taken from the alert that triggered the playbook. For a list of alert parameters, click here .
To do this, drag a File Action Query module onto the canvas. This module allows you to query Cynet's forensic data. In this case, you want discover all the connection that were made by the alert's process. The various file actions are listed here .
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Find Connections
Result Name: Connected IPs
File Action: Connection Established
Parameters: Process Occurrence = alert > Process.OccurenceID (this will retrieve only the connections in which the initiating process has the same ID as the alert process)
Information that can be used as dynamic parameters from this step will appear under the heading Find Connections .
Searching for the connections made by a process can return many results - an array. If you want the next step of the playbook to be repeated on all the results, you must use an Iterator. In this case, the next step, which is to block IPs, needs to be applied on all the connections made by the process.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Iterate over process connections
Iterate On: Find Connections > Connected IPs
Under Modules > Actions are all the actions available in Cynet 360, divided into categories: File, host, user or network. Remediation actions are detailed here (link). For this playbook, select the action Block Traffic, available under Network.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Block IPs on endpoint
Host Name: Alert > HostName (the host name as it appears in the alert, on which to block the IPs)
Domain/IP: Iterate over process connections > Connected IPs_RemoteIP (which IPs to block)
The IPs that were discovered during the investigation are part of the attack but do not appear in the alert information. To view this information and perform actions on it, use the Add Finding module.
This step of the playbook can be performed in parallel to the other investigation steps. click the + under the Iterator and drag the module onto the canvas.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Suspicious IPs
Type: Network Destination
Resource: Iterate over process connections > Connected IPs_RemoteIP
Reason: A connection performed by the suspicious process that triggered the alert
To activate the playbook:
Save the playbook by clicking . The playbook appears in the Playbooks tab.
To activate the playbook, use the Automatic Trigger toggle.
From the dropdown list, select which alert will trigger the playbook or select All. The playbook you created will run the next time this alert is triggered.
This playbook automates a useful and general investigation so you can apply it to all alerts.
The complete playbook as it appears in the canvas:
The following Incident View displays the results of a single run of the playbook:
For more information on the Incident View, click here .

sections.text:
This article describes the full process of creating a playbook to automate the investigation and response to the following event: A Cynet 360 alert that detected a malicious process on an endpoint. As an analyst, I want to know if this process opened any connections to remote IPs/domains. If so, block those connections on the endpoint.
Preliminary event: Cynet 360 triggers an alert regarding a malicious process (e.g., Malicious Process Command, Process Monitoring, etc.).
Since the event that initiates this investigation is an alert, select Alert from the dropdown list. Selecting Alert exposes a list of alert attributes that can be used as dynamic parameters in later steps of the playbook. Dynamic parameters are parameters that their value is calculated at runtime, and are taken from the alert that triggered the playbook. For a list of alert parameters, click here .
To do this, drag a File Action Query module onto the canvas. This module allows you to query Cynet's forensic data. In this case, you want discover all the connection that were made by the alert's process. The various file actions are listed here .
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Find Connections
Result Name: Connected IPs
File Action: Connection Established
Parameters: Process Occurrence = alert > Process.OccurenceID (this will retrieve only the connections in which the initiating process has the same ID as the alert process)
Information that can be used as dynamic parameters from this step will appear under the heading Find Connections .
Searching for the connections made by a process can return many results - an array. If you want the next step of the playbook to be repeated on all the results, you must use an Iterator. In this case, the next step, which is to block IPs, needs to be applied on all the connections made by the process.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Iterate over process connections
Iterate On: Find Connections > Connected IPs
Under Modules > Actions are all the actions available in Cynet 360, divided into categories: File, host, user or network. Remediation actions are detailed here (link). For this playbook, select the action Block Traffic, available under Network.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Block IPs on endpoint
Host Name: Alert > HostName (the host name as it appears in the alert, on which to block the IPs)
Domain/IP: Iterate over process connections > Connected IPs_RemoteIP (which IPs to block)
The IPs that were discovered during the investigation are part of the attack but do not appear in the alert information. To view this information and perform actions on it, use the Add Finding module.
This step of the playbook can be performed in parallel to the other investigation steps. click the + under the Iterator and drag the module onto the canvas.
Populate the module fields as follows:
Display Name: Suspicious IPs
Type: Network Destination
Resource: Iterate over process connections > Connected IPs_RemoteIP
Reason: A connection performed by the suspicious process that triggered the alert
To activate the playbook:
Save the playbook by clicking . The playbook appears in the Playbooks tab.
To activate the playbook, use the Automatic Trigger toggle.
From the dropdown list, select which alert will trigger the playbook or select All. The playbook you created will run the next time this alert is triggered.
This playbook automates a useful and general investigation so you can apply it to all alerts.

title: Hosts Map View
content:
The Hosts Map view displays organizational network segments and endpoints in an inter-connected map. Decoy endpoints can also be displayed in a different color. To access the Host Map view go to Forensic and select Map from the Hosts dropdown list.
Hosts can appear in different colors, where different colors represent the risk status of the host: Red = High Risk, Blue = Low Risk.
To collapse or expand an object, click it. When a host is clicked its detailed information appears below the map. For information on the various tabs, see Investigating Hosts .

title: SonicWall - Block IP
content:
Use the SonicWall Block IP remediation action to block an external IP address in your organization. This procedure was verified on SonicWall version 7.
In the current Beta version, the SOAR remediation actions are provided as scripts, per customer request. For more information, contact Cynet.
You must store your SonicWall credentials in the Security Vault before creating a new Custom Remediation action. In the newly created vault object:
For name and key enter: SonicWall Account
Activate the vault object and enable agent remediation
Add the following variables: Name: Credentials Key: Credentials Value: Your SonicWall credential in the format: "<IP>, <USER>, <PASSWORD>" (include the quotation marks)
Name: Credentials
Key: Credentials
Value: Your SonicWall credential in the format: "<IP>, <USER>, <PASSWORD>" (include the quotation marks)
Now you must create a new custom remediation action to run on an execution server connected to SonicWall using the following parameters:
You must enter all the arguments, in the order in which they appear

title: Port Scanning
content:
Cynet Port Scanner provides visibility on risky exposure to the internet of your subdomains and ports in your organization that shouldn't be exposed to the internet, such as your RDP port. You can use Port Scanner to scan cloud-based or on-premise infrastructure, using an internet domain name or IP range, e.g. cynet.com or 191.1.1.1-192.1.1.1.
To view port scanning results, your Cynet 360 package must include CSPM & SSPM integrations.
To connect the Port Scanner:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations .
From the Port Scanner icon, click the three dots, then select Connect Integration .
Enter the domain you want to scan (without http://www , just the domain) and the IP range.
Cynet 360 immediately begins the initial scan, which can take a few minutes. Following the initial scan, Cynet 360 performs a scan every several hours or when you click the Refresh button in the CSPM & SSPM page.
To view the scan results, navigate to the CSPM & SSPM page .
Each port is displayed as a separate issue. Click the row to see the port configuration of the various sub-domains.
In the Open Issues tab are ports that have been found to be open to the internet but it is recommended to close them.
In the Done tab are ports that were scanned and are in the recommended configuration.

title: Supported Services for CSPM & SSPM
content:
You can manage the following Services with Cynet CSPM & SSPM :
Security best practice is to provide credentials of a user that has the lowest possible privileges, but can manage configuration.

SSPM

When prompted to approve scopes, select all the available checkboxes.
At least one Google Project is required.
For more information, click here .
Users connecting to the integration must be a Global Admin for 365. As part of the implementation, Cynet will create an app within 365 - with the Exchange Admin role.

Operational Notifications for SaaS & Cloud Services

Operational Notifications are email notifications to customers about Operational events requiring their attention which are not security incidents (for which alerts are already generated).
Customers receive an email notification when a SaaS & Cloud service disconnects, which may indicate a configuration or networking issue. Once the integration is reconnected, an additional email notification is sent.

title: Billing
content:
Billing Information

Use the Billing tab to edit billing information for the different sites, and download billing reports for selected periods.

Site Activity

The License Consumption tab displays metrics on tenants and endpoints and compares these to your existing Cynet 360 license. This data provides an overall view of your environment, enabling you to track usage for billing purposes. The data displays information about the previous calendar month. E.g., on April 1 st the data is refreshed to display data for March.
The License Consumption tab contains the following information:
Once a day, we count the number of endpoint that are concurrently active. An active endpoint is an endpoint that was seen in the last 30 minutes. At the end of the month, we survey the daily count of active endpoints. The maximum is the monthly license consumption. For MSSP, we sum up the count of active endpoints across all sites.
In the example above, the monthly usage is 261 endpoints.
To receive a detailed report of the aggregated data displayed in this tab, such as the list of active endpoints, contact your Technical Account Manager.

title: Viewing and Fixing CSPM & SSPM Risks
content:
The CSPM & SSPM page includes the following sections:
The CSPM & SSPM page includes a list of configuration issues, from which you can also perform actions.
For each issue in the list, the following information is displayed:
Note: Some issues pertaining to Office 365 and Entra ID require a premium Microsoft 365 license in order to view them. If you do not have a premium license, the Current Value for these issues will not match the Secure Value and you will not be able to fix them using the CSPM & SSPM page. For a full list of these issues, see this link .

Performing Actions on Issues

Fix: Cynet 360 changes the configuration to the recommended value. The issue status is updated.
Validate: Cynet 360 checks to see if a configuration fix was performed outside of Cynet. If the configuration was fixed, the issue status is updated. If the issue is not yet fixed, an error appears.
Ignore: Leaves the configuration as is and changes the issue status to Ignored .
You can also perform these actions in bulk by selecting several issues and selecting from the Action menu (8).
For information on setting up email notifications for risks, see SSPM Reports & Notifications .

content:
As well as application misconfigurations, the Cloud & SaaS Security Posture Management page also provides visibility on user risks in your connected SaaS application and cloud platforms.
The Users tab displays a list of the users of your connected SaaS application and cloud platforms, with information on the potential risks to your organization.
For each user in the list, the information below is displayed. The collapsed view displays aggregated information for all applications. Expand the row to see the information per application.
