title: CLM Dashboards
content:
Cynet's Centralized Log Management dashboards provide you with actionable insights into your security posture for a number of different key integrations.
The available dashboards include:
Office365 Authentication Activity Dashboard
Fortigate Firewall Dashboard
Meraki Firewall Dashboard
Firepower Firewall Dashboard
Palo Alto Networks Firewall Dashboard
FortiAnalyzer Dashboard
Continue reading for a breakdown of each of these dashboards.

Office365 Authentication Activity Dashboard

The Office365 Authentication Activity dashboard shows the following visualizations:
User Logins Over Time : This visualization illustrates the trend of user logins over a specified period, providing insights into patterns of user activity and potential anomalies.
Added Users : This visualization displays the number of new users added to the system over time, helping to track user growth and adoption of the Office365 application.
Application Usage Over Time : This visualization presents a timeline of application usage within Office365, highlighting periods of high or low activity and identifying trends in application usage patterns.
Successful vs. Failed Logins Over Time : This visualization compares the number of successful and failed login attempts over a specified period, enabling the monitoring of authentication activity and detection of potential security issues.
Top 5 Successful Logins by Source IP : This visualization highlights the top five source IP addresses associated with successful login attempts, providing insights into the locations or systems from which successful logins originate.
Successful Logins : This visualization presents the overall trend of successful login attempts over a specified period, offering an overview of authentication activity and identifying peak login periods.
Failed Logins per User : This visualization shows the number of failed login attempts for each user, allowing for the identification of users experiencing authentication challenges and potential security threats.
Authentication Failure Table : This table provides detailed information about authentication failures, including the user, source IP address, timestamp, and reason for the failed login attempt, facilitating troubleshooting and investigation of authentication issues.
Recent Authentication Activity : This visualization displays a snapshot of recent authentication activity, including both successful and failed login attempts, allowing for real-time monitoring and detection of suspicious activity.
Failed Login Attempts by Source IP Address : This visualization presents a breakdown of failed login attempts by the source IP address, helping to identify potential sources of unauthorized access attempts and security threats.
Failed Logins Reason : This visualization categorizes and displays the reasons for failed login attempts, providing insights into common authentication challenges or issues faced by users.
Top Users with Failed Login Attempts : This visualization identifies and ranks users with the highest number of failed login attempts, helping to pinpoint accounts experiencing repeated authentication failures and potential security risks.
Failed Logins : This visualization presents an overview of all failed login attempts, including details such as the user, source IP address, timestamp, and reason for the failed login, enabling administrators to monitor authentication failures and take appropriate action.

Fortigate Firewall Dashboard

The Fortigate Firewall dashboard shows the following visualizations:
Selector : This visualization allows users to filter and select specific data based on their criteria, providing a tailored view of the data.
Traffic Overview : This visualization offers a comprehensive overview of network traffic, including inbound and outbound data, protocols, and traffic volume.
Top Firewall Actions : Displays the most common actions taken by the firewall, such as allow, block, or drop, providing insights into firewall activity.
Network Protocol Distribution : Illustrates the distribution of network traffic by protocol type, such as TCP, UDP, or ICMP, highlighting the most prevalent protocols.
Traffic by Protocol and Action : Shows network traffic categorized by protocol type and corresponding firewall action, aiding in the analysis of traffic patterns and firewall policies.
Top 10 HTTP Request Referrers : Presents the top referrers of HTTP requests, indicating which sources are generating the most web traffic.
Top Threat Categories : Identifies and ranks the most prevalent threat categories detected by the firewall, helping to prioritize security efforts.
Threat Severity Distribution : Visualizes the distribution of threats by severity level, allowing users to focus on addressing high-severity threats more urgently.
Application Risk Level Distribution : Displays the distribution of application risk levels detected within network traffic, facilitating risk assessment and mitigation strategies.
Top 5 Source IPs : Highlights the top five source IP addresses generating network traffic, providing insights into the origins of traffic flows.
Traffic by Source Port : Illustrates network traffic distribution based on the source port numbers used, aiding in the analysis of communication patterns.
Top 5 Destination Countries: Identifies the top five countries to which network traffic is directed, offering geographic insights into network activity.
Traffic by Destination Port : Displays network traffic distribution based on destination port numbers, helping to identify services and applications accessed by network users.
Top 5 Destination IPs : Highlights the top five destination IP addresses receiving network traffic, revealing the most frequently accessed destinations.

Meraki Firewall Dashboard

The Meraki Firewall dashboard shows the following visualizations:
Traffic Over Time - Source IPs: Overview: This visualization provides a historical view of network traffic over time, focusing on the source IP addresses. It allows you to track changes in traffic patterns originating from different IP addresses over a specified period. Use Case: Useful for identifying trends in network usage from various source IP addresses and detecting anomalies or spikes in traffic volume from specific sources.
Overview: This visualization provides a historical view of network traffic over time, focusing on the source IP addresses. It allows you to track changes in traffic patterns originating from different IP addresses over a specified period.
Use Case: Useful for identifying trends in network usage from various source IP addresses and detecting anomalies or spikes in traffic volume from specific sources.
Top Source IPs : Overview: This visualization displays the top source IP addresses based on the volume of traffic they generate. It helps identify the most active or frequently communicating IP addresses on the network. Use Case: Helpful for pinpointing the IP addresses responsible for generating the most traffic, which could indicate potential issues, such as network congestion, suspicious activity, or misconfigurations.
Overview: This visualization displays the top source IP addresses based on the volume of traffic they generate. It helps identify the most active or frequently communicating IP addresses on the network.
Use Case: Helpful for pinpointing the IP addresses responsible for generating the most traffic, which could indicate potential issues, such as network congestion, suspicious activity, or misconfigurations.
Top Source Ports : Overview: This visualization presents the top source ports used in network traffic, ranked by the volume of data transmitted through each port. It offers insights into the distribution of traffic across different source port numbers. Use Case: Valuable for understanding which source ports are most frequently utilized in network communications, aiding in network optimization, security policy enforcement, and anomaly detection.
Overview: This visualization presents the top source ports used in network traffic, ranked by the volume of data transmitted through each port. It offers insights into the distribution of traffic across different source port numbers.
Use Case: Valuable for understanding which source ports are most frequently utilized in network communications, aiding in network optimization, security policy enforcement, and anomaly detection.
Top Destination IPs : Overview: This visualization highlights the top destination IP addresses based on inbound network traffic. It identifies the most commonly accessed destinations on the network. Use Case: Useful for identifying popular destinations and monitoring traffic patterns to specific IP addresses. It helps detect unusual or unauthorized connections to destinations and assists in network troubleshooting.
Overview: This visualization highlights the top destination IP addresses based on inbound network traffic. It identifies the most commonly accessed destinations on the network.
Use Case: Useful for identifying popular destinations and monitoring traffic patterns to specific IP addresses. It helps detect unusual or unauthorized connections to destinations and assists in network troubleshooting.
Top Destination Ports : Overview: This visualization showcases the top destination ports that receive incoming network traffic, ranked by traffic volume. It offers insights into the distribution of traffic across different destination port numbers. Use Case: Beneficial for understanding which destination ports are most frequently accessed by network traffic, aiding in port usage analysis, security monitoring, and identifying potential vulnerabilities or threats.
Overview: This visualization showcases the top destination ports that receive incoming network traffic, ranked by traffic volume. It offers insights into the distribution of traffic across different destination port numbers.
Use Case: Beneficial for understanding which destination ports are most frequently accessed by network traffic, aiding in port usage analysis, security monitoring, and identifying potential vulnerabilities or threats.
Service Name Breakdown : Overview: This visualization categorizes network traffic based on service names or protocols, providing a breakdown of the types of services or protocols used in communication. Use Case: Helpful for understanding the composition of network traffic in terms of the services or protocols being utilized. It enables administrators to identify the types of applications or services consuming network resources and ensure compliance with network policies.
Overview: This visualization categorizes network traffic based on service names or protocols, providing a breakdown of the types of services or protocols used in communication.
Use Case: Helpful for understanding the composition of network traffic in terms of the services or protocols being utilized. It enables administrators to identify the types of applications or services consuming network resources and ensure compliance with network policies.
Protocol Distribution : Overview: This visualization illustrates the distribution of network traffic by protocol type, such as TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc. It shows the relative proportions of each protocol in the overall network traffic. Use Case: Useful for gaining insights into the composition of network traffic in terms of different protocol types. It helps in understanding network behavior, identifying protocol-specific issues, and ensuring proper protocol usage and configuration.
Overview: This visualization illustrates the distribution of network traffic by protocol type, such as TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc. It shows the relative proportions of each protocol in the overall network traffic.
Use Case: Useful for gaining insights into the composition of network traffic in terms of different protocol types. It helps in understanding network behavior, identifying protocol-specific issues, and ensuring proper protocol usage and configuration.

Firepower Firewall Dashboard

The Firepower Firewall dashboard shows the following visualizations:
Protocol Distribution : Overview: This visualization illustrates the distribution of network traffic based on different protocols, such as TCP, UDP, or ICMP. It provides insights into the types of protocols predominantly used in the network. Use Case: Useful for understanding the composition of network traffic in terms of protocols, identifying any protocol-specific trends or anomalies, and ensuring compliance with network protocol standards.
Overview: This visualization illustrates the distribution of network traffic based on different protocols, such as TCP, UDP, or ICMP. It provides insights into the types of protocols predominantly used in the network.
Use Case: Useful for understanding the composition of network traffic in terms of protocols, identifying any protocol-specific trends or anomalies, and ensuring compliance with network protocol standards.
Denied/Approved Connections : Overview: This visualization categorizes network connections into denied and approved categories, highlighting connections that were either blocked or allowed by the firewall policies. Use Case: Helps in monitoring firewall activity by distinguishing between allowed and denied connections, identifying potential security policy violations or misconfigurations, and assessing the effectiveness of firewall rules.
Overview: This visualization categorizes network connections into denied and approved categories, highlighting connections that were either blocked or allowed by the firewall policies.
Use Case: Helps in monitoring firewall activity by distinguishing between allowed and denied connections, identifying potential security policy violations or misconfigurations, and assessing the effectiveness of firewall rules.
Top 5 Denied Destination IPs : Overview: Identifies the top five destination IP addresses to which connections were denied by the firewall, based on the frequency of denied attempts. Use Case: Useful for pinpointing specific destination IP addresses that are frequently targeted or accessed by denied connections, aiding in identifying potential threats, suspicious activities, or targeted attacks.
Overview: Identifies the top five destination IP addresses to which connections were denied by the firewall, based on the frequency of denied attempts.
Use Case: Useful for pinpointing specific destination IP addresses that are frequently targeted or accessed by denied connections, aiding in identifying potential threats, suspicious activities, or targeted attacks.
Top 5 Denied Source IPs : Overview: Highlights the top five source IP addresses from which denied connection attempts originated, based on the frequency of denied attempts. Use Case: Helps identify the sources of denied connection attempts, allowing security teams to investigate potentially compromised or malicious hosts, and take appropriate action to mitigate security risks.
Overview: Highlights the top five source IP addresses from which denied connection attempts originated, based on the frequency of denied attempts.
Use Case: Helps identify the sources of denied connection attempts, allowing security teams to investigate potentially compromised or malicious hosts, and take appropriate action to mitigate security risks.
Top 5 Denied Source Ports : Overview: Displays the top five source ports from which denied connection attempts originated, ranked by the frequency of denied attempts. Use Case: Useful for identifying specific ports used in denied connection attempts, aiding in the detection of port scanning activities, reconnaissance attempts, or unauthorized access attempts.
Overview: Displays the top five source ports from which denied connection attempts originated, ranked by the frequency of denied attempts.
Use Case: Useful for identifying specific ports used in denied connection attempts, aiding in the detection of port scanning activities, reconnaissance attempts, or unauthorized access attempts.
Top 5 Denied Destination Ports : Overview: Identifies the top five destination ports that were targets of denied connection attempts, based on the frequency of denied attempts. Use Case: Helps identify the ports targeted by denied connection attempts, facilitating the detection of port-based attacks, service probing, or attempts to exploit specific vulnerabilities.
Overview: Identifies the top five destination ports that were targets of denied connection attempts, based on the frequency of denied attempts.
Use Case: Helps identify the ports targeted by denied connection attempts, facilitating the detection of port-based attacks, service probing, or attempts to exploit specific vulnerabilities.
Heat Map - Source IP vs. Destination IP : Overview: Visualizes the relationship between source IP addresses and destination IP addresses in the form of a heat map, representing the volume of traffic between various source-destination pairs. Use Case: Provides a graphical representation of traffic patterns between different hosts, aiding in identifying communication trends, abnormal traffic flows, or potential unauthorized connections.
Overview: Visualizes the relationship between source IP addresses and destination IP addresses in the form of a heat map, representing the volume of traffic between various source-destination pairs.
Use Case: Provides a graphical representation of traffic patterns between different hosts, aiding in identifying communication trends, abnormal traffic flows, or potential unauthorized connections.
Top 10 Log Messages : Overview: Presents the top ten log messages generated by the firewall, highlighting the most significant events or alerts recorded in the logs. Use Case: Offers quick access to the most critical log messages for monitoring firewall activity, identifying security incidents, troubleshooting connectivity issues, or investigating network events.
Overview: Presents the top ten log messages generated by the firewall, highlighting the most significant events or alerts recorded in the logs.
Use Case: Offers quick access to the most critical log messages for monitoring firewall activity, identifying security incidents, troubleshooting connectivity issues, or investigating network events.

Palo Alto Firewall Dashboard

The Palo Alto Firewall dashboard shows the following visualizations:
Top Source IPs by Traffic: Overview: Identifies the top source IPs generating the most traffic in your network. Use Case: Helps identify high-traffic sources that might need further investigation for security or performance optimization.
Overview: Identifies the top source IPs generating the most traffic in your network.
Use Case: Helps identify high-traffic sources that might need further investigation for security or performance optimization.
Destination IPs with Most Traffic: Overview: Shows the destination IPs receiving the most traffic. Use Case: Useful for monitoring frequently accessed destinations, which could indicate critical services or potential attack targets.
Overview: Shows the destination IPs receiving the most traffic.
Use Case: Useful for monitoring frequently accessed destinations, which could indicate critical services or potential attack targets.
Event Actions Overview: Overview: Displays the distribution of different event actions (allow, deny, drop, etc.). Use Case: Provides insight into the network’s behavior, showing how traffic is being handled by the firewall.
Overview: Displays the distribution of different event actions (allow, deny, drop, etc.).
Use Case: Provides insight into the network’s behavior, showing how traffic is being handled by the firewall.
Top Source Users by Traffic: Overview: Identifies users generating the most traffic. Use Case: Helps in monitoring user activity, detecting possible misuse, and managing bandwidth consumption.
Top Source Users by Traffic:
Overview: Identifies users generating the most traffic.
Use Case: Helps in monitoring user activity, detecting possible misuse, and managing bandwidth consumption.
Event Reasons Breakdown: Overview: Breaks down the different reasons for events (e.g., aged-out, tcp-fin, tcp-rst). Use Case: Useful for diagnosing network issues by understanding why certain traffic is being terminated or dropped.
Overview: Breaks down the different reasons for events (e.g., aged-out, tcp-fin, tcp-rst).
Use Case: Useful for diagnosing network issues by understanding why certain traffic is being terminated or dropped.
Top Destination Ports: Overview: Displays the most frequently accessed destination ports. Use Case: Identifies which services are most used, guiding security measures for critical ports.
Overview: Displays the most frequently accessed destination ports.
Use Case: Identifies which services are most used, guiding security measures for critical ports.
Network Transport Protocols Distribution Overview: Analyzes the distribution of different network transport protocols (e.g., ICMP, TCP, UDP). Use Case: Provides an overview of the types of traffic on the network, which is essential for understanding and managing network behavior.
Overview: Analyzes the distribution of different network transport protocols (e.g., ICMP, TCP, UDP).
Use Case: Provides an overview of the types of traffic on the network, which is essential for understanding and managing network behavior.
Network Protocol Usage: Overview: Visualizes the usage of different network protocols (e.g., SSL, DNS-base, incomplete, DNSCrypt). Use Case: Helps understand protocol usage, ensuring the security of encrypted traffic, and identifying unusual traffic patterns.
Overview: Visualizes the usage of different network protocols (e.g., SSL, DNS-base, incomplete, DNSCrypt).
Use Case: Helps understand protocol usage, ensuring the security of encrypted traffic, and identifying unusual traffic patterns.
Source vs. Destination IP Traffic Matrix: Overview: Creates a matrix to visualize traffic interactions between source and destination IPs. Use Case: Useful for detecting communication patterns between specific IPs, which can help in identifying potential threats or understanding data flows.
Overview: Creates a matrix to visualize traffic interactions between source and destination IPs.
Use Case: Useful for detecting communication patterns between specific IPs, which can help in identifying potential threats or understanding data flows.
Time-Series Analysis of Event Actions: Overview: Visualizes trends in event actions (allow, deny, drop, etc.) over time. Use Case: Identifies trends or anomalies in how traffic is processed, important for detecting potential attacks or significant changes in network behavior.
Overview: Visualizes trends in event actions (allow, deny, drop, etc.) over time.
Use Case: Identifies trends or anomalies in how traffic is processed, important for detecting potential attacks or significant changes in network behavior.

FortiAnalyzer Dashboard

The FortiAnalyzer dashboard shows the following visualizations:
Selector : This visualization allows users to filter and select specific data based on their criteria, providing a tailored view of the data.
Traffic Overview : This visualization offers a comprehensive overview of network traffic, including inbound and outbound data, protocols, and traffic volume.
Top Firewall Rules: Highlights the most frequently triggered firewall rules, helping to optimize security policies, detect misconfigurations, and improve network security.
Top Firewall Actions : Displays the most common actions taken by the firewall, such as allow, block, or drop, providing insights into firewall activity.
Traffic by Protocol and Action : Shows network traffic categorized by protocol type and corresponding firewall action, aiding in the analysis of traffic patterns and firewall policies.
Top 5 Source IPs : Highlights the top five source IP addresses generating network traffic, providing insights into the origins of traffic flows.
Traffic by Source Port : Illustrates network traffic distribution based on the source port numbers used, aiding in the analysis of communication patterns.
Top 5 Destination Countries: Identifies the top five countries to which network traffic is directed, offering geographic insights into network activity.
Traffic by Destination Port : Displays network traffic distribution based on destination port numbers, helping to identify services and applications accessed by network users.
Top 5 Destination IPs : Highlights the top five destination IP addresses receiving network traffic, revealing the most frequently accessed destinations.

Top Destination Ports : Overview: This visualization showcases the top destination ports that receive incoming network traffic, ranked by traffic volume. It offers insights into the distribution of traffic across different destination port numbers. Use Case: Beneficial for understanding which destination ports are most frequently accessed by network traffic, aiding in port usage analysis, security monitoring, and identifying potential vulnerabilities or threats.

Service Name Breakdown : Overview: This visualization categorizes network traffic based on service names or protocols, providing a breakdown of the types of services or protocols used in communication. Use Case: Helpful for understanding the composition of network traffic in terms of the services or protocols being utilized. It enables administrators to identify the types of applications or services consuming network resources and ensure compliance with network policies.

Use Case: Useful for gaining insights into the composition of network traffic in terms of different protocol types. It helps in understanding network behavior, identifying protocol-specific issues, and ensuring proper protocol usage and configuration.

title: Malicious Domain Protection
content:
Cynet allows you to monitor the domains accessed by users and block access to malicious web sites. Malicious Domain Protection includes two operation modes:
Detect: When a user tries to access a malicious web site, Cynet triggers an alert.
Detect & Prevent: When a user tries to access a malicious web site, Cynet blocks access to the web site and triggers an alert.
The triggered alert includes information about the category to which the domain belongs and why it is considered malicious. Cynet manages domain categorization. Categories include phishing, malware, spam, etc., and are updated from time to time.
Notes:
Malicious Domain Protection is available only for customers who have the All-in-One solution or Elite customers who purchased the Malicious Domain Protection add-on.
Malicious Domain Protection is available only for Windows OS at this time, and only on machines which have the required hardware specifications .
To enable Malicious Domain Protection:
Navigate to Settings > Groups.
Edit the group for which you want to turn on Malicious Domain Protection.
Under Network Detection and Response (NDR) click Advanced Settings .
Toggle Malicious Domain Protection to On .
Click Ok .
Click Save .
Note: Turning off Malicious Domain Protection will set the NDR to Detect , since only Malicious Domain Protection provides active protection for NDR.
For a list of network-related alerts, click here .

title: Endpoint Rollback
content:
Endpoint rollback enables you to restore encrypted and deleted files from endpoints if they get infected by ransomware, malware or other malicious tools. Having the option to restore critical data could be lifesaving in case of a ransomware incident .
Endpoint rollback can be enabled or disabled for each group individually in the Agent Settings. (For more information on enabling endpoint rollback, see Agent Settings ).
To execute rollback for a particular endpoint:
Navigate to Forensic > Hosts .
Select a host.
Navigate to the Backups tab in the host's navigation bar. The available backups are displayed for you to see.
Select one of the available backups.
Click on the Actions button on the right-hand side of the top toolbar.
Under Remediation , select Rollback to selected backup .
Click OK . After clicking OK , the endpoint will be rolled back to the selected backup.

Backup

Backups occur once every 24 hours, utilizing the System Restore feature of Windows OS. Backups apply to the Desktop , Downloads , Documents , and Pictures folders under the user(s) directory and are limited to 10% of the total disk space.
Note: Only certain file types will be included in backup, see the full list here .

Supported Operating Systems

Rollback is supported only on Windows, on the following operating systems:
Workstation versions: 10/11
Servers: 2016, 2019, 2022
Important! Endpoint rollback is supported only for endpoints with the recommended hardware specifications .
Note: Endpoint Rollback is currently available only for SaaS customers.

title: Automatically Remediating Alerts
content:
Auto-Remediation enables you to create rules for Cynet 360 to automatically perform remediation actions when an alert is generated. These rules can be configured to match alerts based on a number of factors.
There are two ways to create Auto-Remediation rules:
From a specific alert, in the Alerts page. Cynet 360 automatically populates the rule parameters according to the selected alert.
Cynet 360 automatically populates the rule parameters according to the selected alert.
From the Actions page, Auto Remediation tab.
Creating an Auto-Remediation rule includes several steps:
Configuring General Parameters
Matching the rule to alerts and scan groups
Advanced matching options
Matching the rule to specific hosts
Selecting a remediation action.
Selecting whether to close the alert after auto-remediation.
Adding tags to the auto-remediated alerts.
Once you create an Auto-Remediation rule, you can configure additional advanced settings. To edit or delete an auto-remediation rule, navigate to Actions > Auto Remediation .

Configuring General Parameters

Enter the following general parameters:

Matching the Rule to Alerts and Groups

You can create an auto-remediation rule to be applied on specific host groups. The fields required for matching an auto-remediation rule to host groups are:

Advanced Matching Options

Advanced options for matching an auto-remediation rule to an alert are based on files, users, or networks. To match the rule to any file/user/network in an alert, leave these fields blank.

Matching the Rule to Specific Hosts

You can create Host Groups and configure the auto-remediation rule to apply only to alerts triggered on those hosts.
To create a Host Group:
Expand the Hosts to Match section and click Add New Match .
Enter a Group name.
Enter a regular expression (RegExp) to match the names of the hosts you want to include in the group.
To apply the rule to specific operating systems in the host Group, select an operating system from the dropdown list. The list is populated according to the scanned hosts.
Click Save Group .
To edit a Host Group, select it from the list, then click Edit Match .

Selecting a Remediation Action

The available remediation actions depend on the type of alert you want to remediate: File, user, host, or network. The remediation actions include built-in remediation actions, custom remediation actions, and playbooks.
Select an alert type, then a remediation action. Optionally select the options to close the alert or add tags to it after auto-remediation. To save the auto-remediation rule, click Save .

Advanced Auto-Remediation Settings

Advanced auto-remediation settings include:
Remediation Retry Attempts: Set the maximum number of times the system attempts a remediation action. The default is 48 retries.
Remediation Retry Interval: Set the time interval between remediation retries in minutes. The default is 30 minutes.
To change these settings navigate to Settings > Advanced > Remediation Settings.

title: Email Security - Overview
content:
Cynet Email Security is a holistic security solution that provides mail protection for Cloud Email Gateways . It combines a variety of capabilities including attachment and URL scanning to ensure your inbox stays safe, real-time link protection which allows scanning the original target in real-time each visit, attachment extension filtering to block risky attachments and avoid malware disguised as harmless files, and policy controls letting you block what's bad and allow what's trusted using customizable allowlists and blocklists.
Note: Email Security currently only scans emails coming into your organization.
Cynet Email Security provides the following abilities:
Configure your Office365 or Gmail gateway to be used with Cynet Email.
Email attachment protection.
Email link protection (including real-time checking of link targets when you open them).
Expand shortened URLs (such as those from bit.ly) revealing the real destination behind potentially deceptive links.
Tag emails that were sent from a domain that doesn't match the administrator's email domain as External emails. You can also add domains that you consider "safe" and don't need to be tagged as external.
Create allowlists and blocklists for emails using the following parameters: Domain (URL) Sender email Recipient email File SHA256
Domain (URL)
Sender email
Recipient email
File SHA256
For additional information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security - Settings
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security - Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ

Detecting and Remediating Attacks on Your Mailbox

In addition to our Email Security capabilities, we also provide threat detection and remediation against attacks that could compromise your mailbox, such as suspicious email forwarding rules, that could forward your email to an external domain.
For more information about threat detection and remediation, see Remediating Alerts and XDR Alerts .

title: Email Security - Settings
content:
The Email Security settings page is used to enable or disable the different email security features, as well as setting allowed external domains and blocked file extensions.
To access the Email Security Settings page, navigate to Email Security > Settings .
Each of the following options can be toggled on or off, depending on your needs.

Attachment Protection

Toggling this option to On provides scanning for all email attachments that are not password protected.
Toggling the Block Password Protected Attachments option to On blocks password protected ZIP files attached to emails.

Link Protection

Toggling this option to On provides link scanning for all links in your emails.
Toggling the Real-Time Link Scanning option to On provides real-time protection when opening links from emails, ensuring links are valid and not malicious.

QR Code Protection

Toggling this option to On provides QR scanning for all QR codes in your emails.
Toggling the Real-Time QR Scanning option to On provides real-time protection when using QR codes from emails, ensuring links are valid and not malicious.

External Emails Warning

Toggling this option to On tags all emails which arrive from a domain that doesn't match your administrator's Office365 domain with the [External] tag in the email subject.

Allowed External Domains

If there are domains which you consider safe, that you do not want to be tagged as External, you can enter these domains in the Allowed External Domains list.

Email Digest

Schedule either daily or weekly emails containing a digest of all emails that were quarantined. The email digest includes the option to release spam emails yourself, or request that an admin release emails flagged as malicious. If sending the digest on a weekly basis, you can select which day of the week the digest is sent. Additionally, you can edit the email message to be included in the email digest.

Malicious Emails Policy

This section controls what to do when an email is detected as malicious. You can select between tagging potentially dangerous emails with a warning banner, moving them directly to your junk folder, or quarantining them to prevent them from arriving in your inbox.

Blocked File Extensions

If there are file extensions that you always want to block, you can add them to the list of blocked file extensions in this section. Cynet auto-populates this section with a list of recommended file extensions to block. You can remove extensions from the list by clicking on the X next to the file extension you want to remove.

Spam Filtering Policy

This section enables you to differentiate spam emails from malicious emails. You can choose between tagging potential spam emails with a warning banner , moving them directly to your junk folder , and quarantining to prevent them from arriving in your inbox.

Set to Best Practice

Clicking on the Set to best practice button changes the current settings to Cynet's recommended best practice settings.

Reset

The Reset button resets your settings to the most recently saved settings. If you have made changes and not yet saved them, your changes will be reverted.

Save

The Save button saves your current settings.
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security - Overview
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security - Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ

sections.text: This section enables you to differentiate spam emails from malicious emails. You can choose between tagging potential spam emails with a warning banner , moving them directly to your junk folder , and quarantining to prevent them from arriving in your inbox.

sections.heading: Set to Best Practice
sections.text: Clicking on the Set to best practice button changes the current settings to Cynet's recommended best practice settings.

sections.heading: Reset
sections.text: The Reset button resets your settings to the most recently saved settings. If you have made changes and not yet saved them, your changes will be reverted.

sections.heading: Save
sections.text:
The Save button saves your current settings.
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security - Overview
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security - Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ

title: Endpoint Vulnerabilities Management
content:
Cynet offers Endpoint Security Posture Management (ESPM) for Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE).
The Endpoint Vulnerabilities management service allows you to view vulnerabilities of unpatched binaries across all endpoints. You can see vulnerable software products such as Adobe, Office, Chrome and others (all installed software is checked for the presence of vulnerabilities), view impacted endpoints and priority, as well as drilling down to the specific endpoint level. All vulnerabilities are based on those found in the National Vulnerability Database by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) .
The EPS sends a list of all installed apps on all endpoints every 4 hours, and Cynet gets updates from NIST about new and modified CVEs every few minutes.
Note: Endpoint Vulnerabilities Management is only available for Windows operating systems running the recommended hardware specifications .

Viewing Endpoint and Vulnerabilities Management

To access Endpoint Vulnerabilities Management, navigate to Endpoints > Vulnerabilities .
Each row represents a CVE risk in a specific product in the organization. Each risk includes the severity, risk, product affected, initial detection date, and the Endpoints column, which shows the total number of affected endpoints running the software (colored according to the risk severity), along with the total number of endpoints running the software that are running a patched version (shown in green). Not shown are endpoints in the organization which are not running the affected product.
Each row can be expanded for additional information about the risk.
The additional information includes:
The CVE identifier.
The CVE Score (along with its severity).
The affected product.
Affected versions of the product (this also tells you which version you need to update to in order to patch the vulnerability).
A reference with the NIST link for the CVE which gives more information about the vulnerability.
A description of the risk.
You can export the entire list of vulnerabilities to a CSV file, by clicking the Export button at the top right-hand side of the screen.
The search bar allows you to narrow the list by searching for risk ID, product name, or severity.
Additionally, clicking on the menu at the right-hand side of the row, then clicking the Endpoints icon brings you to a list of all the affected endpoints.
It is also possible to ignore endpoint vulnerabilities by clicking on the menu at the right-hand side of the row, then clicking the Ignore button. Ignoring a vulnerability can be done at the organization level or at the endpoint level. If you ignore a vulnerability at the organization level, it is still possible to unignore it for specific endpoints. This allows you to avoid being notified about vulnerabilities that are not of interest to you and simplifies your alert management.
The EPS sends a list of all installed apps on all endpoints every 4 hours, and Cynet gets updates from NIST about new and modified CVEs every few minutes.

title: Endpoint Management
content:
Cynet's Endpoint Management page allows you to view all endpoints that are connected to your organization, as well as a list of those which are not yet connected. From the Endpoints tab, you can see various details about the endpoints in your organization and perform actions on your endpoints.
These details include the endpoint name, IP address, operating system, what group the endpoint belongs to (if applicable), when the endpoint was last seen, and the system status.
If you have Cynet integrated with Entra ID Domain or your local Active Directory (On-Prem) Domain, you can also see various details about endpoints in your organization that are not yet connected to Cynet, from the Non-Deployed Endpoints tab (see also Non-Deployed Endpoint Management ).
Note: After installing the Cynet agent on an endpoint, it can take up to 15 minutes for it to appear in the Endpoint Management page. This is by design as this is the length of time that it takes for the system to digest the data.

Endpoints Status Tiles

You can see the overall status of your organization's endpoints in the tiles at the top of the Endpoints Management page. These show the number of active, faulty, inactive, isolated, and not protected endpoints in your organization. You can also see the status of an individual endpoint from the Status column in the Endpoints list.

Endpoints List

The endpoints list is a list of all the endpoints in your organization.
You can search and filter the endpoints shown in multiple ways:
Using the search function, you can search for a specific endpoint or a specific IP address.
Using the date function, you can filter for endpoints that were last seen in the last 7 days, 14 days, 30 days, 90 days, or All Time.
Using the search filters, you can select from the following filters: OS - The operating system installed on the endpoint. Group - The name of the Group to which the endpoint belongs. Agent - Whether or not the Cynet agent is installed on the endpoint. Outdated Agents - Whether the Cynet agent is up-to-date or outdated. EPP Best Practice - Whether or not the endpoint is using the EPP best practice settings. System Status - The current system status of the endpoint (OK, Isolated, Pending Isolate, Warning, Uninstalled).
OS - The operating system installed on the endpoint.
Group - The name of the Group to which the endpoint belongs.
Agent - Whether or not the Cynet agent is installed on the endpoint.
Outdated Agents - Whether the Cynet agent is up-to-date or outdated.
EPP Best Practice - Whether or not the endpoint is using the EPP best practice settings.
System Status - The current system status of the endpoint (OK, Isolated, Pending Isolate, Warning, Uninstalled).
The Endpoint Health and Connectivity states are:
Active: The endpoint communicated with Cynet for at least 30 minutes in the past 30 days.
Pending Isolate : The Isolate remediation action has been performed on this endpoint but has not yet completed.
Isolated : The Isolate remediation action has been performed on this endpoint and the endpoint is currently isolated from the system.
Uninstalled: The Cynet agent was uninstalled from the endpoint.
OK: No errors or warning were sent in the last communication - meaning the device status is OK.
Warning: A warning was sent in the last communication - meaning the device status has one or more warnings (not OK).
With both the OK and Warning statuses, the endpoint is ACTIVE (You can see that the active filter is ON).
If an endpoint DID NOT communicate in the last 30 days, no matter what the status was before that, the device will not appear in the Endpoint Managements page.
For more information on Active and Inactive endpoints, see Active Endpoints in Cynet .

Endpoint Actions

For each endpoint, there are a number of actions you can perform directly from the Endpoints Management page.
Remove/Uninstall an endpoint
Allow local reinstallation of the Cynet agent on a currently uninstalled host
Pause protection for an endpoint, if this is enabled for the group of which the endpoint is a member.
To remove and uninstall the Cynet agent from an endpoint:
Select the endpoint from which you want to remove the Cynet agent.
Click the Remove button right-hand side above the endpoints list.
Check that the endpoint name is the one you want to remove and click Continue .
After removing an endpoint from the Manage Endpoints page, you can then click Allow Install to allow local reinstallation of the Cynet agent on the host.

title: Email Security - Malicious Emails
content:
The Malicious emails page shows you the list of all emails that were tagged as malicious for the timeframe selected. You can choose the timeframe to display from the dropdown list, and you can sort the emails by any of the available columns.

Viewing Malicious Emails

To access the Malicious Emails page, navigate to Email Security > Malicious Emails from the Cynet toolbar.
On the Malicious emails page, you can select from the following timeframes:
All Time
Today
Last 7 Days
Last 30 Days
Date Range (provides a calendar to select start and end dates).
The list can be sorted by any of the available columns; date , subject , sender , recipients , violation or status . Additionally, you can search the emails according to email subject, recipient or sender email.
You can filter the list using one or more of the following filters: subject, sender, recipients, violation, status or threat category.
Clicking the arrow at the left-hand side of a row expands the details of that email for you to view. The expanded view includes additional details about the email including the following:
E mail ID: The Cynet ID given to the email.
Date: The date when the email was received.
Subject: The original email subject.
Recipients: The recipients of the email.
Action Taken: Describes the action taken on the email.
Malicious Content: Information about the malicious contents of the email, such as URLs that are classified as phishing, spam, untrusted, fraud, malware, or attachments that have prohibited file extensions.
From the Malicious Emails page, you can also add items to the blocklist as follows:
Select the email or emails that you want to add to the blocklist.
Click the three dots icon next to the Release button, and select Add Sender to Blocklist .
Click the Type dropdown to select the type of items to block (Domain, Sender email, recipient email, or File SHA256).
[Optionally] add any additional items you want to add to the blocklist.
Click Add .
For emails which were quarantined, you can preview them from the Malicious Emails page, by selecting the email and clicking Preview . The preview opens a PDF view of the email.
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security Overview
Email Security Settings
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ

title: Email Security - Allowlist and Blocklist
content:
The allowlist and blocklist pages in the Email Security solution allow you to set specific senders, recipients, email domains, attachment types, or sha256 file types that are either always allowed or always blocked. This allows you to block what you know is bad and allow what you know is trusted without the need to check on a case by case basis.

In both the allowlist and the blocklist you can search for specific items using the Search bar. Adding an Item to the Allowlist

In both the allowlist and the blocklist you can search for specific items using the Search bar.
To add an item to the Email Security allowlist:
Navigate to Email Security > Allowlist from the Cynet toolbar.
Click + Add.
Select the type of item to add from the dropdown (available options include Domain, Sender Email, Recipient Email, and File SHA256).
Enter the name of the item to add to the allowlist (for example, an email address or domain).
Press the Enter key on your keyboard.
[Optional] Enter another item name of the same type to add additional items.
Click Add .

Adding an Item to the Blocklist

To add an item to the Email Security blocklist:
Navigate to Email Security > Blocklist from the Cynet toolbar.
Click + Add.
Select the type of item to add from the dropdown (available options include Domain, Sender Email, Recipient Email, and File SHA256).
Enter the name of the item to add to the blocklist (for example, an email address or domain).
Press the Enter key on your keyboard.
[Optional] Enter another item name of the same type to add additional items.
Click Add .
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security - Overview
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security - Settings
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ

title: How to Connect Google Workspaces
content:
Connect your Google Workspaces account to Cynet 360 to view misconfigurations in the CSPM&SSPM page.

Prerequisite

If you don't have a Google project, create one . Cynet CSPM scans every project individually.

Connecting Google Workspaces

To connect your Google Workspaces account:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Integrations .
Click on the Google Workspaces integration.
Connect to the Security Posture & Inventory (SSPM) service by toggling the connector to On .
Select the Google account you want to connect to.
Enter your Google account password and click Continue .
Review the permissions Google is requesting and click Continue .
Optionally click Test to test the connection to Google Workspaces.
Note: If you have a session length set for Google Cloud services, you must ensure Cynet is set as a Trusted app on the App access control page in Google Workspaces, and mark the Exempt trusted apps checkbox under the Reauthentication Policy setting in Google Cloud session control .

title: Email Security - Quarantine
content:
Email Security includes the ability to quarantine malicious emails to prevent them from reaching the inbox. These emails can be seen from the Malicious Emails tab in the Email Security section, and you can perform various actions on them, such as previewing emails, releasing them from quarantine, adding senders to a blocklist, or deleting them entirely.
Quarantined emails can be released or deleted for up to 30 days from receipt, after which their status changes to "Quarantine - Expired".

Quarantine Settings

To turn on the email quarantine setting:
1. Navigate to Email Security > Settings .
2. In the Malicious Emails Policy section, select Quarantine .
3. Click Save .
To view and perform actions on quarantined emails:
1. Navigate to Email Security > Malicious Emails .
2. View emails with the Quarantined status.
3. To release one or more emails from quarantine, select the checkboxes next to the emails you want to release, and click Release .
Once an email is released from quarantine, the email is resent to the original recipient, and will not be stopped again by Cynet.
4. To delete one or more emails, select the checkboxes next to the emails you want to delete, click the 3 dots menu to the right of the Release button, and click Delete .
5. To add the senders of one or more emails to a blocklist, select the checkboxes next to the emails whose senders you want to add to the blocklist, click the 3 dots menu to the right of the Release button, and click Add Sender to Blocklist .
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security - Overview
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security - Email Trace
Email Security - Settings
Email Security - Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - FAQ

title: Enhanced MSSP Operations Migration Notes
content:
As of version 4.20, Cynet has introduced a revamped Users and Sites Management interface, offering a streamlined and user-friendly experience. This new UI simplifies the process of managing Sites, Users, and Roles, making it more intuitive and efficient for administrators. The enhanced interface provides a cleaner layout, intuitive navigation, and improved workflow, enabling seamless management of user accounts, site configurations, and access control across the platform .

Managing Users

Cynet's revamped User Management page streamlines the process of adding, editing, and deleting user accounts, while also enabling effortless management of multi-factor authentication for existing users.
In addition to the simple interface for user management, admins no longer set passwords for new users. Instead, new users receive email invitations and complete registration themselves.
Another useful change allows granting a user access to all sites, including existing ones and any future sites created.

Managing Roles

Managing roles is now easier using Cynet’s new Role Management page UI.

Managing Sites

Cynet’s new Site Management page lets you monitor, manage, and create sites in a convenient and easy manner.
Cynet now provides a hierarchical view of your sites. When managing multiple customers with several sites each, you can assign each customer's sites to a dedicated site manager. This allows site managers to view and manage only the sites assigned to them, ensuring clear separation and access control across different customer environments.

Simplified M SSP Admin Abilities

The new Users and Sites Management UI simplifies the admin abilities of the MSSP Operator and Operator Manage Site roles. In the past, users with these roles who had access to multiple (but not all) sites were able to make changes across sites, such as creating new sites and creating new users with the same access level. This is no longer the case.
Only users that have access to all sites, and have the Operator or Operator Manage Site roles can create new sites and users on the MSSP level. Operators and Operator Manage Site users who have access only to some sites can still create new users on the sites they have access to.
To allow a user with one of these roles to have the ability to manage multiple sites, yet not all, you can create additional levels of hierarchy with the new Site Management option. This will give the user admin abilities for all sites under the new level of hierarchy, allowing the user the ability to manage all sites and users under that level.
For example, in the images below:
User1: Has access to all sites shown in both images. This allows this user to manage sites and users across the MSSP.
User1: Has access to all sites shown in both images. This allows this user to manage sites and users across the MSSP.
User2: Has access to Child 1 & Child 2 (in the second image, these are under the West Division hierarchy). In Image 1, User2 cannot manage users and sites. In image 2, creating the West Division (Site Manager) hierarchy allows User2 to manage sites and users under the scope of the West Division level.
User2: Has access to Child 1 & Child 2 (in the second image, these are under the West Division hierarchy). In Image 1, User2 cannot manage users and sites. In image 2, creating the West Division (Site Manager) hierarchy allows User2 to manage sites and users under the scope of the West Division level.
Image 1
Image 2
For additional information on the simplified MSSP Admin capabilities, see Cross Customer Users .

sections.heading: Managing Sites
sections.text:
Cynet’s new Site Management page lets you monitor, manage, and create sites in a convenient and easy manner.
Cynet now provides a hierarchical view of your sites. When managing multiple customers with several sites each, you can assign each customer's sites to a dedicated site manager. This allows site managers to view and manage only the sites assigned to them, ensuring clear separation and access control across different customer environments.

sections.heading: Simplified M SSP Admin Abilities
sections.text:
The new Users and Sites Management UI simplifies the admin abilities of the MSSP Operator and Operator Manage Site roles. In the past, users with these roles who had access to multiple (but not all) sites were able to make changes across sites, such as creating new sites and creating new users with the same access level. This is no longer the case.
Only users that have access to all sites, and have the Operator or Operator Manage Site roles can create new sites and users on the MSSP level. Operators and Operator Manage Site users who have access only to some sites can still create new users on the sites they have access to.
To allow a user with one of these roles to have the ability to manage multiple sites, yet not all, you can create additional levels of hierarchy with the new Site Management option. This will give the user admin abilities for all sites under the new level of hierarchy, allowing the user the ability to manage all sites and users under that level.
For example, in the images below:
User1: Has access to all sites shown in both images. This allows this user to manage sites and users across the MSSP.
User1: Has access to all sites shown in both images. This allows this user to manage sites and users across the MSSP.
User2: Has access to Child 1 & Child 2 (in the second image, these are under the West Division hierarchy). In Image 1, User2 cannot manage users and sites. In image 2, creating the West Division (Site Manager) hierarchy allows User2 to manage sites and users under the scope of the West Division level.
User2: Has access to Child 1 & Child 2 (in the second image, these are under the West Division hierarchy). In Image 1, User2 cannot manage users and sites. In image 2, creating the West Division (Site Manager) hierarchy allows User2 to manage sites and users under the scope of the West Division level.
Image 1
Image 2
For additional information on the simplified MSSP Admin capabilities, see Cross Customer Users .

title: Managing Integrations by Group for MSP Users
content:
MSP users can manage integrations either per tenant or per Group.
To manage integrations per Group:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations .
Turn the Manage integrations by Group toggle to On .
Select the group for which you want to manage integrations.
Note: To connect each group to a different Office 365 account, select the groups one at a time, and connect each group to the relevant Office 365 account.

title: Endpoint Misconfigurations
content:
Endpoint Security Posture Management (ESPM) logic helps enterprises maintain a robust security posture by ensuring that security policies are consistently applied and enforced across all systems and users.
Cynet's ESPM features include the Misconfigurations page and the Vulnerabilities (CVEs) page (see also Endpoint Vulnerability Management ).
Endpoint Misconfiguration rules are designed to manage and enforce security policies within the customer environment, ensuring that all systems are configured according to best practices. This involves specifying logic for access control, data protection, user authentication, and more across all endpoints, with continuous scanning. These measures help ensure consistent security practices, reduce the risk of cyber attacks, and facilitate the quick identification and mitigation of threats. This approach is also useful for auditing purposes, ensuring compliance with regulations, and identifying areas for improvement.
The main Misconfigurations screen shows all the risks across your organization, their severity, category, the software products they impact and more, as well as the number of endpoints impacted. It shows the number of endpoints running the related software products whose configuration is ok in green , and the number of endpoints with the configuration risk in the relevant severity color .
Note: Misconfigurations that do not currently apply to any endpoints will show zero misconfigurations in orange, and the number of correctly configured machines in green (see image).
You can also drill down to specific risks and endpoints. When expanding a particular line, you see important details including how to remediate. For each risk, you can see the risk prioritization, categorization, and other important context including mapping to MITRE tactics and techniques. Usually there are multiple remediation options with detailed instructions on exactly the script or command to run. You can copy and paste that script into a Run Command remediation action for the relevant endpoints, to fix the risk.
You can also see the number of impacted endpoints and click on the number of endpoints to go to the screen with details of all impacted endpoints.
It is also possible to ignore endpoint misconfigurations by clicking on the Ignore button. Ignoring a misconfiguration can be done at the organization level or at the endpoint level. If you ignore a misconfiguration at the organization level, it is still possible to unignore it for specific endpoints. This allows you to avoid being notified about misconfigurations that are not of interest to you and simplifies your alert management.
Note: The Endpoint Misconfigurations page is only available for Windows operating systems running the required hardware specifications .

Misconfiguration Rules

Cynet's misconfiguration rules can be divided by the different components they relate to as well as the different categories of protection.
The different categories include:
Administrator Protection: Protects against abuse of administrator users. For example, ensuring that the built-in Windows Administrator user is disabled.
Application Protection: Protects against misuse of applications. For example, ensuring that AutoRun and AutoPlay are disabled for new devices added to the system.
Credential Protection: Protects against misuse or abuse of login credentials. For example, ensuring that the system allows resetting of computer account passwords, or prevents the storage of passwords and credentials.
Infrastructure Protection: Protects against attacks on infrastructure. For example, ensuring that domain members require a strong session key.
Malware Protection: Protects against malware attacks. For example, ensuring that Windows Smart Screen is enabled.
Network Protection: Protects against misuse of network connections. For example, ensuring that network services use strong authentication and not basic authentication.
Office Protection: Protects against abuse via Office 365 applications. For example, ensuring that macros are blocked, or that Office ActiveX is disabled.
Security Management: Protects against security breaches. For example, ensuring access to the camera is disabled.
User Protection: Protects against attacks on users. For example, ensuring automatic logons are disabled, or that local accounts without passwords are disabled.
Misconfiguration components include:
Accounts
Application
Network
OS

Prerequisites

In order to use the Endpoint Misconfigurations feature, ensure your system has the following prerequisites:
PowerShell version 4.0 or higher must be installed on the host and allowed to run.
The host's operating system must be Windows 10 or higher. The feature will not run on Windows XP or Windows 7.

Limitations

ESPM is currently available for All-in-One customers only.
Scanning currently occurs every 24 hours.
Note: It is normal to see events in the Windows Event Viewer related to PowerShell, about missing registry keys (such as RestrictRemoteSAM ) . These warnings are generated by our security validation scripts and are not a cause for concern. They do not indicate errors or security issues, but rather are part of Cynet's routine system checks. No action is needed surrounding these events. If a missing key poses a real security risk, it will appear in the management console or via the alerting mechanism, and not just through these informational PowerShell warnings.
If you have further questions or would like assistance interpreting any security-related findings, feel free to reach out to our support team.

title: Microsoft 365 Email Security Integration
content:
Email Flow

The flow of emails through Cynet Mail Protection is as follows:
The sender's mail server sends the email to the Microsoft 365 mail server.
The Microsoft 365 mail server receives the email.
The Microsoft 365 mail server sends the email to Cynet Mail Protection to be scanned.
Cynet Mail Protection sends the scanned email back to the Microsoft 365 mail server.
The Microsoft 365 mail server sends the email to the recipient's mail server.
The recipient receives the email in their inbox.

Prerequisites

Microsoft 365 email exchange.
Customer must have ‘Global Administrator’ and ‘Security Administrator’ permissions in the relevant Microsoft 365 account to perform the integration.
In order for email integration to work, t he 'G lobal Administrator' role must have an active office license that includes both of the following Microsoft license types (or higher): Microsoft Defender for Office 365 (Plan2) Microsoft 365 Business Basic or equivalent
Microsoft Defender for Office 365 (Plan2)
Microsoft 365 Business Basic or equivalent
Note: The account performing the integration must persistently retain the Global Admin privileges in order to maintain the integration.
To successfully complete the Microsoft 365 Email Security integration , the user who runs the integration must:
Belong to: Organization Management View-Only Organization Management
Organization Management
View-Only Organization Management
Have access to these RBAC role permissions: Role Management Remote and Accepted Domains Organization Transport Settings Organization Configuration Distribution Groups Transport Rules Transport Hygiene
Role Management
Remote and Accepted Domains
Organization Transport Settings
Organization Configuration
Distribution Groups
Transport Rules
Transport Hygiene

Integration

Setting up Email Security for Cynet requires you to connect the Microsoft 365 integration and then set the Email Protection toggle to On .
To set up Email Security:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations .
Click on the Microsoft 365 integration.
To turn on Email Protection, set the toggle for Email Protection to On .
To enable Email Protection only for a Limited User Group, mark the Limited User Group checkbox. To configure which emails are included in the Limited User Group, see Microsoft 365 Limited User Group Setup for Email Security .
To configure which emails are included in the Limited User Group, see Microsoft 365 Limited User Group Setup for Email Security .
Click Close .
Note: When Email Protection is activated only for the Limited User Group, o nly inboxes in the "email-protection-inline-group" group will be scanned.
Activating email protection can take up to two minutes. See FAQ below for what happens to emails during these two minutes.
If you are an MSP user managing integrations by Group, see Managing Integrations by Group for MSP Users for information on how to turn on this setting. Note that when managing integrations by group, each group must be connected to Microsoft 365 with a unique set of credentials.

title: On-Premise Autonomous Protection
content:
This page refers to on-premise environments. In SaaS, automated protection is managed by Cynet Automated Response Playbooks .
Autonomous Protection issues automated responses that complement initial detection and prevention alerts. Automated responses include:
Investigation: Queries and results that are either root cause or impact-oriented, based on the attack data provided by the initial alert.
Findings: Full discovery of an attack's root cause or other impacted entities like hosts, user accounts, additional malicious processes, or connection to external malicious addresses.
Remediation: Eradication of the malicious infrastructures and activities disclosed in initial alerts and their findings.
Automated Protection is available for a selected number of alerts, that trigger the following flows:
The results of an Automated Protection investigation are displayed in the Incident View .
To enable Automated Protection:
Navigate to Settings > Scan Groups > Autonomous Protection .
There are two operation modes:
Investigation : Cynet performs automated investigation into the root cause and its impact. It does not apply active remediation actions. Instead, it offers detailed remediation instructions to eliminate the disclosed malicious infrastructure and activity.
Investigation and Remediation : Cynet adds active remediation steps to complete the response process and eradicate the malicious presence and activity from hosts, user accounts, files, processes and network traffic.
If auto-remediation is configured for the originating host, remediation is automatically applied to all hosts in the flow.

title: Configuring and Using Single Sign On (SSO)
content:
Single Sign On (SSO) allows users to authenticate with a single identity provider (IDP) and access multiple applications without needing to repeatedly enter credentials.
To enable SSO login for users of a site:
Navigate to Settings > Users & Roles .
Select the SSO tab.
Click Connect on the desired identity provider.
For further instructions on configuring SSO for Google Workspace, see Configuring SSO for Google .
Once you have successfully connected SSO login for an identity provider, you can then set it up for individual users.
To set SSO login for a specific user:
Navigate to Settings > Users & Roles .
Select the user for which you want to enable SSO login.
Click Edit .
In the Login Method dropdown, select the desired login method ( Azure Active Directory or Google Workspace ).
Click Save .
Click Save .

Login to Cynet Using SSO

After SSO is configured for a user, that user is able to login to Cynet using SSO.
To login using SSO:
Go to your Cynet login page.
Enter your email address.
Click Login .
Login to your identity provider using your credentials.

title: All-in-One Dashboard
content:
Cynet's All-in-One dashboard emphasizes our unique All-in-One cybersecurity platform. The dashboard clearly shows the wide range of coverage Cynet provides and the findings on each protected entity.
The dashboard prominently displays alerts and risks, along with warnings about missing configurations.
You can use the intuitive drill-downs to view details for risks and alerts.
You can view the previous dashboard, which contains the alerts radar and graphs, in the "Main" tab.
The following table provides explanations of the different sections in the All-in-One Dashboard.
For MSSP customers who manage by Group, the SaaS & Cloud and Email Security sections include a Groups dropdown which enables you to select a specific group to view in the dashboard, rather than viewing data for all groups at the same time. To return to viewing all groups, you can select All from the Groups dropdown.

title: How to Send Logs to Cynet's CLM
content:
CLM can collect data from any data source that exports information in CEF-formatted syslog. Supporting a new data source requires Cynet to perform a certification process. Contact Cynet Support to request support for a new data source or to add a data source that does not support CEF-formatted syslog. Cynet evaluates requests based on several factors, such as market needs.
Cynet Support accompanies customers during the CLM onboarding process. As part of the onboarding process, you will be required to provide Cynet with 10-15 syslog messages for a non-certified data source. If you do not know how to send syslog samples, you can use the Cynet Sampler - Logstash configured for for sampling data from your network and sending it to Cynet.
The Microsoft 365 integration screen includes a built-in toggle for CLM. after connecting Microsoft 365, you can activate the toggle and enable Cynet 360 to receive logs from Office application and Azure AD . To view the logs, you must have a CLM license. Once the application is connected, it can take up to one hour to see the relevant events.
To set Fortigate firewall to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the Fortinet documentation .
To configure Fortigate Firewall for Logstash, refer to How to Configure Fortigate Devices for Logstash .
To set Cisco Meraki devices to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the Cisco Meraki documentation .
To set Cisco Firepower Device Manager to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the Cisco Firepower documentation .
To configure Cisco Firepower for Logstash, refer to How to Configure Firepower Management Console For Logstash .
To set Cisco Switch to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the Cisco Switch documentation .
To set Cisco ISE to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the Cisco ISE documentation .
To set VMWareESXi to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the VMWare documentation .
To set Palo Alto Networks firewall to send events to CLM correctly, refer to Palo Alto's documentation here and here .
To ensure proper formatting, do not copy and paste the message directly into the PAN-OS web interface. Instead, paste it into a text editor, remove any line breaks, then copy and paste into the web interface.
To set SonicWall to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the SonicWALL documentation .
To configure SonicWall Devices for Logstash, refer to How to Configure SonicWall Devices for Logstash .
To set IBM 3PAR or AS400 to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the IBM documentation .
To set QNAP NAS to send events to CLM correctly, refer to the QNAP documentation .

title: Email Security - Email Trace
content:
The Email Trace page shows the emails that were received by all inboxes in your Microsoft organization for the timeframe selected. You can choose the timeframe to display from the dropdown list, and you can sort the emails by any of the available columns.
On the Email Trace page, you can select from the following timeframes:
All Time
Today
Last 7 Days
Last 30 Days
Date Range (provides a calendar to select start and end dates).
The list can be sorted by any of the available columns; subject , recipients , sender , date , status or actions . Additionally, you can search the emails according to email subject, recipient or sender email.

Resending Original Emails

From the Email Trace page, you can also resend emails, which can fix encoding or deliverability issues.
To resend an email:
Navigate to Email Security > Email Trace from the Cynet toolbar.
S elect one or more emails to be resent.
Click the Send Original button at the top right-hand side of the page.
For more information on Email Security, see the following articles:
Email Security Overview
Email Security - Malicious Emails
Email Security Settings
Email Security Allowlist and Blocklist
Email Security - Quarantine
Email Security - FAQ
