title: API Users
content:
To allow access to Cynet's API, an API user needs to be created in the Cynet system. The API user provides the Access-Key and Secret-Key which will later be used to authenticate to the Cynet API.
An API user has a name and a role, and can be given access to all sites, or to specific sites.

Creating a New API User

Navigate to Settings > Users & Roles from the Cynet toolbar, and click on the API Users tab.
Click New .
Enter a name for the API user and select a role from the dropdown list.
Select which sites the API user should have access to from the sites list.
Click Add .
Copy the Secret Key displayed and save it somewhere you can access it later.
Click Close .
Upon creation of the API user, two keys are created:
The Access-Key (used in place of the Username when accessing the API) is visible at any time from the API Users page in Cynet.
The Secret-Key (used in place of the Password when accessing the API) is shown only onc e , immediately after the user is created.
Important! Since the Secret Key is only displayed once, immediately after creating the user, you must copy and save it in a safe place before you close it.

Accessing the API

Access to the API is performed using the Get Token API. For more information, see the API Reference Guide .

sections.text:
To allow access to Cynet's API, an API user needs to be created in the Cynet system. The API user provides the Access-Key and Secret-Key which will later be used to authenticate to the Cynet API.
An API user has a name and a role, and can be given access to all sites, or to specific sites.

sections.heading: Creating a New API User
sections.text:
Navigate to Settings > Users & Roles from the Cynet toolbar, and click on the API Users tab.
Click New .
Enter a name for the API user and select a role from the dropdown list.
Select which sites the API user should have access to from the sites list.
Click Add .
Copy the Secret Key displayed and save it somewhere you can access it later.
Click Close .
Upon creation of the API user, two keys are created:
The Access-Key (used in place of the Username when accessing the API) is visible at any time from the API Users page in Cynet.
The Secret-Key (used in place of the Password when accessing the API) is shown only onc e , immediately after the user is created.
Important! Since the Secret Key is only displayed once, immediately after creating the user, you must copy and save it in a safe place before you close it.

sections.heading: Accessing the API
sections.text: Access to the API is performed using the Get Token API. For more information, see the API Reference Guide .

title: Report Scheduling
content:
Cynet provides the ability to schedule reports to be sent to specific recipients on a daily, weekly or monthly basis. Reports are sent on the specified day, at 7am UTC.
To create a new scheduled report, follow these steps:
Navigate to Reports > Report Scheduling from the Cynet toolbar.
Enter a name for the report.
Select the report type from the dropdown. Available reports include: Executive Report (PDF) IT Hygiene - Missing KBs on Host (CSV) IT Hygiene - Agent Validation (CSV) IT Hygiene - Application Patch Validation (CSV) IT Hygiene - Unauthorized Validations (CSV) Inventory Report (CSV)
Executive Report (PDF)
IT Hygiene - Missing KBs on Host (CSV)
IT Hygiene - Agent Validation (CSV)
IT Hygiene - Application Patch Validation (CSV)
IT Hygiene - Unauthorized Validations (CSV)
Inventory Report (CSV)
Select the scope of the report. You can choose between All and Selected Groups . If you choose Selected Groups , select the groups from the dropdown that appears. You can also choose to have separate reports for each group by marking the Separate Groups checkbox.
If you choose Selected Groups , select the groups from the dropdown that appears.
You can also choose to have separate reports for each group by marking the Separate Groups checkbox.
Select the Scheduling Period from the dropdown. You can choose between Daily, Weekly and Monthly.
If you chose Weekly or Monthly in the scheduling period, select the day of the week or month on which the report should be sent. For monthly reports, you can also choose to have it sent on the Last day of the month (regardless of what month it's for).
Enter the emails of the recipients to which the scheduled reports should be sent.
Click Create .

title: Incident View
content:
The graphical Incident View shows the events and related objects collected from multiple hosts that generated an incident. The Incident View features clickable integrated forensic artifacts that display full forensic information.
Alerts that trigger an automated investigation include an Incident View button.
The Investigation tab includes 4 sections:
Summary Text Blocks: Bottom-line results of the automated response process - description, root cause, impact, remediation actions, and further required actions.
Autonomous Responder: A graphic visualization of all the investigation steps, findings, and remediation actions.
Incident Timeline: A verbal description of the entire automated response process.
Incident Artifacts: A graphic representation of the malicious activity that triggered the alert. Click any of the artifacts to view its Details page and access its Action menu.
Each playbook run is summarized in the Incident view, available from the alert that triggered the playbook.

title: Guest Users
content:
In certain scenarios, a customer may wish to give access to their system to someone who already has a user in the Cynet system. For example, a customer who uses Cynet through one of our partners may wish to give the partner some access to their system. In this instance, when the customer admin adds the partner to their system, the partner is created as a Guest user, since they already exist as their own user in the Cynet system.
Guest Users are also useful if you want a user to have access to more than one site, without being an admin for these sites.
The authentication method for guest users is determined according to the authentication type on their primary user. In the Login Method column on the Users and Roles page, their login method will appear as "Guest".

Example Scenario for Guest User Creation

Customer Apex Solutions wants to give access to one or more of their sites to John Smith from Partner Vector Technologies. Since John Smith already exists in Vector Technologies' system, a new user cannot be created for him. Instead, a Guest user will be created for John Smith in Apex Solutions' system.
When adding a guest user, it is possible to give that user a different role from the role they have on their regular user account. For example, John Smith might have the Operator Manage Sites role in Vector Technologies' system, but in Apex Solutions' system, their Guest user might have the Read Only role.

title: How to Configure SonicWall Devices for Logstash
content:
Following these steps will get you through the configuration of a SonicWall firewall’s syslog options to send to an on-site Cynet-Logstash server.

Prerequisites

These instructions assume that you have already configured a Logstash server based on the install package from your SaaS tenant.
Review SonicWall's information about setup for syslog servers . This document provides more explicit details; in particular, the command-line content for mode and transmission.
Ensure you have the IP address of your Logstash server ready. It will be the value in place of “ {$logstash_IP} “ in a later step.

Part 1: Creating an Address Object

SonicWall’s syslog instructions work best if you create an Address Object that will become the value for the Name field.
Click on Object in the top tabs (in the same bar as the hamburger menu).
Select Addresses from the side bar.
Click + Add to the right of the search field. This will open a wizard window.
Enter a short and clear name for your Cynet Logstash server.
Zone Assignment will most likely be LAN, since your Cynet Logstash server would be in your local network, or DMZ if it's kept away from the rest of the local network.
Type defaults to Host. Changing this will change the IP Address field below it to other types of fields, such as MAC for a MAC address.
Provide the value matching the Type, such as 10.1.2.3 for a Host Type's IPv4 address.
Click Save .

Part 2: Adding Syslog Settings

Navigate to Device > Log > Syslog . This will open the default tab of Syslog Settings.
Select the Syslog Servers tab.
Click + Add to the right of the search field. This will open a wizard window:
Accept the default Event Profile (usually zero) unless your admin has specific needs.
The Name or IP Address field will reject an FQDN. Use the Address Object from Part 1 as a Name.
Enter the following values in the wizard fields: Port: 5132 Server Type: Syslog Server Syslog Format: Enhanced Syslog Syslog Facility: (default is "Local use 0") Syslog ID: Cynet
Enter the following values in the wizard fields:
Port: 5132
Port: 5132
Server Type: Syslog Server
Server Type: Syslog Server
Syslog Format: Enhanced Syslog
Syslog Format: Enhanced Syslog
Syslog Facility: (default is "Local use 0")
Syslog Facility: (default is "Local use 0")
Syslog ID: Cynet
Syslog ID: Cynet
Enable Event Rate and Data Rate Limiting only if you find your bandwidth use to be a problem.
Bind to VPN Tunnel and Create Network Monitor Policy in NDPP Mode will not allow you to set anything unless you have previously configured a VPN or other network monitor policy.
Click Save .
As of October 2024, Cynet Logstash can only process CEF logs as UDP. SonicWall uses UDP by default for syslog transmission.

title: How to Configure Firepower Management Console for Logstash
content:
Following these steps will get you through the configuration of a Cisco Firepower Management Console (FMC) syslog options to send to an on-site Cynet-Logstash server.

Prerequisites

These instructions assume that you have already configured a Logstash server based on the install package from your SaaS tenant.
Please review Cisco's information about FMC setup for syslog servers . This document provides more explicit details; in particular, the command-line content for mode and transmission.
Ensure you have the IP address of your Logstash server ready. It will be the value in place of “ {$logstash_IP} “ in a later step.

Firepower Instructions

Log into the Cisco Firepower Device Manager.
Click on the gear icon at the top right of the window, then click on Configuration at the top left.
From the left-hand menu, click on Audit Log . This will bring up a set of fields: Send Audit Log Syslog: Enabled Hosts (Up to 5): provide the IPv4 address of your Logstash server Facility: Choose the types of logs that you wish to send. "LOCAL7" is a common value, as is “ALERT”. Severity: the default of 'INFO' is sufficient. Tag (optional): you can leave this blank. If you wish to confirm the source of your logs, create a tag that makes sense. The final two fields deal with sending audit logs to a web server, unrelated to Logstash.
Send Audit Log Syslog: Enabled
Hosts (Up to 5): provide the IPv4 address of your Logstash server
Facility: Choose the types of logs that you wish to send. "LOCAL7" is a common value, as is “ALERT”.
Severity: the default of 'INFO' is sufficient.
Tag (optional): you can leave this blank. If you wish to confirm the source of your logs, create a tag that makes sense.
The final two fields deal with sending audit logs to a web server, unrelated to Logstash.
Click Test Syslog Server and wait for success or failure.
After you receive the message "Syslog server has been reached.", click Save .
After you receive the message "Syslog server has been reached.", click Save .
This will send via port 514, whereas Cynet Logstash is expecting port 5114.
If you already have a process for forwarding the port on incoming internal TCP traffic, please follow your corporate policies. The section below is a guideline.

Port Redirection

The following command on Windows will redirect port traffic. Note that this may be superseded by Windows Firewall configurations or Group Policies: netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenaddress={$logstash_IP} listenport=514 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=5114
Check the logstash-plain log on the Logstash server for the redirected traffic.

title: Cynet Endpoint Best Practices
content:
It is highly recommended to maintain your endpoint settings in accordance with Cynet's Best Practice settings.
To check your settings:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group whose settings you want to review.
Within any group, click on the Best Practice button to reset settings to best practice.
Alternately, to manually set your groups to best practice settings, ensure your endpoint settings comply with the following:

Endpoint Protection Settings

Cynet recommends having endpoint protection in P revent mode based on best practice.
Enable Prevent on the Endpoint Protection (EPP) setting.
Ensure that all the settings in the Advanced Settings dialog are enabled (the Ransomware decoy files and Antivirus scan Profiles are not required for best practice) as shown in the image below:

Network Detection & Response (NDR) Settings

Cynet recommends having NDR in Prevent mode based on best practice.
Enable Prevent on the Network Detection & Response (NDR) setting.
Click on Network Detection & Response (NDR) > Advanced Settings . Turn on Network Threats . Turn on Domain Filtering . For more information about Domain Filtering, see Domain Filtering .
Turn on Network Threats .
Turn on Domain Filtering . For more information about Domain Filtering, see Domain Filtering .

Agent Settings

Cynet recommends the following Agent settings based on best practice:
From the group you want to edit, click Agent Settings > Manage . Turn on Automatic Agent Update . This setting means that the Cynet agent will be automatically updated whenever there is a new version. Turn on Perform backups to enable rollback . This setting means that regular backups are performed on the endpoint, which enables Cynet to rollback the endpoint to a safe point when necessary. Enable Set an Agent Uninstall Password. This setting is automatically enabled on the tenant level, however it is also recommended to enable it here on the group level.
Turn on Automatic Agent Update . This setting means that the Cynet agent will be automatically updated whenever there is a new version.
Turn on Perform backups to enable rollback . This setting means that regular backups are performed on the endpoint, which enables Cynet to rollback the endpoint to a safe point when necessary.
Enable Set an Agent Uninstall Password. This setting is automatically enabled on the tenant level, however it is also recommended to enable it here on the group level.

title: Web Content Filtering
content:
Cynet's Web Content filtering allows companies to optimize workforce productivity by controlling and limiting access to specific categories, like social media, entertainment, and other non-essential websites, while simultaneously creating a more focused and efficient work environment. Moreover, web content filtering helps organizations maintain regulatory compliance, protect sensitive data, and create a clear framework for acceptable internet usage, ultimately supporting both security objectives and organizational performance goals.
Important! Web Content Filtering is only available for Windows endpoints with the required hardware specifications .

Cynet's Web Content Filtering Features

Our Web Content Filtering offers e xtensive network detection and prevention for endpoints, empowering you to:
Categorize and Control Web Access: Configure and enforce granular policies based on domain categories, allowing or blocking access to websites based on their content (e.g., gambling, social media, etc.).
Utilize Streamlined Management: Create and manage content filtering policies effortlessly through Cynet's intuitive interface, with options to allowlist domains, users, or hosts.
Get Enhanced Visibility: Monitor web activity with detailed logs, including domain, category, and action taken, for comprehensive analysis and reporting.
Improve Network Performance: Optimize bandwidth by restricting access to high-bandwidth activities like streaming and large file downloads.

Key Benefits

Increased productivity control lets you manage employee web access, minimize distractions, and enforce acceptable use policies.
Simplified security management gives you centralized policy configuration and reporting for seamless control across all endpoints.
Improved compliance enables you to meet regulatory requirements by controlling access to sensitive or inappropriate content.
Endpoint-level protection: Cynet's device-level web content filtering maintains protection regardless of where employees connect, eliminating the security gaps inherent in network-based solutions that only function within specific perimeters.
For additional settings and to enable Web Content Filtering, see Configuring Web Content Filtering . To view the full list of Web Content Filtering categories currently supported, see Web Content Filtering - Categories List .

For additional settings and to enable Web Content Filtering, see Configuring Web Content Filtering . To view the full list of Web Content Filtering categories currently supported, see Web Content Filtering - Categories List .

title: File Monitoring
content:
File Monitoring provides increased visibility into forensics on executables and file-based activities on protected assets. Information on process actions is available in the Process Instance page.
File Monitoring is only available in Windows hosts, version 7 and above.
Create a File Monitoring profile to specify the file extension you want Cynet 360 to monitor. It is not recommended to monitor all extensions by default as this creates a heavy burden on the Cynet EPS and might impact performance. You can create multiple File Monitoring profiles and apply different profiles to different Scan Groups. See below for a list of extensions included in the best practice File Monitoring profile.
On-premise customers: Before enabling File Monitoring you must configure the Cynet Analytics Server .
To configure File Monitoring:
Navigate to Settings > Profiles > File Monitor .
In the top-right corner, click Create .
Enter a profile name.
Add file extensions for Cynet 360 to monitor: Enter the file extension, then click +. You can export this list to a JSON file by clicking Export . OR Upload a JSON file that contains a list of file extension by clicking Import .
Specify how often (in minutes) the EPS must send File Monitoring information to the server.
Click Save .
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the Group for which you want to enable File Monitoring and click Edit .
Click on Advanced Settings under Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Turn on the File Monitoring Logs toggle and and select a File Monitoring profile from the dropdown list.
Click Ok .
Click Save .
File Monitoring data is displayed in the Forensic section > Files > Monitor . Expand a file event using the + to display all data collected, parsed, and indexed based on key pair values.
For a more in-depth query into the collected data, use the Free Text Search option to search for raw data on events.

Centralized Log Management

File Monitoring data is also displayed in the Forensic > Analytics section. For more details on how to use Analytics, see Centralized Log Management .

Best Practice Profile

The best practice profile (default extension list) is based on Cynet research and customer feedback. To view the default extension list:
Navigate to Settings > Profiles > File Monitor
Click Edit on the row of the Best Practice list.

title: How to Configure Checkpoint Devices for Logstash
content:
Following these steps will get you through the configuration of a Check Point Security Management syslog options to send to an on-site Cynet-Logstash server.
Checkpoint devices call their overall interface Gaia. This handles both the web interface, Gaia Portal, and their command-line interface, Gaia Clish . Since Clish offers no extra features to configure log exports, we have focused on the website-based steps.
Warning: Checkpoint devices are among a class of network equipment that do not (as of yet) allow syslog output from any port other than 514. Be sure to review the port forwarding section at the end and review your corporate policies.

Prerequisites

These instructions assume that you have already configured a Logstash server based on the install package from your SaaS tenant.
Please review Checkpoint’s documentation for configuring system logging using the Gaia Portal , their web interface brand. This document provides more explicit details; in particular, the command-line content for mode and transmission.
Ensure you have the IP address of your Logstash server ready. It will be the value in place of “ {$logstash_IP} “ in a later step.

Checkpoint Instructions

Log into the Checkpoint device’s web page as admin.
From the left-hand menu, open the System Management section and click on System Logging .
Make sure all three of the following checkboxes have been checked: Send Syslog messages to management server Send audit logs to management server upon success configuration Send audit logs to syslog upon successful configuration
Make sure all three of the following checkboxes have been checked:
Send Syslog messages to management server
Send audit logs to management server upon success configuration
Send audit logs to syslog upon successful configuration
If you use a tool to set your web browser to dark mode, you may need to disable it for the coming steps. The fields in the Add Remote System Logging may otherwise be illegible.
Below the System Logging checkboxes, click on the Add button under Remote System Logging .
This will bring up a set of fields: IP Address : Enter the four tercets of the IPv4 address of your Cynet Logstash server. If you try to paste the full address, it will only paste the first tercet (i.e.: ‘192’ or ‘10’) then throw an error. You will need to type the address and use the right arrow key to change fields. Priority: the default is All , which will include debug logs. The ' Info ' level should be sufficient. The Add Remote Server Logging Entry wizard sits above the System Logging page Click OK and review your results.
IP Address : Enter the four tercets of the IPv4 address of your Cynet Logstash server. If you try to paste the full address, it will only paste the first tercet (i.e.: ‘192’ or ‘10’) then throw an error. You will need to type the address and use the right arrow key to change fields.
If you try to paste the full address, it will only paste the first tercet (i.e.: ‘192’ or ‘10’) then throw an error. You will need to type the address and use the right arrow key to change fields.
Priority: the default is All , which will include debug logs. The ' Info ' level should be sufficient. The Add Remote Server Logging Entry wizard sits above the System Logging page
Priority: the default is All , which will include debug logs. The ' Info ' level should be sufficient.
Click OK and review your results.
Click OK and review your results.
Apply the settings.
Apply the settings.
This will send syslog entries via port 514, whereas Cynet Logstash is expecting Checkpoint traffic on port 5106. If you already have a process for forwarding the port on incoming internal traffic, please follow your corporate policies. Our port forwarding page is a guideline.

Port Redirection (NAT port forwarding)

You will need to configure port forwarding, either on your Cynet Logstash server, or on another network device that provides local Network Address Translation (NAT) services between your syslog device and the Logstash server.
As a safety check, we offer the Windows steps below. Note that this may be superseded by Windows Firewall configurations or Group Policies: Open a command prompt as an Administrator. Enter the following, replacing {$logstash_IP} with the IP address of your Logstash server: netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenaddress={$logstash_IP} listenport=514 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=5106 Check the logstash-plain log on the Logstash server for the redirected traffic.
As a safety check, we offer the Windows steps below. Note that this may be superseded by Windows Firewall configurations or Group Policies:
Open a command prompt as an Administrator.
Open a command prompt as an Administrator.
Enter the following, replacing {$logstash_IP} with the IP address of your Logstash server: netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenaddress={$logstash_IP} listenport=514 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=5106
Enter the following, replacing {$logstash_IP} with the IP address of your Logstash server:
netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenaddress={$logstash_IP} listenport=514 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=5106
netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenaddress={$logstash_IP} listenport=514 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=5106
Check the logstash-plain log on the Logstash server for the redirected traffic.
Check the logstash-plain log on the Logstash server for the redirected traffic.
For Linux devices running Logstash, we suggest reviewing iptables configuration with your networking team.
For Linux devices running Logstash, we suggest reviewing iptables configuration with your networking team.

title: How to Connect Zoom
content:
Connect your Zoom account to Cynet 360 to view misconfigurations in the SSPM page. To connect Zoom, you need to provide your Zoom credentials.
To connect to Zoom:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations from the Cynet toolbar.
Click on the Zoom integration.
Turn on the Connect toggle.
Enter your Zoom credentials in the Email Address and Password fields, and click Sign In .
To disconnect Zoom from Cynet:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations from the Cynet toolbar.
Click on the Zoom integration.
Turn off the Connect toggle.

title: Inactivity Timeout Duration
content:
As part of the User settings, you can adjust the inactivity timeout duration, specifying how long users remain logged in before being automatically logged out.
This setting controls both console users and API authentication tokens.
To set the inactivity timeout duration:
Navigate to Settings > Users & Roles > Settings tab from the Cynet toolbar.
Enter the desired timeout duration in minutes. The default timeout is 20 minutes, and you can change it to anywhere between the minimum of 10 minutes and maximum of 1,440 minutes.
Click Save .
Note: Inactivity logout time can be configured either on the Account level or the Site level. If it is configured differently for each, the Account level is the value that will be prioritized.

title: Microsoft 365 Limited User Group Setup for Email Security
content:
When you choose to use Email Security with a limited user group, Cynet automatically creates a group called email-protection-inline-group in Microsoft online exchange. To begin receiving scanned emails, you need to assign users to the group in the Microsoft 365 admin center. For instructions on how to do this, see below.
In the Microsoft 365 Admin Center, go to Active Teams and Groups > email-protection-inline-group .
Click Add Members .
Repeat for each member you want to add to the group.
Disable and Enable Email Protection in the Cynet Console to expedite integration. Microsoft caches transport rules and group memberships for performance, so changes can take up to 4 hours to update. To expedite the integration, disable and then re-enable the Email Protection toggle.

title: Checkpoint Sandbox - Send to Analysis
content:
Use the CheckPoint Sandbox Send to Analysis remediation action to send a file to Checkbox Sandbox for malware analysis.
In the current Beta version, the SOAR remediation actions are provided as scripts, per customer request. For more information, contact Cynet.
You must store your Checkbox Sandbox credentials in the Security Vault before creating a new Custom Remediation action. In the newly created vault object:
For name and key enter: TE-APIKEY
Activate the vault object and enable agent remediation.
Get the CheckPoint API key from the customer. According to checkpoint docs, on local installation of Threat Emulation the API Key should be located in this path of the machine: /opt/CPUserCheckPortal/phpincs/conf/TPAPI.ini
Add the following variable:
Click the lock icon to encrypt and save.
Now you must create a new custom remediation action using the following parameters:
You must enter all the arguments, in the order in which they appear
In the Actions page, you can check if the file was sent successfully. For the analysis results, navigate to Checkpoint Sandbox.

title: How to Connect Microsoft 365
content:
Connect your Microsoft 365 account to view misconfigurations in the SSPM page, to get logs from Microsoft 365 for CLM, and to use Email Security features.
Note: To connect Cynet to Microsoft 365, the Microsoft 365 E3 license or higher, or the Microsoft Entra ID P1 license or higher is required. Additionally, to get logs for CLM, the " Privileged Role Administrator " role is required.
To connect to Microsoft 365:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations from the Cynet toolbar.
Click on the Microsoft 365 integration.
Turn the toggles for the services which you want to connect to On .
Enter the Microsoft 365 credentials of the account with which you want to log in on the screen that appears. If you are already logged into Microsoft with a different account, click Use another account , and then log in with the credentials you want to use. Note: The user connecting to M365 must have the M365 Global Admin role.
Click Next .
Open your Microsoft Authenticator app, and enter the number shown on the screen, to sign in.
Click Accept at the bottom of the page, to accept the permissions required for connection to Cynet.
After accepting the permissions, you are taken back to the Cynet Integrations page, and the Microsoft 365 integration should show as connected.

title: Remediation Via Remote Desktop Access
content:
Cynet's Remote Desktop Access capability empowers customers to securely access and manage their endpoints directly from the Cynet console. Whether for troubleshooting, system maintenance, or other administrative tasks, this feature provides seamless remote control, reducing the need for physical access or third-party tools.

Key Benefits

Centralized Access: Control all your endpoints from one unified interface.
Enhanced Security: Remote sessions are encrypted, ensuring safe communication.
Streamlined Operations: Simplify troubleshooting and maintenance without additional tools.
Real-Time Monitoring: Observe and manage endpoint activities in real time.

Supported Operating Systems

Remote Desktop Access is supported on the following operating systems:
Windows Servers running version 2016 and above.
Endpoints running Windows 10 and above.

Getting Started

Before using Remote Desktop Access, ensure the following:
The Cynet agent is installed and running on the endpoint.
The endpoint is connected to the Cynet network .
Your network is open to internet .
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Forensic > Hosts .
Select a host and search for Remote Desktop Access in the Actions tab.
Allow 10-30 seconds for the host to establish connection. On slower internet connections, this may take longer .
Wait for Endpoint user approval (will timeout if the user doesn't respond within 30 seconds).
The target user receives an approval request and must approve the request. If the user doesn't approve the request within 30 seconds, it will automatically be approved. Once approved, the following screen appears:
Select the user and enter the password.
To copy and paste data within the remote desktop session, use the tab at the left-hand side of the screen.

FAQs

Q: Is the session activity logged? A: Absolutely. All sessions are logged for auditing and security purposes.
Q: Can multiple users access the same endpoint simultaneously? A: No. To ensure security, only one user can access an endpoint at a time.
Q: Is the communication secured? A: Yes. The communication is encrypted the entire way with SSL.

title: Windows Events
content:
The Cynet agent can collect logs from Windows-based endpoints, and include the data in the endpoint forensic information.
Cynet 360 includes a default Windows Events profile. The Best Practice profile includes Windows events that can be extremely valuable when investigating suspected malicious presence within the environment. The collected events provide direct insight into potential user account and networking anomalies. You can apply Windows event collection to specific Groups.
On-premise customers: Before enabling Windows Events you must configure The Cynet Analytic server. For Instructions, see Cynet Analytics Server .
To enable Windows event monitoring:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group for which you want to enable Windows event monitoring.
Click on Advanced Settings under Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Turn on the toggle for Windows Events and select a profile from the dropdown list.
Click Ok .
Click Save .
Windows events are displayed in the Forensic section > Hosts > Windows Events . Expanding a specific event displays full details of the collected event, divided into metadata fields.

Centralized Log Management

Windows events are also displayed in the Forensic > Analytics section. For more details on how to use Analytics, see Centralized Log Management .

Configuring Sysmon Events in Cynet

If you have Sysmon installed on your endpoints, Cynet gives you the option to collect Sysmon events in additional to other Windows events. The following procedure explains how to configure Sysmon events in Cynet.
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Profiles > Windows Events .
Click Create .
Enter a name for the new profile.
In the Rules section, under Type , select Event Log Full Name from the dropdown.
Enter Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational as the value, and click the plus icon, to add the rule.
In the Polling Interval section, enter the time interval (in minutes) at which the endpoint scanner should search Windows events. The default is 30 minutes.
Click Save . The new profile appears beneath the Best Practice profile.
To set the new profile for a specific group:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group for which you want to set the new Windows event profile.
Click on Advanced Settings under Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Make sure the toggle for Windows Events is turned on, and select the new profile from the dropdown list.
Click Ok .
Click Save .
Windows events are displayed in the Forensic section > Hosts > Windows Events . Expanding a specific event displays full details of the collected event, divided into metadata fields. To see which events are Sysmon events, check the System Channel field in the expanded event.

Cynet 360 includes a default Windows Events profile. The Best Practice profile includes Windows events that can be extremely valuable when investigating suspected malicious presence within the environment. The collected events provide direct insight into potential user account and networking anomalies. You can apply Windows event collection to specific Groups.
On-premise customers: Before enabling Windows Events you must configure The Cynet Analytic server. For Instructions, see Cynet Analytics Server .
To enable Windows event monitoring:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group for which you want to enable Windows event monitoring.
Click on Advanced Settings under Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Turn on the toggle for Windows Events and select a profile from the dropdown list.
Click Ok .
Click Save .
Windows events are displayed in the Forensic section > Hosts > Windows Events . Expanding a specific event displays full details of the collected event, divided into metadata fields.

sections.heading: Centralized Log Management
sections.text: Windows events are also displayed in the Forensic > Analytics section. For more details on how to use Analytics, see Centralized Log Management .

sections.heading: Configuring Sysmon Events in Cynet
sections.text:
If you have Sysmon installed on your endpoints, Cynet gives you the option to collect Sysmon events in additional to other Windows events. The following procedure explains how to configure Sysmon events in Cynet.
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Profiles > Windows Events .
Click Create .
Enter a name for the new profile.
In the Rules section, under Type , select Event Log Full Name from the dropdown.
Enter Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational as the value, and click the plus icon, to add the rule.
In the Polling Interval section, enter the time interval (in minutes) at which the endpoint scanner should search Windows events. The default is 30 minutes.
Click Save . The new profile appears beneath the Best Practice profile.
To set the new profile for a specific group:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group for which you want to set the new Windows event profile.
Click on Advanced Settings under Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Make sure the toggle for Windows Events is turned on, and select the new profile from the dropdown list.
Click Ok .
Click Save .
Windows events are displayed in the Forensic section > Hosts > Windows Events . Expanding a specific event displays full details of the collected event, divided into metadata fields. To see which events are Sysmon events, check the System Channel field in the expanded event.

title: Gmail Email Security Integration
content:
Setting up Cynet's Email Security for Gmail requires the following high-level steps:
Enable the Cynet Email Protection toggle for the Google Workspaces integration in Cynet .
Configure the Inbound Gateway in Google Workspaces .
Create the Cynet Gateway Host in Google Workspaces .
Configure the integration in Google Workspaces. For Limited Group integration: Create the group to which the Cynet Forward rule should be applied. Create the Cynet Forward rule. For Full organization integration: Create the Cynet forward rule.
For Limited Group integration: Create the group to which the Cynet Forward rule should be applied. Create the Cynet Forward rule.
Create the group to which the Cynet Forward rule should be applied.
Create the Cynet Forward rule.
For Full organization integration: Create the Cynet forward rule.
Create the Cynet forward rule.
Important!
You MUST turn on the toggle in Cynet before doing any configuration in Google Workspaces. If you neglect to do this, you will not receive any of your emails.

Email Flow

The flow of emails through Cynet Mail Protection is as follows:
The sender's mail server sends the email to the Gmail mail server.
The Gmail mail server receives the email.
The Gmail mail server sends the email to Cynet Mail Protection to be scanned.
Cynet Mail Protection sends the scanned email back to the Gmail mail server.
The Gmail mail server sends the email to the recipient's mail server.
The recipient receives the email in their inbox.

Setting Up Email Security for Gmail

Step 1: Enable the Cynet Email Protection toggle for the Google Workspaces integration.
In the Cynet console, navigate to Settings > Integrations > Google Workspace .
Toggle the Email Protection option to On .
Log in with your Google account.
Choose an account and enter your password then click Next .
Select what Cynet can access and click Continue .
Step 2: Configure the Google Workspaces Inbound Gateway for Cynet.
Navigate to https://admin.google.com/ac/apps/gmail/spam .
Edit the Inbound Gateway section, and make sure the Inbound Gateway checkbox is checked.
In the Gateway IPs section: Add the following gateway IPs: 18.210.90.90/32 18.200.154.75/32 52.17.93.142/32 52.2.165.75/32 Ensure that the following checkboxes are checked: Automatically detect external IP (recommended) Require TLS for connections from the email gateways listed above
Add the following gateway IPs: 18.210.90.90/32 18.200.154.75/32 52.17.93.142/32 52.2.165.75/32
18.210.90.90/32
18.200.154.75/32
52.17.93.142/32
52.2.165.75/32
Ensure that the following checkboxes are checked: Automatically detect external IP (recommended) Require TLS for connections from the email gateways listed above
Automatically detect external IP (recommended)
Require TLS for connections from the email gateways listed above
In the Message Tagging section, ensure that the checkbox for Message is considered spam if the following header regexp matches is checked, and enter the follwoing Regexp: X-Cynet-Action: moveToJunk
X-Cynet-Action: moveToJunk
Select the Message is spam if regexp matches option.
Click Save .
Step 3: Create the Cynet Gateway Host
Navigate to https://admin.google.com/ac/apps/gmail/hosts .
Click Add Route .
Enter the name as Cynet GW .
Specify the email server using the following details: Single Host If your Cynet server is based in the EU, use eu.smtp.cymail.io If your Cynet server is based in the US, use us.smtp.cymail.io Set port 25 . Ensure the following checkbox is checked: Require mail to be transmitted via a secure (TLS) connection (Recommended)
Single Host If your Cynet server is based in the EU, use eu.smtp.cymail.io If your Cynet server is based in the US, use us.smtp.cymail.io
If your Cynet server is based in the EU, use eu.smtp.cymail.io
If your Cynet server is based in the US, use us.smtp.cymail.io
Set port 25 .
Ensure the following checkbox is checked: Require mail to be transmitted via a secure (TLS) connection (Recommended)
Require mail to be transmitted via a secure (TLS) connection (Recommended)
Click Save .
Click Save again.
Step 4: Configure the integration in Google Workspaces:
Use this procedure for Limited Group integration . Limited group integration allows you to use Cynet Email Security on a set group of users, before configuring the integration for your entire organization.
Use this procedure for Full Organization integration . Full organization integration configures Cynet Email Security for your entire organization.

title: Remediation Via Remote Shell
content:
Cynet's Remote Shell capability empowers customers to securely access and execute commands on their endpoints directly from the Cynet console browser. Whether for troubleshooting, system maintenance, or other administrative tasks, this feature provides seamless command-line access, reducing the need for physical access or third-party tools.

Key Benefits

Centralized Command Access: Execute commands on all your endpoints from one unified interface.
Enhanced Security: Remote shell sessions are encrypted, ensuring safe communication.
Streamlines Operations: Simplify troubleshooting and maintenance without additional tools.
Real-Time Interaction: Execute commands and view responses in real-time.

Supported Operating Systems

Remote shell is supported on the following operating systems:
Windows servers running 2008R2 and above.
Endpoints running Windows 8 and above.
Endpoints running Windows 7 support only Remote Shell 32 Bit .

Getting Started

Before using remote shell, ensure the following:
The Cynet agent is installed and running on the endpoint.
The endpoint is connected to the Cynet network.
Your network is open to internet.
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Forensic > Hosts .
Select a host and search for Remote Shell or Remote Shell 32 Bit in the Actions tab. Choose Remote Shell for standard operations on 64-bit systems. Choose Remote Shell 32 Bit when working with 32-bit applications or systems.
Choose Remote Shell for standard operations on 64-bit systems.
Choose Remote Shell 32 Bit when working with 32-bit applications or systems.
Allow 10-30 seconds for the host to establish connection. On slower internet connections, this may take longer.
Once connected, you can begin entering commands directly in the shell interface.

FAQs

Q: Is the session activity logged? A: Absolutely. All command executions are logged for auditing and security purposes.
Q: Can multiple users access the same endpoint's shell simultaneously? A: No. To ensure security, only one user can access an endpoint's shell at a time.
Q: Is the communication secured? A: Yes. The communication is encrypted the entire way with SSL.
Q: What's the difference between Remote Shell and Remote Shell 32 Bit? A: Remote Shell is optimized for 64-bit Windows environments, while Remote Shell 32 Bit is designed specifically for 32-bit applications and systems. Choose the appropriate option based on your target environment.

title: Gmail Limited Group Integration for Email Security
content:
For Limited Group integration in Gmail, perform the following steps (for full organization integration, see Full Organization Integration ):
Step 1: Create the limited group in Google Workspaces:
Navigate to https://admin.google.com/ac/groups .
Click Create group .
Enter the following group details: Group Name: Enter email-protection-inline-group as the group name. Group Email: Enter the email address for the group (the first half should correspond to your group name, email-protection-inline-group and after the @ sign should be your company domain (for example, @mycompany.com). Group Description: Enter a description for your group, such as "Cynet protection group". Group Owners: Optionally enter a Group Owner . Ensure the Mailing checkbox is checked.
Group Name: Enter email-protection-inline-group as the group name.
Group Email: Enter the email address for the group (the first half should correspond to your group name, email-protection-inline-group and after the @ sign should be your company domain (for example, @mycompany.com).
Group Description: Enter a description for your group, such as "Cynet protection group".
Group Owners: Optionally enter a Group Owner .
Ensure the Mailing checkbox is checked.
Click Next .
Enter the following in the Access Type settings: Select Custom . Mark the following for Group Owners : Who can contact group owners Who can view conversations Who can view members Who can manage members In the Who can join the group section, select the Anyone in the organization can join option.
Select Custom .
Mark the following for Group Owners : Who can contact group owners Who can view conversations Who can view members Who can manage members
Who can contact group owners
Who can view conversations
Who can view members
Who can manage members
In the Who can join the group section, select the Anyone in the organization can join option.
Click Create Group .
[Optional]: Add members to the group: Click Add members to email-protection-inline-group . Enter the email addresses of the members you want to add to the group. [Optional]: If you have already tested the limited user group and want to now add all the users in your organization to the group, check the Add all current and future users of [your organization] to this group with All Email setting checkbox. Click Add to Group .
Click Add members to email-protection-inline-group .
Enter the email addresses of the members you want to add to the group.
[Optional]: If you have already tested the limited user group and want to now add all the users in your organization to the group, check the Add all current and future users of [your organization] to this group with All Email setting checkbox.
Click Add to Group .
Step 2: Create the Cynet Forward Rule in Google Workspaces:
Navigate to: https://admin.google.com/ac/apps/gmail/compliance .
In the Content compliance section, click Add another rule .
Set the rule name as Cynet Forward .
In Email messages to affect select the following checkboxes: Inbound Internal - Sending
Inbound
Internal - Sending
In the section Add expressions that describe the content you want to search for in each message , set the following: Set the dropdown to If ALL of the following match the message Exclude the following source IPs: 18.200.154.75/32 18.210.90.90/32 Exclude mail with the header: X-Cynet-Tenant-Token: <tenant> IMPORTANT! The tenant token must be copied from the Cynet integration!
Set the dropdown to If ALL of the following match the message
Exclude the following source IPs: 18.200.154.75/32 18.210.90.90/32
18.200.154.75/32
18.210.90.90/32
Exclude mail with the header: X-Cynet-Tenant-Token: <tenant> IMPORTANT! The tenant token must be copied from the Cynet integration!
In the section If the above expressions match, do the following , check the following checkboxes: Add X-Gm-Original-To header Add X-Gm-Spam and X-Gm-Phishy headers Check the Add custom headers checkbox, then: Add the following custom header: Header Key: X-Cynet-Tenant-Token Header Value: <token> (the tenant token copied from the Cynet integration) Check the Change route checkbox. From the dropdown, select Cynet GW . Under Encryption (onward delivery only) , check the Require secure transport (TLS) checkbox. Click Show Options . Under Account types to affect, check the following checkboxes: Users G roups Under Envelope filter, check the Only affect specific envelope recipients checkbox. Select Group membership (only received mail) from the dropdown. Select the email-protection-inline-group from the groups list.

title: Time Zone for Alerts and Forensic
content:
You can change the time zone in which the time of Alerts and Forensics are displayed. If you don't set your preference, the default is for the system to show local browser time.
To change your preferred time zone:
Navigate from the Cynet toolbar to Settings > Configuration .
Scroll down to the Time Zone section, and select the preferred time zone from the dropdown menu.
Click Save .
Note: This setting changes what's displayed in the Cynet console UI. This means that previously generated data and alerts shown in the UI will also be shown in the selected time zone.

title: FAQ for Email Security
content:
To start with, you can try resetting the settings to " Best Practices ".
Alternately, if you have made changes to your configuration, and don't want to reset them, you can try turning off Real Time Scanning and Tag External Emails .
Additionally, Cynet provides an Emails Trace tab which displays all emails with the option to re-send the original email without scanning or changing it.
In some cases, the email will be intercepted by Microsoft 365 quarantine, in this case the admin will have to release it from the Microsoft 365 management console.
We recommend starting out with the default settings. The Tag with Warning Banner option allows you to make sure the system does not interfere with the standard email flow. After gaining confidence in the detections, you can set it to Move to Junk or Quarantine to make sure malicious emails do not arrive in the recipient’s inbox.
The regular link protection scans all links in the email’s body on arrival. To ensure continuous protection, Real-time link scanning replaces the URLs with a Cynet link and when the user visits the URL, it is scanned in real-time.
All the emails are stored in a dedicated encrypted private S3 bucket per Customer before handling. If the email is not stored, Cynet will not acknowledge the email was accepted and the email provider will continue to send it until acknowledged. Since the system is fully stateless at any point of time it will handle unhandled emails which are stored persistently as if nothing happened.
In addition, the service is built on a highly available infrastructure, both multi zone and regional redundancy to ensure that no email loss occurs. In the catastrophic event that Cynet is unavailable (both availability zones are down), the standby site located on a different region will start to handle the emails. This will be done automatically without the need for the Customer to change anything.
When you send an email, it follows a structured process to ensure safety, deliverability, and functionality. Here’s a clear breakdown of how it works:
Email Rules We Apply:
Move to Junk: If our system marks an email as spam, it adjusts the spam level so the email server routes it to the junk folder by default. However, stricter tenant policies may prevent it from even reaching the junk folder.
Add a Safety Warning: If flagged, we add a warning banner to indicate the email may not be safe.
Forward for Processing: Emails are forwarded to our system for analysis, with necessary tenant-specific identifiers attached. Exception: Emails sent from our IPs skip this forwarding step to avoid loops.
Connectors in Use:
To Cynet: Sends emails securely from Microsoft or Gmail to our system.
From Cynet: Sends emails back to Microsoft or Gmail after processing, with proper permissions to avoid being flagged as spoofed.
Email Flow Explained:
The email is received by Microsoft or Gmail and passes through all the rules set in the tenant’s account.
If eligible, the email is forwarded securely to our system.
We securely store the email, then analyze its content (links, attachments) and make necessary updates (e.g., rewriting links).
The email is then sent back to Microsoft or Gmail for final delivery.
Microsoft or Gmail processes the email again before sending it to its intended destination (inbox, junk, or quarantine).
Notes:
Microsoft's decision to route an email to junk is influenced by multiple factors, including SPF/DKIM results, sender reputation, email content, and other contextual elements.
During forwarding of the email back to Microsoft for final delivery, SPF and DKIM signatures are broken, as the original sender’s authentication details cannot remain intact through our processing. This is an expected part of the workflow.
Key Points:
Microsoft retries email delivery for up to 24 hours if our systems experience downtime.
We ensure all emails are processed thoroughly, and only after confirming successful receipt from Microsoft do we start analysis.
If any internal processing issues arise, we retry three times. If errors persist, we deliver the original email without modification.
This process ensures a seamless and secure flow, maintaining the highest standards of email safety and reliability.
When switching mailbox protection settings from "Limited User Group" to "Entire M365 Tenant", administrators should be aware of the following considerations:
Transition Characteristics:
The configuration change may take up to 2 minutes to fully propagate
During this transition period, two potential scenarios can occur:
Mail Forwarding Scenario : Emails forwarded to CYNET will be temporarily queued. Emails will be processed and scanned once CYNET connection is re-established.
Emails forwarded to CYNET will be temporarily queued.
Emails will be processed and scanned once CYNET connection is re-established.
New Incoming Mail Scenario : New emails received during the transition will be temporarily routed to a designated mailbox with CYNET scanning capabilities.
New emails received during the transition will be temporarily routed to a designated mailbox with CYNET scanning capabilities.
To successfully complete the Office 365 Email Security integration , the user who runs the integration must belong to the following role groups:
Organization Management
View-Only Organization Management
The user must also have access to the following RBAC role permissions:
Role Management
Remote and Accepted Domains
Organization Transport Settings
Organization Configuration
Distribution Groups
Transport Rules
Transport Hygiene

title: Cynet Analytics Server
content:
(Relevant to on-premise customers only)
Cynet uses an analytics server that you need to configure if you enable File Monitoring and Windows Events. For hardware requirements, click here .
To configure the Analytics Server:
Navigate to Settings > Configurations .
Select the Configure Analytics Server check box.
Enter the connectivity settings for the analytics server.
To test the settings, click Test Connectivity .
Click Save .

title: Active Endpoints in Cynet
content:
An endpoint that communicated with Cynet for at least 30 minutes in the last 30 days is considered active, and is counted as an "Active" endpoint in all UI locations:
Dashboard
Endpoints pages
Inventory report
MSP cross site view
An uninstalled or removed endpoint is not counted even if it was active in the last 30 days before its removal.

Identifying Endpoints

Cynet identifies endpoints by the combination of their host name and their HD Serial number.
It is possible for multiple endpoints with different names to have the same HD Serial number. For example, concurrent "persistent" VDIs. Cynet considers the VDI instance as a single endpoint (it consumes a single license, is listed as a single endpoint in the Forensic page, etc). Each VDI is protected and is counted separately, as with any other endpoint.
It is possible for multiple endpoints with the same name but different HD Serial numbers to be in the system in the following cases:
An MSP which has the same endpoint name in different end-customers.
A machine that has been replaced. During the 30-day window after replacement, both the old machine and the new machine are visible. You can choose to manually remove the old machine if you want to prevent it from being counted.
There are certain edge cases which can complicate endpoint identification. Following are the two scenarios with ways to mitigate them:
When a host is renamed: Scenario: When you rename a host, the new name will appear as a new endpoint. Mitigation: The old endpoint with the old name will be ignored automatically after 30 days (like a machine that was replaced). To remove the old endpoint immediately, you can manually remove the endpoint from the console and it will not be counted in the active endpoint count.
Scenario: When you rename a host, the new name will appear as a new endpoint.
Mitigation: The old endpoint with the old name will be ignored automatically after 30 days (like a machine that was replaced). To remove the old endpoint immediately, you can manually remove the endpoint from the console and it will not be counted in the active endpoint count.
When you have "non-persistent" VDIs using different names: Scenario: When you are running a pool of machines (let's say 20), however you do not run more than 2-3 machines at the same time. If all machines run at some point during the 30 day period, all machines in the pool will be counted as active. Mitigation: If possible, you should update the system to reuse existing machines rather than rotating the pool.
Scenario: When you are running a pool of machines (let's say 20), however you do not run more than 2-3 machines at the same time. If all machines run at some point during the 30 day period, all machines in the pool will be counted as active.
Mitigation: If possible, you should update the system to reuse existing machines rather than rotating the pool.

title: Fixing Endpoint Misconfigurations
content:
You can fix risks found in the Endpoint Misconfigurations page directly from the Cynet console. You can also undo fixes that were performed by Cynet.
To fix or unfix a risk:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to the Endpoint Misconfigurations page.
Select the misconfiguration you want to fix and click on the Endpoints button at the top right.
Click the Fix/Unfix icon on the row of the endpoint you want to fix/unfix.
It is also possible to bulk fix/unfix multiple endpoints at the same time:
From the endpoints drilldown page, select the endpoints to fix/unfix.
Click the Fix/Unfix button at the top right-hand side of the page.
Note: The Fix button only appears for endpoints that are currently online.
Fixing a risk is an asynchronous operation. This means that when you fix a risk, the system sends a command to the endpoint, which can take some time to run.

Which Misconfigurations Can Be Fixed Automatically?

The following Endpoint Misconfigurations can be fixed automatically by using the Fix button (all others must be manually fixed using the instructions in the Misconfiguration details):
Ensure Automatic Logons is Disabled
Ensure 'Bluetooth Audio Gateway Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Bluetooth Support Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure camera access is Disabled
Ensure 'Configure Solicited Remote Assistance' is set to 'Disabled'
Ensure 'Do not allow COM port redirection' is set to 'Enabled'
Ensure 'Downloaded Maps Manager Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Geolocation Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Infrared Monitor Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Lanman Server' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Link-Layer Topology Discovery Mapper Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'LxssManager Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Microsoft FTP Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Microsoft iSCSI Initiator Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'OpenSSH SSH Server Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Peer Name Resolution Protocol Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Peer Networking Grouping Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Peer Networking Identity Manager Service' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Print Spooler' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'RDP Clipboard Redirection' is set to 'Disable'
Ensure 'Remote Registry' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Restricted Remote Administration Mode' is Enabled
Ensure 'Routing and Remote Access' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'Rpc Locator' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'TermService' is set to Disabled
Ensure 'User Mode Port Redirector' is set to Disabled

title: How to Integrate Cynet 360 with NinjaOne
content:
NinjaOne is a cloud-based remote monitoring and management (RMM) platform that lets IT teams and managed service providers monitor, manage, secure, and automate endpoints and servers from a single dashboard.
Cynet enables you to configure your NinjaOne app to install and monitor our system.
Integration of Cynet with NinjaOne requires the following:
Setup the Cynet Installer in NinjaOne.
Configure NinjaOne to Monitor the Cynet installation.
Configure NinjaOne to Monitor if Cynet is running.
Note: The Cynet Installer supports Windows 10 and 11, and Windows Server 2016 and above.

Setting Up the Cynet Installer in NinjaOne

From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Users & Roles > API Users tab.
Click New to create a new API user.
Give the user a meaningful name.
Set the User Role to Operator Manage Sites .
Select All sites .
Click Add .
Copy the Access-Key and Secret-Key and save them to a text file in a secure location.
From the Ninja One web UI, navigate to Administration > Organizations > Organization Custom Fields .
Create new fields as shown in the following table:
Automation – Read Only
API – Write Only (if you choose to set those fields from the API)
An optional field. If not supplied, the automation will use the default group from the Cynet console.
The resulting table should look like the following image:
From the Organization Custom Fields page, click Edit Custom Tab .
Add the new fields to whichever tab you want.
Navigate to Administration > Library > Automation .
Click Add > Installation .
In the General tab: Enter the name and description. Set the Operating System to Windows . Set the Architecture to All . Under Installer, select URL , and enter the following link: https://static.cynet.com/ninja_one/install_cynet.exe Set the Category to Maintenance . Set Run As to System . Optionally add one or more parameters such as the group name using the format group-name group1 . If you set one or more parameters, you can choose one of them to be the default value.
Enter the name and description.
Set the Operating System to Windows .
Set the Architecture to All .
Under Installer, select URL , and enter the following link: https://static.cynet.com/ninja_one/install_cynet.exe
Set the Category to Maintenance .
Set Run As to System .
Optionally add one or more parameters such as the group name using the format group-name group1 . If you set one or more parameters, you can choose one of them to be the default value.
Populating the fields can be done either manually or using an automation script.
Manual Custom Fields Update
Navigate to Dashboards and click on the desired organization (in the Overview section).
Click Custom and select the tab where you added the custom fields.
Click Edit (at the top right).
Enter the relevant information in each field and click Save .
Automatic Custom Fields Update (Mass Update)
Create a new NinjaOne web API key: Navigate to Administration > Apps > API . Click Add Client App . Enter the following information: Set the Application platform to Web . Enter a name. Enter the following Redirect URI: http://localhost:8000/callback Under Scopes, mark the checkboxes next to Monitoring and Management . Under Allowed grant types, mark the checkbox next to Authorization code . Click Add to get the Client ID and Client Secret. Save these credentials in a safe place.
Navigate to Administration > Apps > API .
Click Add Client App .
Enter the following information: Set the Application platform to Web . Enter a name. Enter the following Redirect URI: http://localhost:8000/callback Under Scopes, mark the checkboxes next to Monitoring and Management . Under Allowed grant types, mark the checkbox next to Authorization code .
Set the Application platform to Web .
Enter a name.
Enter the following Redirect URI: http://localhost:8000/callback
Under Scopes, mark the checkboxes next to Monitoring and Management .
Under Allowed grant types, mark the checkbox next to Authorization code .
Click Add to get the Client ID and Client Secret. Save these credentials in a safe place.
Download the mass update application .
Create a credential file: Create a new file called api_creds.json and save it in the same folder as the mass update application. Paste the following structure into it: { "client_id":"", "client_secret": "" } Populate the fields with the Client ID and Client Secret from the previously created API key. Save the file.
Create a new file called api_creds.json and save it in the same folder as the mass update application.
Paste the following structure into it: { "client_id":"", "client_secret": "" }
Populate the fields with the Client ID and Client Secret from the previously created API key.
Save the file.
Create a data file. This is a CSV file which stores the organization name, the custom field label, and the value you want to set. Create a new CSV file in the same folder with the mass update application and credential file. Enter the following columns in the file: organization_name field_name field_value Populate the file with the relevant values (see example in image below). Note: The CynetAccessKey and CynetSecretKey fields should be populated with the values of the Cynet API key created in Step 1 at the beginning of this document. These are different from the NinjaOne API key values created in Step 5 as part of preparing for the mass custom fields update. Save the file.
Create a new CSV file in the same folder with the mass update application and credential file.
Enter the following columns in the file: organization_name field_name field_value
organization_name
field_name
field_value
Populate the file with the relevant values (see example in image below). Note: The CynetAccessKey and CynetSecretKey fields should be populated with the values of the Cynet API key created in Step 1 at the beginning of this document. These are different from the NinjaOne API key values created in Step 5 as part of preparing for the mass custom fields update.
Save the file.
Open a PowerShell window in the folder with the three new files and run the following command: .\mass_custom_fields_update.exe -c api_creds.json --data-file data.csv --region na Ensure that the -c flag points to the API credential file and the --data-file points to the CSV file. Use --region to target a specific NinjaOne region: na: North America eu: EMEA oc: APAC
Ensure that the -c flag points to the API credential file and the --data-file points to the CSV file.
Use --region to target a specific NinjaOne region: na: North America eu: EMEA oc: APAC
na: North America
eu: EMEA
oc: APAC
When executing the script, you must authenticate it from a web browser. Click on the link provided in the CLI and authenticate with the NinjaOne login page.
When completed successfully, you will see a successful message page in the browser and the code in the terminal will run immediately, updating the custom fields in the different organizations.
When the script finishes executing all the updates, check the values and timestamp (of the last update) in the NinjaOne application.

Configure NinjaOne to Monitor the Cynet Installation

From the NinjaOne web UI, navigate to Administration > Policies > Agent Policies .
Select the desired policy for your Windows environment.
In the Conditions section, click Add a Condition .
Click Select a Condition .
Enter the following details: Condition: Software Presence: Doesn't exist Names: CynetEPS
Condition: Software
Presence: Doesn't exist
Names: CynetEPS
Click Add .
In the main Condition window, configure the following: Enter a Name, Severity and Priority. In the Auto-reset section, mark the checkbox for When no longer met . Add a notification channel. Under the Notify Technicians section, select Send notifications and mark the checkbox for Notify on Reset .
Enter a Name, Severity and Priority.
In the Auto-reset section, mark the checkbox for When no longer met .
Add a notification channel.
Under the Notify Technicians section, select Send notifications and mark the checkbox for Notify on Reset .
Click Add . When the application is removed, you receive a health warning:
If you want to integrate the Cynet EPS to install automatically: In the main Conditions page, under the Automations section, click Add . Select your Cynet Installer automation. Under Run As , select System , and set the default arguments. Click Apply .
In the main Conditions page, under the Automations section, click Add .

sections.text:
NinjaOne is a cloud-based remote monitoring and management (RMM) platform that lets IT teams and managed service providers monitor, manage, secure, and automate endpoints and servers from a single dashboard.
Cynet enables you to configure your NinjaOne app to install and monitor our system.
Integration of Cynet with NinjaOne requires the following:
Setup the Cynet Installer in NinjaOne.
Configure NinjaOne to Monitor the Cynet installation.
Configure NinjaOne to Monitor if Cynet is running.
Note: The Cynet Installer supports Windows 10 and 11, and Windows Server 2016 and above.

sections.heading: Setting Up the Cynet Installer in NinjaOne
sections.text:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Users & Roles > API Users tab.
Click New to create a new API user.
Give the user a meaningful name.
Set the User Role to Operator Manage Sites .
Select All sites .
Click Add .
Copy the Access-Key and Secret-Key and save them to a text file in a secure location.
From the Ninja One web UI, navigate to Administration > Organizations > Organization Custom Fields .
Create new fields as shown in the following table:
Automation – Read Only
API – Write Only (if you choose to set those fields from the API)
An optional field. If not supplied, the automation will use the default group from the Cynet console.
The resulting table should look like the following image:
From the Organization Custom Fields page, click Edit Custom Tab .
Add the new fields to whichever tab you want.
Navigate to Administration > Library > Automation .
Click Add > Installation .
In the General tab: Enter the name and description. Set the Operating System to Windows . Set the Architecture to All . Under Installer, select URL , and enter the following link: https://static.cynet.com/ninja_one/install_cynet.exe Set the Category to Maintenance . Set Run As to System . Optionally add one or more parameters such as the group name using the format group-name group1 . If you set one or more parameters, you can choose one of them to be the default value.
Enter the name and description.
Set the Operating System to Windows .
Set the Architecture to All .
Under Installer, select URL , and enter the following link: https://static.cynet.com/ninja_one/install_cynet.exe
Set the Category to Maintenance .
Set Run As to System .
Optionally add one or more parameters such as the group name using the format group-name group1 . If you set one or more parameters, you can choose one of them to be the default value.
Populating the fields can be done either manually or using an automation script.
Manual Custom Fields Update
Navigate to Dashboards and click on the desired organization (in the Overview section).
Click Custom and select the tab where you added the custom fields.
Click Edit (at the top right).
Enter the relevant information in each field and click Save .
Automatic Custom Fields Update (Mass Update)
Create a new NinjaOne web API key: Navigate to Administration > Apps > API . Click Add Client App . Enter the following information: Set the Application platform to Web . Enter a name. Enter the following Redirect URI: http://localhost:8000/callback Under Scopes, mark the checkboxes next to Monitoring and Management . Under Allowed grant types, mark the checkbox next to Authorization code . Click Add to get the Client ID and Client Secret. Save these credentials in a safe place.
Navigate to Administration > Apps > API .
Click Add Client App .
Enter the following information: Set the Application platform to Web . Enter a name. Enter the following Redirect URI: http://localhost:8000/callback Under Scopes, mark the checkboxes next to Monitoring and Management . Under Allowed grant types, mark the checkbox next to Authorization code .

Click Add to get the Client ID and Client Secret. Save these credentials in a safe place.
Download the mass update application .
Create a credential file: Create a new file called api_creds.json and save it in the same folder as the mass update application. Paste the following structure into it: { "client_id":"", "client_secret": "" } Populate the fields with the Client ID and Client Secret from the previously created API key. Save the file.
Create a new file called api_creds.json and save it in the same folder as the mass update application.
Paste the following structure into it: { "client_id":"", "client_secret": "" }
Populate the fields with the Client ID and Client Secret from the previously created API key.
Save the file.
Create a data file. This is a CSV file which stores the organization name, the custom field label, and the value you want to set. Create a new CSV file in the same folder with the mass update application and credential file. Enter the following columns in the file: organization_name field_name field_value Populate the file with the relevant values (see example in image below). Note: The CynetAccessKey and CynetSecretKey fields should be populated with the values of the Cynet API key created in Step 1 at the beginning of this document. These are different from the NinjaOne API key values created in Step 5 as part of preparing for the mass custom fields update. Save the file.
Create a new CSV file in the same folder with the mass update application and credential file.
Enter the following columns in the file: organization_name field_name field_value

These are different from the NinjaOne API key values created in Step 5 as part of preparing for the mass custom fields update.
Save the file.
Open a PowerShell window in the folder with the three new files and run the following command: .\mass_custom_fields_update.exe -c api_creds.json --data-file data.csv --region na Ensure that the -c flag points to the API credential file and the --data-file points to the CSV file. Use --region to target a specific NinjaOne region: na: North America eu: EMEA oc: APAC

When the script finishes executing all the updates, check the values and timestamp (of the last update) in the NinjaOne application.

title: Creating Custom Remediation Actions
content:
Creating a Custom Remediation Action enables you to add remediation actions to the ones built-into Cynet 360. A Custom Remediation Action can be a response to a file, host, user, or network alert. You can run a Custom Remediation Action on the Cynet Server (on-premise only), an endpoint, or on Execution Servers . The behavior of a remediation action changes according to the execution location and the menu from which you execute it. Custom Remediation Actions appear in the Remediation tab of the Action menu.
To Create a Script-Based Custom Remediation Action:
Navigate to Settings > Remediation > Custom Remediation tab.
In the top-right corner, click Create .
Select the Script type of customer remediation and click Create .
Enter a name for the remediation action.
Select the category to which the remediation action applies: File, host, user, or network.
Select the location on which to run the remediation action: Cynet server (on-premise only), endpoint, or Execution Server.
Upload the remediation script for the action: Select one or multiple files from your computer, then click Upload Files . You can edit the files directly in Cynet 360. If you uploaded multiple files, you must select the file to execute.
If the remediation script includes arguments, add them by type: Dynamic : Arguments related to the alert. Static : General arguments. Environmental : Arguments related to the user environment. Vault : Arguments stored in the Security Vault. Runtime : Arguments that you want to enter their value only when executing the remediation action. See the limitation below for using runtime arguments. Select the check box to add an argument prefix, add a prefix, then click +.
Dynamic : Arguments related to the alert. Static : General arguments. Environmental : Arguments related to the user environment. Vault : Arguments stored in the Security Vault. Runtime : Arguments that you want to enter their value only when executing the remediation action. See the limitation below for using runtime arguments. Select the check box to add an argument prefix, add a prefix, then click +.
Specify the operation timeout in minutes.
Click Save . The Custom Remediation Action is added to the available remediation actions.
The Custom Remediation Action is added to the available remediation actions.
Note : You can use an API call as a step within a playbook, which allows you to integrate with any external application. The information you gather can be parsed using the JSON parser and included in the playbook investigation.
To Create a Web API Call-Based Custom Remediation Action:
Navigate to Settings > Remediation > Custom Remediation tab.
In the top-right corner, click Create .
Select the Web API Call type of custom remediation, and click Create .
Enter a name for the remediation action.
Select the entity type to which the remediation action applies: File, host, user, or network.
[Optional] Select an integration to gather details from.
Select the method to use for the API call: GET, POST, PATCH, PUT, DELETE.
Enter the URL for the API call. You can optionally enter dynamic queries to the end of the URL, such as File Name, File Sha256, File Path, etc.
Enter any headers for the API call, and their values (click the plus button to add additional headers). You can optionally enter dynamic queries in the values.
Enter the body of the API call. You can also enter optional dynamic queries in the body of the API call.
Click Save .
If you add a Custom Remediation Action that contains a runtime argument to a playbook, the runtime argument value must be empty.
If you add a Custom Remediation Action that contains a runtime argument to an auto-remediation rule, you must enter a static value for the runtime argument. You must delete the auto-remediation rule before deleting or renaming the runtime argument.
