title: Reports at the Account Level
content:
Customers managing multiple sites can generate and download reports for one or more sites directly from the Reports page at the account level. Reports generated in this way may be generated either per site, or on the account level as an aggregated report (depending on the type of report requested). You can also schedule reports to run on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis and be sent to a specified email recipient.
Available report types include:
Executive Report
IT Hygiene Assessment Reports
Site Utilization Report: Account-level report which presents per-site utilization for active endpoints, CLM data, and unique integrations.
Endpoint Usage Report: Account-level report for MSPs and customers managing multiple sites which presents endpoint inventory for endpoints across the entire account.
Vulnerability Report: Site-level report for MSPs and customers managing multiple sites, presenting all CVEs per endpoint. Each row in the report has a single endpoint and a single CVE.
Misconfiguration Report: Site-level report for MSPs and customers managing multiple sites, presenting all misconfigurations per endpoint. Each row in the report has a single endpoint and a single misconfiguration.

Generating and Downloading Immediate Reports

To generate and download a report:
From the Cynet toolbar at the Account level, navigate to the Reports page.
Select a report type.
Click Generate .
Select the report scope (All Sites or Selected Sites).
If generating the report for selected sites, select which sites from the Select Sites dropdown.
If generating an Executive Report, select the period for which you want to generate the report.
Click Download .

Scheduling Reports

To schedule a report:
From the Cynet toolbar at the Account level, navigate to the Reports page.
Click Schedule .
Enter a name for the scheduled report.
Set the report level (either site level or account level).
Select one or more report types from the dropdown list.
Set the scope (either all sites, or selected sites and choose which sites from the dropdown list).
Select the scheduling period (for weekly reports also select which day of the week, and for monthly reports select which day of the month).
Enter the emails of the people who should receive the scheduled report(s).
Click Save .
The report or reports will be generated and sent to the specified recipient as scheduled.

title: Disable/Enable Active Directory User
content:
Cynet’s Disable/Enable AD User remediation allows you to securely disable or enable Active Directory accounts directly from the Cynet console. This automation streamlines user management and supports hybrid environments with Azure AD sync.

Key Benefits

Disable or enable AD accounts across your environment from the Cynet console.
Hybrid AD Support: Automatically triggers Azure AD sync for hybrid environments, ensuring changes propagate to the cloud.
Audit & Logging: All actions are logged for traceability and compliance.

Supported Environments

Windows Server 2012 and above (Domain Controller with Active Directory)

Getting Started

Prerequisites:
The Cynet agent is installed and running on the Domain Controller that the user is associated with.
The server is connected to the Cynet network.
Your network is open to the internet.
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Forensic > Users (or from Actions > Users , if an action has been performed on the user and it appears there).
Select a user and search for Disable User - AD (On-Prem) in the Actions tab.
Click the Disable User - AD (On-Prem) remediation action.
Click OK in the popup window that appears. Once the action succeeds, you will see a Success message and the action will appear in the Actions log (when you navigate to Actions > Users ).
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Forensic > Users (or from Actions > Users , if an action has been performed on the user and it appears there).
Select a user and search for Enable User - AD (On-Prem) in the Actions tab.
Click the Enable User - AD (On-Prem) remediation action.
In the prompt that appears, enter a new password for the user, and click OK . Once the action succeeds, you will see a Success message and the action will appear in the Actions log (when you navigate to Actions > Users ).
Note: When you enable an AD user account that was previously disabled, you are required to enter a new password for the AD user. They must then change their password once logged in.

Authentication & Execution

Q: What user credentials are used to execute the script since AD administrator credentials are no longer required?
A: The script runs under the system user. This allows it to disable users without requiring explicit AD administrator credentials.
Q: Does this require having an agent on the PDC (Primary Domain Controller)?
A: Yes, an agent is required on the PDC.

User Account Management

Q: Does the tool work for both regular and administrator AD accounts?
A: Yes, it works for both regular and administrator domain user accounts.
Q: Is the user logged out after the disable action from all machines?
A: No, disabling the account doesn't log out active sessions. It does attempt to log this user off of the AD machine where the script is run.
To log the user out of any other machine, you can use the Run Command Custom Remediation on any host the user is logged into. Execute quser to see active sessions and session IDs. Execute logoff <session id> to terminate specific user sessions on that machine.
Q: Can the actions be run multiple times?
A: The disable action can be run multiple times without issues. However, the enable action cannot be run on an already enabled user, so it will fail on the second attempt, or even on the first attempt if the user is already enabled.

Password Management

Q: What password requirements does the tool follow? What happens if the customer has stricter AD password policies?
A: The enable action forces the following constraints:
Length >= 8 characters
At least one uppercase letter
At least one lowercase letter
At least one digit
If these requirements aren't met, an error will appear in the log: "Password must be a string with at least 8 characters, containing at least one lowercase letter, one uppercase letter, and one digit."
If AD has additional requirements and the user's password doesn't meet them, the operation will fail with the message: "The password does not meet the length, complexity, or history requirement of the domain".
Q: Can the password inserted in the enable action be seen in logs and UI displays?
A: No, passwords are encrypted for security reasons in both debug logs and UI displays.
Q: What happens if my password is set to never expire?
A: Setting a user's password to never expire is not recommended. However, if a user's password is set this way, resetting the password after the user has been disabled and then re-enabled is not required.

title: CyOps Incident Response Settings
content:
The Cyops Incident Response settings enable you to control whether you would prefer CyOps to be proactive in remediating your incidents or to collaborate with you when remediating incidents. The Proactive option allows CyOps to remediate incidents faster, giving them authorization to carry out specific remediation actions without needing to wait for you to respond after being contacted, and increases the value provided by CyOps. The Collaborative option means that CyOps will always contact you before taking any remediation actions, which may lead to slower response times.

Proactive Incident Response

With Proactive CyOps enabled, you can specify exactly which remediation actions CyOps is authorized to perform without first initiating contact, and which they must still contact you before performing.
To enable proactive CyOps as an MSP:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > CyOps.
Select Proactive in the CyOps Incidence Response Settings page.
Select which actions CyOps will be able to perform proactively.
Optionally add any exclusions to each of the authorized remediation actions. Click Exclusions under the relevent remediation action. Select the site for which you want to add the exclusion. For Host Isolation exclusion, select either one or more Groups, or one or more Endpoints to exclude. For Disable users exclusions, enter one or more user names to exclude. Click Apply .
Click Exclusions under the relevent remediation action.
Select the site for which you want to add the exclusion.
For Host Isolation exclusion, select either one or more Groups, or one or more Endpoints to exclude.
For Disable users exclusions, enter one or more user names to exclude.
Click Apply .
Make sure the "I agree to the ProActive Terms & Conditions" checkbox is checked, and click Save .
Note: If you are a direct customer wanting to enable Proactive CyOps, please open a CyOps case requesting to enable it.

Collaborative Incident Response

With Collaborative CyOps enabled, CyOps MDR will contact you before taking any remediation actions.
The CyOps team monitors critical and high severity alerts only. When Collaborative incident response is enabled, the CyOps team contacts a customer ASAP via telephone only if there is reason to believe that a triggered alert or discovered artifacts in your environment require an Incident Response. Any follow-up communication will be performed via the ticketing system.
Cases in which the CyOps team contacts customers via email are:
Advise and assist with alert whitelisting
Penetration tests performed by the customer
Reply with file verdict for Send to SOC Analysis action, performed from the console
Address cases opened via the ticketing system
CyOps analysts identify one or more alerts that suggest an attack may be imminent or actively occurring and require containment
To enable Collaborative CyOps as an MSP:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > CyOps.
Select Collaborative in the CyOps Incidence Response Settings page.
Click Save .

sections.text: The Cyops Incident Response settings enable you to control whether you would prefer CyOps to be proactive in remediating your incidents or to collaborate with you when remediating incidents. The Proactive option allows CyOps to remediate incidents faster, giving them authorization to carry out specific remediation actions without needing to wait for you to respond after being contacted, and increases the value provided by CyOps. The Collaborative option means that CyOps will always contact you before taking any remediation actions, which may lead to slower response times.

sections.heading: Proactive Incident Response

With Proactive CyOps enabled, you can specify exactly which remediation actions CyOps is authorized to perform without first initiating contact, and which they must still contact you before performing.
To enable proactive CyOps as an MSP:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > CyOps.
Select Proactive in the CyOps Incidence Response Settings page.
Select which actions CyOps will be able to perform proactively.
Optionally add any exclusions to each of the authorized remediation actions. Click Exclusions under the relevent remediation action. Select the site for which you want to add the exclusion. For Host Isolation exclusion, select either one or more Groups, or one or more Endpoints to exclude. For Disable users exclusions, enter one or more user names to exclude. Click Apply .
Click Exclusions under the relevent remediation action.
Select the site for which you want to add the exclusion.
For Host Isolation exclusion, select either one or more Groups, or one or more Endpoints to exclude.
For Disable users exclusions, enter one or more user names to exclude.
Click Apply .
Make sure the "I agree to the ProActive Terms & Conditions" checkbox is checked, and click Save .
Note: If you are a direct customer wanting to enable Proactive CyOps, please open a CyOps case requesting to enable it.

title: Configuring SSO for Google
content:
To configure SSO to connect to Google:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Users & Roles > SSO .
On the Google Workspace tile, click Connect .
To complete the configuration and allow Cynet login using Google Workspace, you need to make Cynet a Trusted App in Google Workspace, as follows:
Go to https://admin.google.com/ac/owl/list?tab=configuredApps .
Click Configure new app .
Search for " 426288918503-6b3a5h9ma8o8lhd04hcadhj7k6s321fj.apps.googleusercontent.com ".
Select it and set access to Trusted .
Click Save .
Once you have completed the configuration, you can add new users with Google SSO login as described in Configuring and Using Single Sign On (SSO) .

title: How to Connect Salesforce
content:
Connect your Salesforce account to Cynet 360 to view misconfigurations in the SSPM page. To connect Salesforce, you need to provide your Salesforce credentials.
To connect to Salesforce:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations from the Cynet toolbar.
Click on the Salesforce integration.
Turn on the Security Posture & Inventory (SSPM) toggle.
Enter your Salesforce credentials in the Username and Password fields, and click Log In .
Note: The following OAuth Scopes are used by Cynet to initiate and maintain the integration with Salesforce:
Access the identity URL service (id, profile, email, address, phone)
Manage user data via APIs (api)
Perform requests at any time (refresh_token, offline_access)

title: Web Content Filtering - Configuration
content:
To access Web Content Filtering, navigate to Web Content Filtering from the Cynet toolbar.
Important! Web Content Filtering is only available for Windows endpoints with the required hardware specifications .
The Web Content Filtering page contains the following tabs:
Network Activity: Shows you all the sites that have been visited in the date range selected. You can filter by protocol, category, policy or status.
Allowlist: Shows a list of all domains and sites that have been added to allowlists. You can also add new sites and domains to the allowlist from this tab.
Blocklists: Shows a list of all the Web Content Filtering blocklists. Each blocklist contains one or more blocked domains. You can also add and edit Web Content Filtering blocklists from this tab. To apply a blocklist to a Group, go to the Web Content Filtering > Profiles tab, modify the profile that the Group is assigned to, and add the blocklist to the profile.
Profiles: Shows a list of all existing web content filtering policies and allows you to create new policies.
To add a new site or domain to an allowlist:
Navigate to Web Content Filtering > Allowlist tab.
Click Add .
Enter allowed criteria in at least one of the fields. Either a domain which should be allowlisted or a specific user or host to allowlist.
Click Save .
To create a new blocklist:
Navigate to Web Content Filtering > Blocklists tab.
Click New.
Enter a name and description for the new blocklist.
To add domains to the blocklist individually: Click New . Enter the domain to add to the blocklist. Optionally mark the Include Subdomains checkbox to include subdomains. Click Add .
Click New .
Enter the domain to add to the blocklist.
Optionally mark the Include Subdomains checkbox to include subdomains.
Click Add .
To load a predefined list of domains to the blocklist: Click the arrow to the right of the Delete button. Click Import CSV . Upload the CSV file with your predefined list of domains. The CSV file should be formatted with a domain colum and a subdomainsIncluded column as shown in the image below: Click Continue .
Click the arrow to the right of the Delete button.
Click Import CSV .
Upload the CSV file with your predefined list of domains. The CSV file should be formatted with a domain colum and a subdomainsIncluded column as shown in the image below:
Click Continue .
Click Save .
To add a new Web Content Filtering profile:
Navigate to Web Content Filtering > Profiles tab.
Click New .
Enter a name and optional description for the new policy.
Mark the checkboxes for the categories you want to track and/or block. For a full list of categories currently supported, see Web Content Filtering - Categories List .
Optionally expand the Blocklists section and mark the checkboxes for Blocklists you want to track or block for this profile.
In the Assigned Groups section, select the groups to which the profile should be applied.
Click Save .

Web Content Filtering Endpoint Pop-Up

When a user attempts to access a domain restricted by your organization's WCF policy, a pop-up notification is displayed on the endpoint. The pop-up provides information about the blocked domain, including the action taken, domain category, process involved, timestamp, and a description of why access was restricted.
To configure endpoint alert display settings:
Navigate to Settings > Groups and edit the group for which you want to configure the pop-up alert notifications.
Under Agent Settings , click Manage .
Toggle the Display Alerts on Endpoint setting to On .
Click Ok .
Click Save .

title: Web Threat Protection
content:
Web Threat Protection (also known as Malicious Domain Protection ) focuses on security. It functions as a digital doorkeeper, blocking access to websites that may compromise your network and preventing users from accessing harmful or compromised websites known to distribute malware, conduct phishing activity, or support other cyber threats. This layer protects against web-borne attacks that could compromise endpoints or lead to data breaches.
Moreover, Web Threat Protection establishes controls and limits on available content, enhancing security, and optimizing network efficiency.

Cynet's Web Threat Protection Features

Our Web Threat Protection offers extensive network detection and prevention for endpoints, empowering you to:
Prevent Threats in Real-Time: Leverage accurate and up-to-date malicious domain database and a local cache for rapid identification and mitigation of threats, ensuring minimal latency for legitimate web traffic.
Utilize Flexible Remediation Options: Choose between detection and prevention modes to either alert on, or alert and block connections to malicious domains, providing adaptable security measures.
Utilize Streamlined Management: Create and manage allowed and blocked domains, users, or hosts effortlessly through Cynet's intuitive interface.

Key Benefits

Strengthened endpoint security extends Cynet's robust protection to endpoints, closing security gaps and safeguarding against web-borne threats.
Endpoint-level protection: Cynet's device-level Web Threat Protection maintains protection regardless of where employees connect, eliminating the security gaps inherent in network-based solutions that only function within specific perimeters.
For more information including how to enable Web Threat Protection, see Malicious Domain Protection .

title: Email Security - Digest
content:
The daily or weekly email digest contains a list of all emails that were quarantined during the period in question.
To release an email flagged as Spam, users can click on the Release button in the email. You can also preview the email before releasing it.
To release an email flagged as Malicious, users can click the Request to Release button in the email, and an admin will review the request, and either approve or deny it as appropriate.
Admins have the power to:
Approve or deny release requests
Preview message content
Customize the digest email message.
The system also maintains comprehensive audit logs for all release actions, which can be viewed by admins by navigating to Email Security > Requests .
Note: When a user asks to release an email from quarantine, the entry appears under the Requests tab on the Email Security page. Administrators should consistently monitor this tab to ensure timely approvals.

title: Managing Endpoints Across Sites
content:
Admins of MSPs or customers managing multiple sites can manage endpoints from all their sites from the Account level, using the Endpoint Management page, without going into each individual site.
Note: Users only have access to sites for which they have permissions.
To access the Endpoint Management page at the account level, navigate to Endpoints > Management .
The account-level Endpoints Management page is almost identical to the site-level Endpoints Management page, with the addition of the Site column, which lets you know which site each endpoint belongs to. You can click on the arrow at the left of each endpoint row, to open the drilldown, which shows the general, status, and security information for the specific endpoint.
For a full description of the Endpoints Management page, including how to search and filter, available actions, and an explanation of the different endpoint health and connectivity states, see Endpoint Management .

title: Non-Deployed Endpoint Management
content:
Cynet 360 allows you to monitor and manage "Non-Deployed" endpoints. These are devices discovered on your network that have logged in during the past 30 days, and do not yet have the Cynet agent installed. These assets can be identified by scanning your Entra ID domain or your local Active Directory (AD) (On-Prem) domain.
Endpoints on which the Cynet Agent is not deployed create a large surface area for potential attacks and represents a significant risk to the entire enterprise. The ability to monitor these endpoints enables you to reduce this risk by bringing them under security management.

Prerequisites for Discovery via Active Directory

Cynet Agent Installation: At least one AD Domain Controller must have the Cynet agent installed.
Agent Version: For discovery on the local AD (On-Prem), t he Cynet agent installed on your l ocal AD Domain Controller must be v4.30 or higher .

Prerequisites for Discovery via Entra ID

Integration with Cynet's Microsoft 365 integration using the SSPM toggle.
This prerequisite is required due to the permissions needed to view non-deployed endpoints, which are used in the Microsoft 365 integration.
Note: When populating the Non-Deployed Endpoints tab from Active Directory, only the first 15 characters of the endpoint name are matched.

Viewing Non-Deployed Endpoints

To see a list of Non-Deployed endpoints, navigate to Endpoints > Management > Non-Deployed Endpoints .
The table provides essential details for each endpoint, including:
Endpoint Name: The endpoint's name on the network or AD.
OS Information: The operating system detected on the endpoint including which OS version.
Discovery Type: How the device was found (Local AD (On‑Prem), Cloud (Entra ID) or Both).
Last Seen: The most recent time the device was detected on the network.
Filtering and searching the non-deployed endpoints list:
Use the search bar to find a specific endpoint or IP address.
Use the filter bar to narrow the list by: OS: The operating installed on the endpoint. OS Version: The version of the operating system installed on the endpoint. Cynet Agent Status: The current status of the Cynet agent on the endpoint. Discovery Type: How the device was found, if more than one option is possible.
OS: The operating installed on the endpoint.
OS Version: The version of the operating system installed on the endpoint.
Cynet Agent Status: The current status of the Cynet agent on the endpoint.
Discovery Type: How the device was found, if more than one option is possible.
Use the date range to narrow the list by when the device was last detected on the network.
The non-deployed endpoints list is refreshed every 4-6 hours.

Exporting a List of Non-Deployed Endpoints

After narrowing the list as needed, click the Export icon to download the current view as a CSV file.

title: Remediating Alerts
content:
When remediating an alert you can select from the following actions:
Cynet 360 built-in remediation actions, listed below. You cannot edit these remediation actions, nor add them to playbooks. Icons indicate the operating system in which every action is supported.
Custom Remediation Actions that you created. For more information, see Creating Custom Remediation Actions .
Playbooks: A group of remediation actions. For more information see Remediation Playbooks .
For information on when to find remediation actions in the Cynet 360 console, and where you can monitor performed actions, see Taking Action .
Cynet 360 includes remediation actions for every alert type: file, user, network, and host (as detailed below). Note however, that it does not include built-in remediations for every possible alert.
New built-in remediations are added relatively often, and if no built-in remediation exists for your specific need, you can create a Custom Remediation , as mentioned above.

File Remediation Actions

The following remediation actions can be performed on files observed on endpoints in the environment:

User Remediation Actions

The following remediation actions can be implemented on users observed in the environment:

Network/Host Remediation Actions

The following remediation actions can be implemented to block network connections observed in the environment or on hosts scanned in the environment:
For Windows: .bat files
For Mac and Linux : .sh files
Windows 10 and above desktop operating systems.
Windows Server 2016 and above server operating systems.
Notes:
If the service on which an Isolate custom remediation is dependent is disabled when the remediation is applied, the custom remediation will enable the service.
The Isolate/Un-isolate remediation does not adhere to the Remediation Settings. This means that the remediation retry attempts and remediation retry interval do not apply to this remediation.
Some remediation actions appear twice in the Action menu. When creating a playbook, you can only use actions that are marked For playbook or custom remediation actions.

XDR Remediation Actions

XDR remediation actions are taken on entities that are not endpoints, that triggered XDR alerts. For example, Microsoft 365, firewalls, VPNs, etc.
XDR remediation actions can be performed manually or used as part of Remediation Playbooks.
Known Limitations:
For MSPs managing customers as groups, XDR remediation actions for Microsoft 365 are currently unavailable.
Currently XDR remediation actions are unavailable as auto-remediations.

title: Endpoint Consumption
content:
The Cynet console enables you to keep track of your organization's endpoint consumption and see when you are nearing the limit of your allocated endpoints.
When you are reaching or have gone over the limit of your allocated endpoints, a warning appears in the Active Endpoints tile. This tile can be found in the Endpoint Management page for both MSP and Direct customers, as well as on the Sites page for MSP customers.

Viewing Endpoint Consumption as an MSP

For MSPs , endpoint consumption is shown in the Active Endpoints counter in both the Endpoint Managements page and the Sites page (both at the Account level).
View from the Endpoints Management Page:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Endpoints > Management .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.
View from the Sites page:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Sites .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.

Viewing Endpoint Consumption as a Direct Customer

For direct customers , endpoint consumption is shown in the Active Endpoints counter in the Endpoint Managements page.
To view endpoints consumption:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Endpoints > Management .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.

sections.text:
The Cynet console enables you to keep track of your organization's endpoint consumption and see when you are nearing the limit of your allocated endpoints.
When you are reaching or have gone over the limit of your allocated endpoints, a warning appears in the Active Endpoints tile. This tile can be found in the Endpoint Management page for both MSP and Direct customers, as well as on the Sites page for MSP customers.

sections.heading: Viewing Endpoint Consumption as an MSP
sections.text:
For MSPs , endpoint consumption is shown in the Active Endpoints counter in both the Endpoint Managements page and the Sites page (both at the Account level).
View from the Endpoints Management Page:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Endpoints > Management .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.
View from the Sites page:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Sites .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.

sections.heading: Viewing Endpoint Consumption as a Direct Customer
sections.text:
For direct customers , endpoint consumption is shown in the Active Endpoints counter in the Endpoint Managements page.
To view endpoints consumption:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Endpoints > Management .
If the Active Endpoints tile appears in a warning state (red or orange), hover over the overconsumption icon to view your endpoints usage status.

title: Anti-Flood Logic for Alert Email Notifications
content:
Cynet applies anti-flooding logic to email notifications for alerts. Once the anti-flooding limit is reached on a particular site for a specific alert type, no more emails are sent for that alert type for that site for another 24 hours. When this happens, all further emails of the same type are consolidated into a single email, and sent at the end of the 24 hours.
The anti-flooding limit is set to 30 emails within one minute per site.
The anti-flooding logic is therefore applied only if more than 30 emails from the same alert type were sent in the space of one minute, from the same site . When this occurs, the following events take place:
An email is sent saying that more than 30 alerts were triggered in the last minute, and we will stop sending emails for that alert type for 24 hours.
After the 24 hours is up, an aggregated email is sent listing all the alerts of the specified type that were triggered during the previous 24 hours.
Rate limiting is reassessed after 24 hours.
Since the anti-flooding logic only applies to email notificatins, the full list of alerts remains available in the Cynet console and can be viewed there at any time.

title: Taking Action
content:
There are multiple actions you can perform on an alert to remediate the issue, analyze it, or manage future events. The same actions are available for objects in the Forensic page, although the remediation action behavior is different.
The tabs in the Actions side-panel vary according to the type of alert you select.
All actions performed by Cynet 360 are recorded in the Actions page. For more information, see Monitoring Actions .
Cynet 360 includes the following actions:
Open: The alert appears in the alert page and requires your attention.
Close: Close an alert when you finish handling the incident. By default, closed alerts do not appear in the Alert page. If the exact same alert is triggered again, the alert reopens and appears in the Alert page. Click here for more information on alert grouping.
Ignore: Ignore an alert if the incident that triggered it is not relevant at the moment, and does not require your attention. If the incident is never relevant to your organization, consider allowlisting the alert. If the exact same alert is triggered again, the alert does not reopen, but continues to appear in status Ignore.
Penetration Testing
False Positive
Requires Details
In Progress

title: Analyzing a File
content:
The Analysis tab in the Action side-panel enables you to perform analysis on files scanned by Cynet 360. The following analysis options are available:
This remediation action is not relevant for an alert triggered by a suspicious process or heuristic alerts. Send to SOC is only relevant for an alert that contains a suspicious file.
To view the status of an action you performed, navigate to the Actions page. See Monitoring Actions .

Configuring Dynamic Analysis Settings

The SSE sandbox enables dynamic file analysis and can be configured for either a local sandbox (available for On-Premises customers) or a cloud-based sandbox (available for SaaS customers).
Files are sent periodically, every 10 seconds, and the information sent with the file includes the file path, file hash (generally sha256), and the host it was sent from.
Navigate to Settings > Analysis .
Mark the checkbox for Enable SSE Sandbox Analysis .
Select the sandbox to enable: To enable the cloud-based sandbox, mark the checkbox for Enable Cloud Sandbox . To enable a local sandbox, mark the checkbox for Enable Local Sandbox and enter the Local Sandbox IP address .
To enable the cloud-based sandbox, mark the checkbox for Enable Cloud Sandbox .
To enable a local sandbox, mark the checkbox for Enable Local Sandbox and enter the Local Sandbox IP address .
Click Save .

title: Remediation Playbooks
content:
Cynet 360 includes advanced playbooks for incident investigation and response, which will make these playbooks obsolete.
Remediation Playbooks enable you to store a collection of Custom Remediation Actions and run them sequentially or in parallel. Playbooks can be used manually or as part of auto-remediation.
Some remediation actions appear twice in the Action menu. To playbooks, you can add only actions that are marked For playbook or custom remediation actions .
To create a Remediation Playbook:
Navigate to Settings > Remediation > Playbooks tab.
In the top-right corner, click Create .
Enter a name for the playbook.
Select whether to run the playbook remediation actions in sequential manner or in parallel.
Select the remediation actions to include in the playbook. A playbook is executed on a single location, which is determined by the first remediation action in the playbook.
A playbook is executed on a single location, which is determined by the first remediation action in the playbook.
Click Save .

title: Execution Servers
content:
Use the Execution Servers tab (found under Settings > Remediation ) to specify one or multiple servers on which to run a custom remediation action .
The available servers listed in the Execution Servers tab are Windows servers that recently sent a heartbeat. When selecting servers, make sure these are highly available and secure, and have access to the tools used by the remediation script.

title: How to Integrate Cynet 360 with Datto
content:
By integrating with Datto, you can use the RMM platform to verify that the Cynet EPS is installed and running, which version is running, as well as automatically create Datto tickets from Cynet alerts. Monitoring scripts for the Cynet EPS are available for all operating systems.
This integration guide includes two sections:
Adding Cynet to Datto’s monitoring dashboard
Configuring email processing from Cynet to Datto

Monitoring Cynet Using Datto RMM

To add Cynet to Datto’s monitoring Dashboard:
Log in to your Datto account.
In the CoStore, search for Cynet. Once found, click the Cynet search result. A dialog box opens.
A dialog box opens.
Click Add to my Component Library . Cynet Monitor is added to the Components tab.
Cynet Monitor is added to the Components tab.
Navigate to New UI > Policies > Monitoring .
Click Create Policy .
In the Create Policy page, enter the required parameters.
Click Add Monitor .
For Type , select Component .
For Component Monitor , select the Cynet Monitor.
Enter the Cynet EPS version to validate against.
Click Add Monitor .
Click Save and Deploy or Save and Deploy Later . Cynet is added to Datto RMM.
Cynet is added to Datto RMM.

Configuring Incoming Email Processing

Using Incoming Email Processing you can automatically create a ticket in your Datto Service Desk from a Cynet Alert. To configure this feature, you must also configure Cynet to send alerts to a specific email address.
To configure Incoming Email Processing:
Navigate to Admin > Activations .
Select the Incoming Email Processing check box.
Navigate to the Automation tab > Email Notifications and Surveys > Incoming Email Processing.
Click . For automatic ticket creation, Datto creates a mailbox. Alerts are sent to this mailbox, then automatically converted to a ticket.
For automatic ticket creation, Datto creates a mailbox. Alerts are sent to this mailbox, then automatically converted to a ticket.
Enter a name for the mailbox.
Enter an email for the mailbox. The full email address appears below. You can configure additional settings regarding attachments and email headers.
You can configure additional settings regarding attachments and email headers.
Navigate to the Ticket tab to configure settings regarding the ticket that is created.
For Ticket Account , click , then select the account to which the new ticket will be associated. Note for Global users: Every mailbox is associate with a single account. If you manage multiple accounts/sites, you must create a different mailbox for each Cynet site.
Note for Global users: Every mailbox is associate with a single account. If you manage multiple accounts/sites, you must create a different mailbox for each Cynet site.
Click Save & Close .
To configure Cynet 360 to send alerts to by email:
In the Cynet UI, Navigate to Settings > Alerts > Local Email Alert Recipients.
For Local Email Alert Recipient , copy the email address that appears in the Datto Incoming Email Processing page.
Click Save. Alerts are sent to the Datto mailbox for ticket creation.
Alerts are sent to the Datto mailbox for ticket creation.

title: Monitoring Actions
content:
Use the Actions page to monitor any action you performed on an object. From this page you can also perform actions and create auto-remediation rules.
The Action menu is also available in the top-right corner of the Actions page, and enables you to take action on every object displayed. For information on the actions you can perform in Cynet 360, see Taking Action .
The following information is displayed in each tab:
You can sort the display by any displayed column by clicking in the column heading, and you can also export the display to Excel.

sections.text:
Use the Actions page to monitor any action you performed on an object. From this page you can also perform actions and create auto-remediation rules.
The Action menu is also available in the top-right corner of the Actions page, and enables you to take action on every object displayed. For information on the actions you can perform in Cynet 360, see Taking Action .
The following information is displayed in each tab:
You can sort the display by any displayed column by clicking in the column heading, and you can also export the display to Excel.

title: NextGen Antivirus
content:
Cynet's NextGen antivirus reduces the attack surfaces in your environment by protecting you from known malware and zero-day attacks. Cynet's NextGen antivirus is composed of these engines:
Core antivirus: Identifies and prevents execution of malware with known signatures. Malware signatures are updated, at least, twice a day.
CyAI: Engine based on machine learning. Operational in all Windows-based endpoints in which the core antivirus is turned on.
Threat intelligence: The agent sends file attributes to the server for immediate detection and response. The Cynet server scans the file metadata for indications of known malware in the Cynet threat intelligence database and third party intelligence feeds.
Cynet's NextGen antivirus monitors file creation. Cynet triggers an alert when a malicious file is saved on the endpoint, even if not executed (the alert is Infected File - File Dumped on Disk).
It is not recommended to use an antivirus in addition to Cynet's NextGen antivirus, as this may cause performance issues.
If you do decide to enable an antivirus in addition to Cynet antivirus, you must prevent the tools from identifying each other as malware. To add the appropriate exclusions to Cynet 360, navigate to Settings > Allowlist & Exclusions > Exclusions . For a list of Cynet 360 paths and directories that must be excluded, click here .
If a legitimate file keeps triggering AV alerts, consider allowlisting the file.
Antivirus settings are applied per Group and are located under EPP Advanced Settings. If EPP is turned on, AV is automatically turned on, in the same mode as EPP (Detect or Prevent). If EPP is set to Prevent, you can separately set Antivirus to Detect (you cannot set AV to Prevent if EPP is set to Detect).
Antivirus modes:
Detect: When a malicious file is executed, Cynet 360 triggers an alert.
Prevent: When a malicious file is executed, Cynet 360 triggers an alert and renames the file to block its execution. You can undo the Rename action by selecting Undo File Remediation from the Actions menu * .
* If you try undoing the remediation action and the AV alert is immediately triggered again or the action fails, you need to allowlist the alert before undoing the file remediation. After allowlisting the file, wait for the allowlisting to take affect, then undo the file remediation again.
Note: Undo File Remediation is only supported for Windows endpoints.
In Linux endpoints, Cynet NextGen AV requires additional components. Click here for details.

title: Protection Settings
content:
The following table lists and explains the available security mechanisms in Cynet 360, and their mode of operation in Best Practice configuration.
If Prevention is enabled, Cynet 360 performs prevention actions on the endpoint when a threat is detected. These actions block the malicious activity from occurring and affecting other endpoints in the organization.
Cynet 360 perform remediation actions only on alerts of critical or high severity.

Cynet 360 perform remediation actions only on alerts of critical or high severity.

title: Security Vault
content:
The Security Vault is a secure infrastructure for storing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and tokens. The sensitive information is encrypted at rest and can be masked in the UI.
The sensitive information stored in the Security Vault can be used in Custom Remediation Actions to integrate with, or access, third-party systems.
To access the Security Vault navigate to Settings > Security > Security Vault .
To add a vault object:
Click Add .
Enter an object name.
Enter the object key.
Use the toggle to activate the object and/or allow the object to be used in a Custom Remediation Action.
Click Save .
To add credentials to a vault object:
Click the object name. The vault object opens.
Click Add .
Enter the name, key, and value of the credentials you want to store.
To encrypt the value in the database, click (optional).
To mask the value in the UI, click (optional).
Click Save . The credentials are added to the vault object.

title: Threat Hunting
content:
Threat Hunting enables you to scan endpoints on demand, according to known Indicators of Compromise (IoCs). As opposed to continuous file scanning that Cynet 360 performs on the host, that targets running processes, Threat Hunting can be used to discover files that have been saved on the host, even if not opened by the user.
When you create a search, it immediately starts running on all Groups for which Threat Hunting is enabled. If Cynet 360 finds a file that matches the defined Indicator of Compromise on the scanned hosts, it triggers an alert. The name of the triggered alert is Threat Hunting, of type Host.
To enable Threat Hunting:
Navigate to Settings > Groups .
Select the group for which you want to enable Threat Hunting and click Edit .
Click the Advanced Settings for Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) .
Turn the Threat Hunting toggle to On .
Click Ok .
Click Save .
To create a Threat Hunting search:
Navigate to Settings > Threat Hunting .
From the dropdown list, select an indication type: File SHA256, MD5, name or path.
Enter an indicator value.
Enter a description for the alert that is triggered when Cynet 360 finds an IoC on a host, then click + . Cynet 360 generates an separate alert for every IoC found.
Cynet 360 generates an separate alert for every IoC found.
To search for IoCs only on specific file extensions, enter the extension, then click + .
To search for IoCs only on specific file extensions, enter the extension, then click + .
To search for IoCs only in specific directories, enter the directory name, then click + . You can repeat steps 4/5/6 to add more indicators/file extensions/directories.
To search for IoCs only in specific directories, enter the directory name, then click + .
You can repeat steps 4/5/6 to add more indicators/file extensions/directories.
Select the severity of the alerts that are triggered when Cynet 360 finds an IoCs on a host.
Select the severity of the alerts that are triggered when Cynet 360 finds an IoCs on a host.
Select the Suppress check box to stop a running scan (if exists) and start the new scan. To wait for the current scan to finish, leave the check box cleared.
Select the Suppress check box to stop a running scan (if exists) and start the new scan. To wait for the current scan to finish, leave the check box cleared.
To start the Threat Hunting scan on all hosts, click Save Changes .
To start the Threat Hunting scan on all hosts, click Save Changes .
To stop the current scan, click Stop Current . Any host that hasn't been scanned yet is not scanned. To restart the scan, click Restart Current . All hosts are scanned, even if previously scanned. To delete all IoCs, click Clear Search Criteria .
To stop the current scan, click Stop Current . Any host that hasn't been scanned yet is not scanned.
To restart the scan, click Restart Current . All hosts are scanned, even if previously scanned.
To delete all IoCs, click Clear Search Criteria .
Note : The default time interval for this feature is 30 minutes.

title: The Radar
content:
The Radar provides a high-level overview of the current security status of the environment. It displays the current number of open alerts, analyzed files and scanned hosts. The radar enables navigation to other areas of the console based on this information.
The information in the radar page is updated every 15 minutes. All times are displayed in the Cynet 360 console in UTC.
To display the number of open alerts of a specific severity, position the cursor on a colored section of the Open Alerts ring.

sections.text:
The Radar provides a high-level overview of the current security status of the environment. It displays the current number of open alerts, analyzed files and scanned hosts. The radar enables navigation to other areas of the console based on this information.
The information in the radar page is updated every 15 minutes. All times are displayed in the Cynet 360 console in UTC.
To display the number of open alerts of a specific severity, position the cursor on a colored section of the Open Alerts ring.

title: Viewing Alerts
content:
The Alerts page provides a customizable view of the alerts generated by the system. Alerts can be filtered and actions can be performed on them. Alerts continue to appear in the console for at least two years.
System alerts are only displayed in the default All view that displays all alert types.
First Seen/Last seen: The date and time that the alert was triggered. If First seen is different from Last Seen, it means that the exact same alert was triggered several times, but displayed once.
Description of the host that triggered the alert.
Cynet's recommendations for remediation.
Any tags that have been applied to the alert.
The processes that triggered the alert. The last process in the tree is the malicious process that triggered the alert. Click on each of the processes to view its occurrence details.
The MITRE ATT&CK tactic and technique related to the alert (available for specific alerts).
Comments added by users.
Some alerts also indicate the file path and hash.
There are many actions you can perform on a alert from the Alert page. For details, see Taking Action .

title: Monitoring Endpoint Deployment
content:
When the Cynet agent is deployed it performs an initial scan to investigate elements on the endpoint, such as processes, services, etc. The agent also performs this scan whenever an endpoint restarts.
The Scanner page provides information about the status of the Cynet scan, the connectivity status of the agent, and enables you to start/stop scans (in on-premise version only). The Scanner page displays all the endpoints that sent a heartbeat since the last time the page was refreshed (using the Restart Scans button), including inactive and uninstalled endpoints.

Endpoint Information

For each endpoint in your environment, the following information is provided:
The Scan Group to which the endpoint belongs
When the endpoint was last scanned
The agent Scan Mode on the endpoint
Connectivity status of the endpoint: Indicates the status of the TCP and HTTPS connectivity. The agent sends the Cynet server a heartbeat every five minutes. An endpoint connectivity status changes to Inactive if it has been more than 25 minutes since the last heartbeat. Inactive endpoints are not removed from the Scanner page.

Endpoint Actions

For each endpoint in your environment, the following actions are available:
Stop scan (available in on-premise version only)
Restart scan: Clears all displayed scan history and initiates the scanner service to rescan the endpoint . To rescan a specific endpoint, select it, then click the Actions icon.
Uninstall the agent from the endpoint / enable reinstallation of the agent on the endpoint. When uninstalling an agent, Cynet 360 attempts to connect to the endpoint and uninstall the agent. Whether the operation is successful or not, the agent is blocked from resuming activity. If, after uninstalling the agent, you want to reinstall it, you must first select E nable Reinstall Endpoints from the Action menu. Only an Operator can uninstall the agent from an endpoint.

Scanner Page Actions and Information

The following actions and information are available for the Scanner page data:
View numbers for the last day: The number of endpoints scanned in the last 24 hours and the number of endpoints that are associated to a Group but have never been scanned. This number may not match the number of endpoints displayed on the screen as it refers to endpoints scanned in the last 24 hours only .
Restart scans - stop all current scans and refresh the display with connected endpoints only.
Export the filtered or complete information on the screen to an Excel file
Export a report of scan errors to an Excel file
Export an excel report of endpoints that have not been scanned in the last 24 hours (Export Un-Scanned Endpoints)
Reload Scanner page data
Generate EPS: Generates an installer file for the Cynet agent, including the relevant arguments.

title: Agent Scan Mode
content:
You can select different methods in which the agent scans a Windows endpoint. In Mac and Linux endpoints, the only available Scan Mode is Light-Agent. Scan Mode is set per Scan Group .
Relevant to on-premise versions only.

Light Agent

The Cynet service, CynetLauncher, is set as a persistent service on the endpoint. Once the endpoint reboots, CynetLauncher restarts the Cynet EPS. This Scan Mode ensures continuous scanning.

Always On

The Cynet service, CynetLauncher, is copied onto the endpoint. After dispatching the Cynet EPS, CynetLauncher is deleted and will not exist if the endpoint reboots .

Interval

The Cynet EPS is dispatched to the endpoint at the specified scan interval. The Cynet EPS self-terminates on the endpoint at the end of the scan cycle. This Scan Mode is also referred to as Agentless.

title: Managing Users for On-Premise Customers
content:
Use the Settings Users tab to manage your local users. From this tab you can perform the following actions:
Manage user authentication to the Cynet console: On-premise customers can configure Cynet 360 to authenticate users via AD. For more information, see Integrating with Active Directory . Alternately, user authentication is performed locally.
Add and delete users.
Manage roles (see Role Based Access Control ).
Change user passwords.
Manage multi-factor authentication .
To create a local user:
Navigate to Settings > Users > Create Local User .
Enter a user name. You can't use a user name that already appears in the system (across all Cynet sites and customers).
Set a password for the user. Passwords must be between eight and twenty characters in length and must include: Lower case letters, upper case letters, numbers, and symbols. Users are required to update their passwords every 90 days.
Select a role for the user, then click Add . The user is added to the list of local users. Every user listing includes the actions you can perform on the user, according to you permissions: Remove Change email Reset password Manage MFA Change role
Remove
Change email
Reset password
Manage MFA
Change role

title: Integrating Cynet 360 with Active Directory
content:
Integrate with Active Directory (AD) to authenticate users who log in to the Cynet 360 console, and import user attributes.
Integration with Active Directory is available for on-premise customers only
Manage domain credentials for Cynet to use within each domain to validate AD user authentication to the console.
To add AD credentials:
Navigate to Settings > Integrations .
In the Active Directory section, click Create .
In the dialog box, enter an account name, credentials and the domain. The account used to validate the credentials does NOT need to be a domain administrator. It only needs to be a domain user.
The account used to validate the credentials does NOT need to be a domain administrator. It only needs to be a domain user.
Click Validate Credentials .
If the credentials were successfully validated, click Save . The new credentials appear in the Manage Active Directory Integrations section. You can edit or delete these credentials at any time.
The new credentials appear in the Manage Active Directory Integrations section. You can edit or delete these credentials at any time.
Once you have configured the AD domain, you can assign roles to AD groups in the Users tab. For more information on Cynet users and roles, see Managing Users .
The Active Directory Authentication section maps users or groups in Active Directory domains to roles in the Cynet console.
Before configuring AD authentication settings, you must enable AD authentication at the top of the Users tab.
To add an AD group or user for Cynet console authentication:
Navigate to S ettings > Users .
Under Authentication Settings select an authentication method: Local only, AD only, or local and AD.
Scroll down to Active Directory Authentication Settings .
Enter the name of the AD group. If you have a large number of AD groups, it is recommended to import groups using a name pattern. For example, entering adm* will retrieve all groups starting with 'adm'. If you do not enter a group name, all the groups in the domain are retrieved.
From the dropdown list, select a domain. This list is populates by domains configured in the Integrations tab.
Click Fetch Groups . The groups included in the specified domain are retrieved.
The groups included in the specified domain are retrieved.
Select the AD Group to map.
Select the AD Group to map.
Select the role to assign to the group, then click Add . All Users in this group can be authenticated to the console using the Active Directory credentials. Added Groups appear in Enabled Identities .
All Users in this group can be authenticated to the console using the Active Directory credentials. Added Groups appear in Enabled Identities .
To add an AD user, select the AD domain, enter the user name, select the role, then click ADD .

title: Importing User Data
content:
You can configure Cynet to import user attributes from a Comma Separated Values (CSV) file or from Active Directory.
User attributes that can be imported are:
User name
Mobile number
Office phone number
Role
Department
Once imported, these attributes appear under User Details in the Forensic page.

Importing from CSV File

Navigate to Settings > Configuration > Import User Data from CSV File . The values in the left column are the attributes Cynet can receive. In the right column you must enter the corresponding CSV index. You must include a user name.
The values in the left column are the attributes Cynet can receive. In the right column you must enter the corresponding CSV index. You must include a user name.
For User Name, enter the corresponding CSV index.
If you want to add attributes, Click + and add an attribute to the left column. Enter the corresponding CSV index.
Repeat until all the required fields are listed.
Click Save .
Click Choose File . Go to the location of the CSV file on your computer.
Select the CSV file and click Upload CSV File . The file is parsed according to the field mapping configured in the section above.
The file is parsed according to the field mapping configured in the section above.
Note: When entering phone numbers in the CSV file, you must use the format + [ country code ][ area code ][ phone number ] without hyphens, as shown in the screenshot above. For example, +442012345678 or +15557654321.

Importing from Active Directory

Importing user data from AD is available for on-premise customers only
To import user attributes from AD:
Navigate to Settings > Configuration > Import User Data Settings . The values in the left column are the attributes Cynet can receive. In the right column you must enter the corresponding attribute ,as they appears in AD. You must include a user name.
The values in the left column are the attributes Cynet can receive. In the right column you must enter the corresponding attribute ,as they appears in AD. You must include a user name.
For User Name, select the corresponding AD attribute: Name or CN (common name). Active Directory attribute names are case sensitive.
Active Directory attribute names are case sensitive.
If you want to add attributes, Click + and add an attribute to the left column. Enter the corresponding AD attribute.
Repeat until all the required fields are listed.
Click Save .

title: Role Based Access Control
content:
Using Role Based Access Control you can create roles and assign specific permissions to every role. Roles can then be assigned to local and AD users and groups.
From the list of local users you can change the role assigned to a user. Changing user roles is only available if your assigned role has the appropriate permissions.
Dashboard : Has permission to view the Dashboard page only.
Operator : Has all permissions to one or several sites.
Operator manage sites : Like an operator, has all permissions to one or several site, and can create new sites. Viewing other users in the Users tab: A user with the role Operator Manage Sites can see other users with this role only of they manage the same sites.
Global manager : Includes administrative rights to the Global Manager server, and has full administrative access to all the sites managed by the Global Manager server.
Navigate to Settings > Users > Create User Role .
Enter a role name.
Select the permissions for the new role, then click Add . A global operator must also select a site. See Global Roles . The new role is added to the list of roles above. These roles are available when crating a new user or editing a user's role. In the Manage Roles section you can remove a role or edit its permissions.
The new role is added to the list of roles above. These roles are available when crating a new user or editing a user's role. In the Manage Roles section you can remove a role or edit its permissions.
You cannot remove a role if it is assigned to a user.

Navigate to Settings > Users > Create User Role .
Enter a role name.
Select the permissions for the new role, then click Add . A global operator must also select a site. See Global Roles . The new role is added to the list of roles above. These roles are available when crating a new user or editing a user's role. In the Manage Roles section you can remove a role or edit its permissions.
The new role is added to the list of roles above. These roles are available when crating a new user or editing a user's role. In the Manage Roles section you can remove a role or edit its permissions.
You cannot remove a role if it is assigned to a user.

title: Global Users and Roles
content:
Multi-tenant architecture includes the following built-in roles:
Operator : Has all permissions to one or several sites.
Operator manage sites : Like an operator, has all permissions to one or several site, and can create new sites. Viewing other users in the Users tab: A user with the role Operator Manage Sites can see other users with this role only if they manage the same sites.
Global manager : Includes administrative rights to the Global Manager server, and has full administrative access to all the sites managed by the Global Manager server.
Users and roles in a multi-tenant architecture include a additional parameter: Site. When creating a new role the operator selects one or several sites which the role can access. Assigning a role to a single site means that a user has a regular standalone experience using Cynet, without any global dashboards. Having access to multiple sites enables a global dashboards view and site navigation.
When creating a new user, the list of sites that you can assign to the user is composed of the sites defined in the role you selected. You can limit the number of sites to which the user has access with regards to the role.
Assigning a custom role to a site includes the following limitations:
You can assign a role to a new user only if you manage the sites to which the role is assigned.
You can delete a custom role only if you manage all the sites to which the role is assigned.
For more information on managing users and sites, see Managing Users and Managing Sites .

title: Deception
content:
Cynet 360 deploys deception entities (honeypots) on your network to lure attackers. When these entities are used, Cynet 360 detects them and triggers an alert on the illicit activity. See related alerts here .
Each Deception alert contains details about which type of deception has been triggered, the attacker IP address, the Victim IP address, hostname, and File name, if applicable (this information does not appear in the Forensic data, but only in the alert).
The types of deceptions are decoy users, files, and network. Every deception type must be activated for a specific Group under Settings > Groups , then configured according to the instructions below.

Local Deception Users

Cynet implants invalid credential information in the Windows Credential Manager. This data can be accessed by Windows or other applications that use the saved credentials. A decoy user is deployed locally and must be associated with a Group.
Cynet 360 triggers an alert when it detects an attempt to login with decoy user credentials.
To configure deception users locally:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Users .
Click Create and select the Group with which the decoy user should be associated.
Enter a Username and Host Name . Certain common usernames, such as Administrator, cannot be used as decoy usernames.
For User Type : The Credentials Manager differentiates between credentials based on the type of authentication. Select: Windows : Credentials used by Windows and its services. or Generic : General saved credentials that are defined by applications used on the computer.
Windows : Credentials used by Windows and its services.
or
Generic : General saved credentials that are defined by applications used on the computer.
Click Add .
To edit an existing decoy user, click Edit and change the required settings.
To remove an existing decoy user, click Remove .

Active Directory Deception Users

Active Directory decoy users are created in the Active Directory domain as a user object. Authentication events or network attacks that include those users trigger alerts indicating that those accounts are being abused.
To configure Active Directory deception users:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Groups and edit the group for which you want to configure the deception users. Note: Active Directory deception users must be enabled on all groups that contain Active Directory Domain Controllers.
Under Deception , click on Advanced Settings .
Toggle the Deception Active Directory Users toggle to On .
Click Ok .
Click Save .
As soon as Cynet recognizes that you have turned on Deception for Active Directory users, the system automatically creates deception users.
Note: For this mechanism to work as expected, you need to apply the recommended configuration in the Group Policy on the Active Directory Domain Controller, setting the following UI path to Success and Failure: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Advanced Audit Policy Configuration\Audit Policies\Account Logon\Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations

Deception Files

Cynet 360 deploys different type decoy files on selected Groups. When a decoy file is opened, it communicate with the Cynet server and generates an alert. You can also create new decoy files.
The implanted ODBC can also be found in Control Panel > System & Security > Administrative Tools > Data Sources (ODBC).
To configure decoy files:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Files.
Click the decoy file you want to deploy.
Click + .
Under Groups is the list of Scan Group in which Deception Files is enabled. From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
Select the directories in which the decoy file should be saved on the host.
In the Scan Group row, Click . The File Deception settings are saved.
The File Deception settings are saved.
To add a new decoy file:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Files , then click Add New Decoy File .
Upload a file to use as a decoy. Maximum file size is 500KB.
Click + .
Under Groups is the list of Scan Group in which Deception Files is enabled. From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
Select the directories in which the decoy file should be saved on the host.
Click Generate Test File . A test decoy file is downloaded locally. Open the file, then verify that an alert was triggered.
A test decoy file is downloaded locally. Open the file, then verify that an alert was triggered.
Click Save .
To monitor the deployment status of the decoy files, navigate to Actions > Files > Decoy Files Status .

Deception Network

Cynet 360 broadcasts decoy hosts to expose an attacker that has accessed your network and is monitoring its activity. An alert is triggered when the attacker tries resolve the fake hostname to an IP.
Deception network alerts are not supported in Windows 10 builds 1809 and above.
When configuring network deception you must select a Group. Cynet 360 randomly selects hosts from the selected Group and broadcasts them to other hosts in the subnet, with the decoy host name you entered.
For network deception, you must enable NetBIOS and SMB1.0 on the hosts in the selected Group.
To configure the deception network:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Network .
In the Max Decoy Hosts Per Endpoint field, enter the maximum number of hosts for each endpoint. The default is 10.
Select a Scan group in which to apply a deception network.
To manually add a single decoy, click Add .
Enter a name for the decoy host. You should give the decoy host a name that implies it is a significant host in the organization, such as Main DC.
Select an OS for the decoy host.
Enter a domain name for the decoy host. If other hosts in your network do not have a domain name, it is recommended to leave this field empty. Domain Name is limited to 14 characters.
Enter a username to be displayed with the decoy host. Adding a username increases the chance of an attacker attempting to access the host.
Click Save .
You can import multiple decoys by uploading a CSV file. To see the required format of the CSV file, click Import , then Download CSV Example.
You can add and import as many decoys as needed, however only the maximum number is distributed on a network. Cynet 360 randomly selects between the uploaded decoys.
To delete a decoy, select it from the list and click Delete Selected Decoys .
To delete all decoys, click Delete All Decoys .
Cynet 360 automatically selects new decoys after decoys are added, edited or deleted.

Cynet 360 deploys deception entities (honeypots) on your network to lure attackers. When these entities are used, Cynet 360 detects them and triggers an alert on the illicit activity. See related alerts here .
Each Deception alert contains details about which type of deception has been triggered, the attacker IP address, the Victim IP address, hostname, and File name, if applicable (this information does not appear in the Forensic data, but only in the alert).
The types of deceptions are decoy users, files, and network. Every deception type must be activated for a specific Group under Settings > Groups , then configured according to the instructions below.

sections.heading: Local Deception Users
sections.text:
Cynet implants invalid credential information in the Windows Credential Manager. This data can be accessed by Windows or other applications that use the saved credentials. A decoy user is deployed locally and must be associated with a Group.
Cynet 360 triggers an alert when it detects an attempt to login with decoy user credentials.
To configure deception users locally:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Users .
Click Create and select the Group with which the decoy user should be associated.
Enter a Username and Host Name . Certain common usernames, such as Administrator, cannot be used as decoy usernames.
For User Type : The Credentials Manager differentiates between credentials based on the type of authentication. Select: Windows : Credentials used by Windows and its services. or Generic : General saved credentials that are defined by applications used on the computer.
Windows : Credentials used by Windows and its services.
or
Generic : General saved credentials that are defined by applications used on the computer.
Click Add .
To edit an existing decoy user, click Edit and change the required settings.
To remove an existing decoy user, click Remove .

sections.heading: Active Directory Deception Users
sections.text:
Active Directory decoy users are created in the Active Directory domain as a user object. Authentication events or network attacks that include those users trigger alerts indicating that those accounts are being abused.
To configure Active Directory deception users:
From the Cynet toolbar, navigate to Settings > Groups and edit the group for which you want to configure the deception users. Note: Active Directory deception users must be enabled on all groups that contain Active Directory Domain Controllers.
Under Deception , click on Advanced Settings .
Toggle the Deception Active Directory Users toggle to On .
Click Ok .
Click Save .
As soon as Cynet recognizes that you have turned on Deception for Active Directory users, the system automatically creates deception users.
Note: For this mechanism to work as expected, you need to apply the recommended configuration in the Group Policy on the Active Directory Domain Controller, setting the following UI path to Success and Failure: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Advanced Audit Policy Configuration\Audit Policies\Account Logon\Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations

sections.heading: Deception Files
sections.text:
Cynet 360 deploys different type decoy files on selected Groups. When a decoy file is opened, it communicate with the Cynet server and generates an alert. You can also create new decoy files.
The implanted ODBC can also be found in Control Panel > System & Security > Administrative Tools > Data Sources (ODBC).
To configure decoy files:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Files.
Click the decoy file you want to deploy.
Click + .
Under Groups is the list of Scan Group in which Deception Files is enabled. From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
Select the directories in which the decoy file should be saved on the host.
In the Scan Group row, Click . The File Deception settings are saved.
The File Deception settings are saved.
To add a new decoy file:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Files , then click Add New Decoy File .
Upload a file to use as a decoy. Maximum file size is 500KB.
Click + .
Under Groups is the list of Scan Group in which Deception Files is enabled. From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
From the dropdown list, select the Scan Groups to which the file should be deployed.
Select the directories in which the decoy file should be saved on the host.
Click Generate Test File . A test decoy file is downloaded locally. Open the file, then verify that an alert was triggered.
A test decoy file is downloaded locally. Open the file, then verify that an alert was triggered.
Click Save .
To monitor the deployment status of the decoy files, navigate to Actions > Files > Decoy Files Status .

sections.heading: Deception Network
sections.text:
Cynet 360 broadcasts decoy hosts to expose an attacker that has accessed your network and is monitoring its activity. An alert is triggered when the attacker tries resolve the fake hostname to an IP.
Deception network alerts are not supported in Windows 10 builds 1809 and above.
When configuring network deception you must select a Group. Cynet 360 randomly selects hosts from the selected Group and broadcasts them to other hosts in the subnet, with the decoy host name you entered.
For network deception, you must enable NetBIOS and SMB1.0 on the hosts in the selected Group.
To configure the deception network:
Navigate to Settings > Deception > Network .
In the Max Decoy Hosts Per Endpoint field, enter the maximum number of hosts for each endpoint. The default is 10.
Select a Scan group in which to apply a deception network.
To manually add a single decoy, click Add .
Enter a name for the decoy host. You should give the decoy host a name that implies it is a significant host in the organization, such as Main DC.
Select an OS for the decoy host.
Enter a domain name for the decoy host. If other hosts in your network do not have a domain name, it is recommended to leave this field empty. Domain Name is limited to 14 characters.
Enter a username to be displayed with the decoy host. Adding a username increases the chance of an attacker attempting to access the host.
Click Save .
You can import multiple decoys by uploading a CSV file. To see the required format of the CSV file, click Import , then Download CSV Example.
You can add and import as many decoys as needed, however only the maximum number is distributed on a network. Cynet 360 randomly selects between the uploaded decoys.
To delete a decoy, select it from the list and click Delete Selected Decoys .
To delete all decoys, click Delete All Decoys .
Cynet 360 automatically selects new decoys after decoys are added, edited or deleted.

title: Multi-Factor Authentication
content:
Cynet 360 requires multi-factor authentication from users attempting to log into the console. Operators can manage MFA for local users.
MFA is not available for users managed via AD.
To configure MFA for a User :
Navigate to Settings > Users .
For the user to which you want to assign MFA, click Manage MFA . A dialog box opens that displays the MFA status of the user.
A dialog box opens that displays the MFA status of the user.
Select Assign MFA .
Enter the user’s phone number. The required format is + <country code><phone number>. For example, +000123456789.
Click Assign MFA .
On the user's next login attempt, the phone validation screen opens. Cynet 360 sends a code to the user by SMS. If the user's details do not include a phone number, the code is sent via email. Users who do not have an email address associated with their Cynet 360 account, must contact Cynet Support.
The verification code sent by SMS/email is valid for two hours. If required, the user can click Resend SMS to request a new code. The user enters the verification code.
The user must set up Microsoft or Google authenticator applications. Click here for instruction on setting up Microsoft or Google Authenticator application.
Using the authenticator application, the user scans the QR code or enters the manual key string.
Subsequent logins are verified with the code provided by the Google or Microsoft authenticator application.

title: Auditing Actions
content:
The Audit Page provides a full audit trail for all user actions performed in the system. You can save the audit records in the database and in external files.
This article describes how to access and use the Audit trail using the Cynet UI and external log files.

Auditing Using the Cynet UI

To navigate to the Audit page, click .
The Audit page displays all user actions performed in the system in the following columns:
Select the number of entities to display per page. The default is 25.
To export the data to a spreadsheet, click .

Auditing Using External Log Files

By default, all audit records are located in the following path: C:\Cynet360\logs\audit.
Audit files are cyclical. New files are created in the following format for every system restart or for every 50 MB: “CF_<CREATE_HOUR>T<CREATE_MINUTE>T<CREATE_SECOND>_<YYYYMMDD>.txt”
Files are text-based and include all audit records.
Each record appears in a new line: date hour Audit action:<action code>;UserName:<User>; category:<category>; details:<description+Json>”

title: Alert Reports
content:
The Alerts Report contains statistics on all alerts generated by the system within the specified date range. These reports can be filtered to show alerts for files, hosts, users, or networks.
The Alerts report includes three charts:
Alerts by Type: Alerts by type over the given date range. Each object type is highlighted in a different color.
Alerts by Status: The number of alerts based on their current status (open or closed). This chart indicates the incidence of high, medium, and low severity alerts.
Radar area: Specific areas associated with these alerts in the given date range. All file, user, host, and network traffic generating these alerts is displayed.
Under the charts the alerts are listed as they appear in the Alerts page.
To generate an alert report:
In the Cynet toolbar click Reports > View Reports .
Select Alert Report from the list.
In the Date Range fields, enter the start and end date.
Click Go . The report is displayed on the screen. You can export the report to PDF by clicking the PDF icon in the top-right corner.
The report is displayed on the screen. You can export the report to PDF by clicking the PDF icon in the top-right corner.
