title: Release Notes V4.31 (SaaS)
content:
Released February 2026. Version 4.31 (SaaS) includes the following features:

Endpoint Management Enhancements

Customers now have the ability to view all machines on their local Active Directory (On-Prem) domain which have logged in during the past 30 days, and do not yet have the Cynet agent installed on them (known as non-deployed endpoints). This enables the security admin to reduce the organization's attack surface by bringing these devices under security management.
Endpoints that do not have the Cynet agent installed are shown in the new Non-Deployed Endpoints tab in the Endpoint Management page. For each non-deployed endpoint, you can see the endpoint name, ID, OS, OS version, Discovery type (Local AD (On‑Prem), Cloud (Entra ID) or Both), Cynet Agent status, and when the endpoint was last seen by Entra ID or by the Local AD.
Note: For discovery on the local AD (On-Prem), at least one AD Domain controller must have t he Cynet agent installed. The installed version must be v4.30 or higher .
For more information, see Non-Deployed Endpoint Management .
You can now easily identify isolated endpoints in the Endpoints Management page. A new Isolated state is displayed for isolated endpoints, along with a dedicated filter to view all isolated hosts. When isolation is in effect, a dynamic counter appears at the top of the Endpoint Management page, and the Endpoint Security section of the dashboard includes a new cube highlighting isolated endpoints. Both indicators allow drill-down to a filtered Endpoint Management view for quick investigation and response.
For more information, see Endpoint Management .
Customers can now see when their site is overconsuming the number of purchased endpoints. This appears in different ways, depending on the customer type:
For Direct customers , overconsumption is shown on the Endpoint counter in the Endpoints Management page. Additionally, an email is sent once a week using Operational Notifications when the account is in an error state (with overconsumption of resources).
For MSPs , overconsumption is shown in the Endpoint counter in the Sites page, as well as in the Endpoint Managements page (both at the Account level). Additionally, an email is sent once a week using Operational Notifications when the account is in an error state (with overconsumption of resources).
For more information, see Endpoint Consumption .

Single Sign-On (SSO) with Google Workspace

You can now sign in to the Cynet Console using your Google Workspace account via Single Sign‑On (SSO), providing faster and more secure access. To enable this, navigate to Users & Roles > SSO and connect your Google Workspace account.
Depending on your organization’s security settings, you may need to approve Cynet as a trusted application within Google Workspace.
For more information, see Configuring SSO for Google .

Ignoring Endpoint Vulnerabilities

You can now ignore endpoint vulnerabilities by clicking on the Ignore button. Ignoring a vulnerability can be done at the organization level or at the endpoint level. If you ignore a vulnerability at the organization level, it is still possible to unignore it for specific endpoints. This allows you to avoid being notified about vulnerabilities that are not of interest to you and simplifies your alert management.
For more information, see Endpoint Vulnerabilities Management .

Temporarily Pause Cynet Agent [BETA]

Customers can now temporarily pause Cynet Agent protection on selected endpoints to perform updates and maintenance without disruption. When enabled at the group level, a Pause Protection action is available per endpoint, allowing admins to pause protections for a time period ranging from 30 minutes up to 6 hours.
While paused, the Cynet Agent continues reporting telemetry, without triggering alerts or preventing detected activities. The paused state persists through reboots, and all activity is clearly indicated in the console. Protections automatically resume when the timer expires. Protection can be manually resumed at any time through the Cynet Console. To prevent misuse, pausing is limited to online endpoints only, up to 5 hosts per action, with a maximum duration of 6 hours. Endpoints cannot be uninstalled or modified while in a paused state.
Note: When pausing the Cynet Agent, it can take a few minutes before the action is applied to the endpoint.
For more information, see Temporarily Pause Agent Protection .
Note: This feature is currently in Beta. To be added to the beta phase, contact your customer service manager.

PSA/RMM - ConnectWise Billing Integration

We now support automated usage-based billing for MSPs through the ConnectWise integration. MSPs can opt in to Cynet billing by mapping Cynet services to ConnectWise products and agreements. Once configured, Cynet automatically updates the relevant ConnectWise agreement additions on a monthly basis with the actual number of consumed endpoints, enabling accurate, hands-off billing of end customers based on Cynet usage data.

Updated XDR Naming to Include ITDR

To better reflect the full scope of detection and response capabilities available in the platform, XDR has been updated to explicitly include Identity Threat Detection and Response (ITDR).
XDR in the All-in-One dashboard is now labeled XDR / ITDR .
Alerts previously labeled as XDR are now labeled XDR / ITDR – Extended Detection & Response / Identity Threat Detection & Response .
Alert filtering options have been updated to reflect the new XDR / ITDR naming.
ITDR capabilities were already included as part of our unified XDR offering. This update aligns the user interface and alert terminology with the actual functional coverage, making it clearer that identity-based threats are monitored, detected, and handled as part of the same workflow.
Note: The name change applies only to alerts generated after the upgrade to version 4.31; alerts that existed prior to the upgrade are not affected.
For more information, see XDR / ITDR Alerts .

Operational Notification for SaaS and Cloud Integration Issues

Saas and Cloud integrations issues are now included in our Operational Notifications. Operational Notifications are email notifications to customers about Operational events requiring their attention which are not security incidents (for which alerts are already generated).
Customers will now get an email notification when a SaaS and Cloud integration disconnects, which may indicate a configuration or networking issue. Once the integration is reconnected, an additional email notification is sent.
For more information, see Supported Services for CSPM & SSPM .

Timestamp Alignment Enhancements

Alert timestamps in exports from the Alerts page are now aligned with the timestamps shown in the console. Additionally, timestamps on the Forensics page now align with the time zone definition.
For more information, see Time Zone for Alerts and Forensic .

MSP Multi-Group Mode Enhancement

MSPs that manage customers by group can now apply Group tags to File Monitoring and Windows Events logs, making it easier to filter, correlate, and analyze activity across customers within the Analytics view within the Forensics page.
For more information, see Managing Integrations by Group for MSP Users .

New APIs for Exposing Vulnerabilities and Misconfigurations

Six new API calls have been added, enabling you to get a list of Vulnerabilities and Misconfigurations via the API. The new API calls are as follows:
Post Misconfigurations : Creates a paginated list of all misconfigurations that are present on endpoints in the specified site.
Get Misconfiguration Filter Fields : Retrieves available filter fields which may be used to filter the misconfigurations list created in Post Misconfigurations .
Get Misconfiguration Endpoints : For the specified misconfiguration risk, this API call retrieves a paginated list of affected endpoints.
Post Vulnerabilities (CVEs) : Creates a paginated list of all vulnerabilities (CVEs) that are present on endpoints in the specified site.
Get Vulnerabilities (CVE) Filter Fields : Retrieves available filter fields which may be used to filter the vulnerabilities list created in Post Vulnerabilities (CVEs) .
Get Vulnerabilities (CVE) Endpoints : For the specified vulnerability, this API call retrieves a paginated list of affected endpoints.

Moving Endpoints Between Tenants

It is now possible for customers with multiple tenants to request endpoints be moved between tenants. To do this, please open a Support ticket .

Improved Uninstall Password Management

We have enhanced endpoint security by removing the ability to disable the agent uninstall password, ensuring that protection against unauthorized agent removal is always enforced. Administrators can still change the uninstall password as needed. Strong password requirements and audit logging continue to apply.
This change applies to endpoints deployed via MSI only.

Endpoint Watchdog Improvements (Gradual Rollout)

As part of ongoing efforts to improve agent continuity and stability, we are introducing a Watchdog mechanism to the Cynet Agent that provides enhanced endpoint monitoring and automated recovery capabilities. This includes a new service and process on each endpoint, and improves resilience and anti-tampering through proactive monitoring and recovery.
In the rare event that the Cynet Agent is restarted by the Watchdog mechanism, a Log-Only alert is generated for visibility.
Currently the Watchdog mechanism is available only for endpoints running Windows OS. It will be updated on all environments running Cynet v4.30 and above, to support backward compatibility.

We have deployed major updates to the CyAI NextGen AntiVirus (NGAV) malware detection model, enhancing prevention capabilities across a broader range of malicious executable scenarios. The updated model improves detection and prevention of zero-day threats using Cynet’s internally built AI models, including cases where traditional rule-based detections may fail. Prior to release, the model underwent a rigorous observation period and demonstrated stable performance with a false positive rate below strict internal thresholds, maintaining a false positive rate below 1%. These improvements strengthen CyAI NGAV’s preventive capabilities and support continued expansion of proactive protection coverage.

We have deployed major updates to the CyAI NextGen AntiVirus (NGAV) malware detection model, enhancing prevention capabilities across a broader range of malicious executable scenarios. The updated model improves detection and prevention of zero-day threats using Cynet’s internally built AI models, including cases where traditional rule-based detections may fail.
Prior to release, the model underwent a rigorous observation period and demonstrated stable performance with a false positive rate below strict internal thresholds, maintaining a false positive rate below 1%. These improvements strengthen CyAI NGAV’s preventive capabilities and support continued expansion of proactive protection coverage.
CyAI Prevention is applied automatically for customers who have the Antivirus in Prevent mode, with the Next-Generation (Machine Learning & AI) toggle enabled.

Added Support for Additional Linux OS

Support has now been added for Linux Fedora operating system up to version 42.
For more information, see Supported Operating Systems .

Visual Enhancements to Console Pages

The Configuration page in the Settings has been refreshed with an updated look and feel that better aligns with the rest of the UI. This visual update delivers a more consistent, modern experience across the console, improving clarity and usability while keeping all existing functionality unchanged.

* Note Regarding Gradual Rollout of New Features

Features marked as Gradual Rollout are being released to customers on a gradual basis during the weeks following the release.
We appreciate your patience while the rollout is in progress, and look forward to its completion.

sections.text: Released February 2026. Version 4.31 (SaaS) includes the following features:

sections.heading: Endpoint Management Enhancements
sections.text:
Customers now have the ability to view all machines on their local Active Directory (On-Prem) domain which have logged in during the past 30 days, and do not yet have the Cynet agent installed on them (known as non-deployed endpoints). This enables the security admin to reduce the organization's attack surface by bringing these devices under security management.
Endpoints that do not have the Cynet agent installed are shown in the new Non-Deployed Endpoints tab in the Endpoint Management page. For each non-deployed endpoint, you can see the endpoint name, ID, OS, OS version, Discovery type (Local AD (On‑Prem), Cloud (Entra ID) or Both), Cynet Agent status, and when the endpoint was last seen by Entra ID or by the Local AD.
Note: For discovery on the local AD (On-Prem), at least one AD Domain controller must have t he Cynet agent installed. The installed version must be v4.30 or higher .
For more information, see Non-Deployed Endpoint Management .
You can now easily identify isolated endpoints in the Endpoints Management page. A new Isolated state is displayed for isolated endpoints, along with a dedicated filter to view all isolated hosts. When isolation is in effect, a dynamic counter appears at the top of the Endpoint Management page, and the Endpoint Security section of the dashboard includes a new cube highlighting isolated endpoints. Both indicators allow drill-down to a filtered Endpoint Management view for quick investigation and response.
For more information, see Endpoint Management .

title: End of Life V4.4
content:
Cynet aims to deliver the best quality product to its growing customer community, and constantly improve its offering. To this end, Cynet periodically publishs an End of Life statement, which includes previous Cynet versions, legacy features and agent support for operating systems.
The End-of-Life process will be performed gradually, based on industry standards, and will be a part of the official release of the latest version.
This announcement will be in affect with the full release of Cynet Version 4.4.

Supported Cynet Version

In accordance with Cynet's Support Policy , with the release of Cynet 360 V4.4, older Cynet 360 versions reach the following stages:
Cynet 360 V4.2: End of software maintenance
Cynet 360 V4.0: End of support

Deprecated Features

As of Cynet version 4.4, the following features are no longer available:

Supported Operating Systems

With the release of Cynet version 4.3, the operating systems listed in the following table are no longer supported. Although these operating systems may continue to be functional and able to run the Cynet Agent, they are no longer a part of the validation and sanity tests performed during the version release.
The unsupported operating systems have reached their official End of Life, as published by the vendor. As a result, Cynet has limited capabilities in automation tests and frameworks to validate functionality and troubleshoot issues as part of the standard quality tests. If an issue arises when using an unsupported operating system, the customer is required to upgrade their operating systems to a supported version.
For more information, contact the Support team.

title: Support Policy
content:
To ensure continued innovation and standardize the Cynet 360 lifecycle, Cynet periodically discontinues support of Cynet 360 on-premise versions.
This policy applies to server and endpoint versions of Cynet 360.
With the release of a new Cynet version, older Cynet versions will reach the following stages:
End of software maintenance : Cynet Support continues to support this version and suggest workarounds. Customers continue to get Threat Intelligence updates, but no hotfixes are provided. Applies to version X when version X+2 is released.
End of support : The version has reached its end-of-life stage. Cynet Support assists with system functionality only but do not provide troubleshooting. Customers are advised to update their Cynet 360 version to the latest version. Applies to version Y when version Y+4 is released.
An example of Cynet’s Support Policy following the release of Cynet 360 version 4.3:
This information is also published in an end-of-life statement, which is published with every major release. The end-of-life statement also includes deprecated features and unsupported operating systems.

Operating System Support

The following policy applies to all operating systems supported by Cynet, as listed here .
Cynet begins supporting a new operating system major version within 90 days of its release date, and within 30 days of the release date of a minor version.
Cynet will continue to support operating systems and application versions discontinued by their vendor for up to 90 days from the operating system or application version end-of-life announcement by the vendor (provided that Cynet supported the operating system version or application version in question). Cynet will not provide hot fixes or updates for discontinued operating systems.
Cynet will make all reasonable attempts to provide support for Cynet products on operating system and application versions throughout the vendor supported life cycle. Non the less, Cynet reserves the right to stop supporting certain 3rd-party products on specific vendor operating system and application versions at any time.
Technical support for a discontinued operating system, application, or hardware provided by a 3rd-party vendor will end with the notification of obsolescence.

title: Release Notes V4.4
content:
New Features

Cynet version 4.4 includes new features and improvements.
Cynet 360 now includes a Security Information & Events Management (SIEM) intended to enhance forensic capabilities. The native Cynet SIEM collects logs and data from firewalls, endpoints and SaaS applications and enables correlating alerts and IR/forensic flows.
To participate in the Beta program, contact your Customer Success representative. After the Beta stage, SIEM will be available for additional cost to SaaS customers only.
SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM) has been added to Cynet 360. The Cynet SSPM periodically scans your SaaS application to detect misconfigurations and risks. You can then apply remediation actions to easily fix these misconfigurations.
To participate in the Beta program, contact your Customer Success representative. After the Beta stage, SSPM will be available for additional cost to SaaS customers only.
You can now expand Cynet remediation actions to other systems such as firewall and Active Directory. The new Security Orchestration, Automation & Response (SOAR) capabilities enable remediation actions to run on any Windows server in the network. This server is typically a secure and highly available server, used for 3rd party integrations and remediation actions on a remote server, as well as any other remediation action you require.
The new remediation actions are not yet integrated into Cynet 360. Contact your Customer Success representative to receive the scripts.
The following detections and protections have been added to the Cynet EPS agent for protected Linux devices:
Password recovery is now a self-service process for all users. A link to reset user password is sent securely to the user's verified email address.
Cynet integration with 3rd party SIEM providers is improved to send information over TCP and secure connection. Buffer size, previous limitation, and field mapping have also been improved.
Interval and Always On Scan Modes are no longer available for Mac hosts. Light Agent will be set automatically for Mac hosts during the next update, together with EPS re-dispatch.
When upgrading a host, a database migration will be performed, changing the Scan Mode to Light Agent.
File exclusion specifies file activities that are excluded from detection during a scan. When defining a file exclusion, Cynet now supports using wildcards in the file name or path.
The Cynet EPS can now operate on Mac hosts using Apple M1 ARM architecture.
The un-quarantine file action is now available from every Actions menu in Cynet 360.
From the current version of Cynet 360, the verbose logging of the MSI Installer, used by Cynet 360, is disabled by default. If you require this log for support purposes, you can produce it using the command line.
A new alert has been added to Cynet 360, for Windows hosts:
Malicious script command: Triggered by script-based attacks, this alert represents Cynet's Advanced Fileless Attack Protection. This mechanism utilizes additional features of the Windows Anti-Malware Scan Interface (AMSI) to prevent execution of Weaponized office docs, typically sent as part of a spear-phishing campaign.

Known Issues

This section lists the known issues in Cynet 360 v4.4
When connecting one external storage device to different ports of an endpoint, the events are listed with different Device IDs in the Device Control page.

title: Release Notes V4.3
content:
New Features

Cynet version 4.3 includes new features and improvements.
Cynet has expanded its capabilities against advance attacker activities in Linux hosts. These capabilities leverage the Sysdig package to perform deeper inspection into systems and process activities. For more information, click here .
Following the addition of Driver based protection, three alerts are added to Cynet 360 for Linux hosts:
Unauthorized File Operation Attempt
Process monitoring
Reverse shell
The following detections and protections have been added to the Cynet EPS agent for protected Linux devices:
Cynet is working on expanding MITRE-based EDR in future versions.
In case of malicious file download, the Incident view has been enhanced to provide additional information on the origin of the file. Leveraging Cynet’s file monitoring capabilities, Root Cause Analysis identifies the origin of the malicious file (e.g., the web browser). Cynet then perform remediation actions, such as deleting the file and isolating the infected host.
The new Security Vault is a secure infrastructure for storing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and tokens. The sensitive information is encrypted at rest and can be masked in the UI.
The sensitive information stored in the Security Vault can be used in custom remediation actions to integrate with, or access, third-party systems.
This feature expands the remediation options that Cynet provides its customers.
The UBA Management enables creating custom alerts for various user activities.
The UBA Management has been expanded so you can create and customize alerts for different triggers, such as host related activities. The SMS verification is no longer mandatory.
To expand your ability to monitor the system, the Forensic section now includes user login events. There are two available user logins views:
All user logins on all hosts, useful for advanced IR searches and threat hunting.
User logins on a specific host
A new set of out-of-the-box remediation actions have been added to the Cynet 360 UI. You can use these remediation actions as standalone actions or add them to a playbook.
These remediation actions include:
File: Quarantine, delete file, kill process, undo [recent] file remediation.
User: Enable/disable local user.
Host: Isolate/un-isolate host, show IPSec rules/status, allow/block RDP.
Network: Block/unblock traffic.
You can now manage exclusion policies across a global environment. Global exclusion profiles are enforced on all tenants/sites, and a local admin can also add and manage exclusions profiles.
Process exclusion specifies the activities that are excluded from detection during a scan. When defining a process exclusion, Cynet now supports using wildcards in the process name or path.
Display options for Global SIEM Settings are managed by a newly added feature flag (by default FALSE = hidden).
A new custom role can be associated to one or several sites, with the following limitations: Only managers with one or more sites associated with the role can assigned, manage or delete it (deleting is available only if the role is not assigned to any user).
It is now possible to copy the Alert Description from the Alert page to the clipboard and paste it in other apps.
The script command in the Alert Description has been decrypted in this version of Cynet, and the description also includes the text string.
Cynet now supports Elastic Stack v7.12 (FM & GM).

Known Issues

This section lists the known issues in Cynet 360 v4.3
It is recommended to use AMSI mechanism only if Kernel Driver is enabled. Latency is observed when using the AMSI mechanism in ETW mode.

See operating system requirements here .
The local Virtual Private Network (VPN) functionality requires iOS 11 and higher or Android 6 and higher.
A 64-bit version of the device is required for iOS devices. For 32-bit devices, an earlier version of Cynet 360 Mobile is required from the App Store or Google Play Store.
For full Knox device action support, Android version 7 and Knox version 2.7 or later are required.

title: Cynet 360 Mobile Overview
content:
Cynet 360 Mobile provides continuous detection and mitigation of malicious events affecting devices running the iOS or Android platforms. This is accomplished through real-time forensic data analysis by a detection engine that has been enhanced by machine learning. A central console is used to configure policies and manage threat events.
While a user can interact with Cynet 360 Mobile app to see current device security status, health, and previous events, there is no requirement for any interaction. Mitigation is performed by the application and through Mobile Device Management (MDM) integration via a supported MDM vendor. The type of mitigation performed is determined by the MDM integration and can range from a simple notification to the user to a wipe of all company data from the device.

App - Console Communication

Cynet 360 Mobile application communicates to the console every eight hours or when an event occurs to upload certain device information to determine if there are any policy updates that need to be incorporated. When the app is initially started, it requests the latest detection engine from the console. The latest threat policy is also downloaded along with the privacy settings. The information passed up to the console is configured with the privacy template on the console.

Detection Engine

Cynet 360 Mobile includes a revolutionary cyberattack detection engine that uses statistical models to dynamically detect advanced host- and network-based attacks as well as malicious applications on mobile devices. Unlike other threat detection systems, the detection engine monitors the whole device for malicious behavior without reliance on signatures. This approach allows Cynet to find and protect against both known and unknown threats in real-time, regardless of the threat source and with or without being connected to the Internet.
In addition, for Android and iOS with MDM integration, the malware engine scans for suspicious applications installed on the device and scans applications prior to being installed on the device to offer continuous protection. If malware is detected during scanning, action can be taken to remove a suspicious application even if the device has no network connectivity. If an internet connection is available, Cynet 360 Mobile performs additional malware scanning analysis against proprietary database. This database is built using data from Cynet's research team as well as other sources.
The behavioral detection engine sits on the device, within the Cynet 360 Mobile application, to detect threats without the need for internet connectivity or special privileges, preventing a compromised device from gaining access to the corporate network. This unique approach protects the end user’s privacy and prevents excessive battery drain that occurs when data is sent to the cloud to determine if a certain behavior is malicious. All decisions are made on the device.

title: Application Scanning
content:
iOS

When using Cynet 360 Mobile through an MDM integration, the applications on the device are scanned to see if any are malicious, alerting the user as needed. Cynet maintains a database of malicious iOS applications, which is built using data from Cynet's security research team and other sources. Unknown apps are evaluated in real-time and a decision is made as to whether they are legitimate or malicious.

Android Scan Modes

Cynet 360 Mobile for Android checks for suspicious applications in these modes:
Automatic Mode before Install: When the user downloads an application from the browser, email, or any other client (which saves the file on the SDCARD), Cynet 360 Mobile scans it. The app then alerts the user if the application is suspicious before the application is installed and provides a button to delete the suspicious application from the device.
Automatic Mode after Install: When a new application is installed, Cynet 360 Mobile scans it. If it is suspicious, an event is sent to Cynet 360 Mobile Console and an alert displays with an option to uninstall the application.

title: Threat Policy
content:
The threat policy contains rules for the actions to take when certain threats are identified. You can view the policy on the Policy page of Cynet 360 Mobile Console. The threat policy is updated in these ways:
The console sends a notification that a new threat policy is available. Then, Cynet 360 Mobile app pulls the updated policy information.
The app polls the console to see if there is an updated threat policy available to download. The time interval for this polling is currently configured by Cynet.

Threat Policy and Severity

Behavior on the device is different based on the severity level set on each threat. These threat severity levels are set by the administrator and the policy configuration is useful for different use cases. The severity options are:
Critical
Elevated
Low
Normal
Threat information is displayed in a variety of ways on the device:
Dashboard : This is the initial screen on the Cynet 360 Mobile app and shows the different topic categories of Device, Network, Web, and Apps (if configured). See the About the Dashboard.
Full Event Log (see below): This shows a log of all the threats encountered, other than threats with the severity of Normal. For threats classified as Low severity, the user does not receive notification other than an entry in the event log. For more information, see Full Event Log.
User Alerts : This is a pop-up message that is enabled by the administrator per threat. The dashboard shows elevated severity threats as risks detected and critical severity threats as threats detected, and the total number of both as active issues.
The dashboard also shows the number as the number of active issues.
The administrator enables or disables user alerts for each threat on the threat policy. Setting a user alert causes a message to display a warning for the user of the device.

title: Deployment and Installation
content:
The Cynet 360 Mobile application is deployed using one of the following modes:
With MDM integration: This installation is referred to as Enterprise. If a customer has an MDM solution, Cynet strongly suggests that the MDM be used to distribute the application. These steps are described in separate MDM integration guides specific to the supported MDM. For MDM deployment and installation information, follow the instructions to complete the MDM integration in the specific vendor-related MDM guide.
Without MDM integration: Without an MDM implementation, the user is instructed to download the MTD application from the Apple App Store or Google Play Store.

Initializing the App

When the app is pushed down to the device, it has to either be launched by the user or configured for protecting the device on the MDM. If it is launched by the user, an initial set of installation screens is displayed. The user launches this by tapping on the Cynet 360 Mobile icon that shows up on the device. An MDM configuration can push the app installation and include some tailoring of activation, depending on the MDM.

Activation

The user can activate the app in one of the following ways:
Pressing the activation link (URL).
Logging in with Microsoft.
Scanning a QR code with the camera of the device.
Activation with MDM integration is recommended, if possible, and involves several configuration options for activation. Activation through an email to the device user: The administrator can request an email notification for each iOS and Android device. This is done when the administrator adds the MDM in Cynet 360 Mobile console. The email contains an app activation link for each new device that is synchronized from the MDM. Automatic activation for iOS devices. Automatic activation for the Android’s personal profile when using Android Enterprise.
Activation through an email to the device user: The administrator can request an email notification for each iOS and Android device. This is done when the administrator adds the MDM in Cynet 360 Mobile console. The email contains an app activation link for each new device that is synchronized from the MDM.
Automatic activation for iOS devices.
Automatic activation for the Android’s personal profile when using Android Enterprise.
Automatic activation without MDM integration is possible in certain cases, such as when the app does not come from the App Store, Play Store, or is a branded app.

title: App Activation with MDM Integration
content:
An enterprise device is defined as a device managed by an enterprise’s MDM. If MDM integration is enabled and MTD has been pushed to an iOS or Android enterprise device from the MDM, then the device can sometimes be transparently activated without the user having to activate it manually. If these conditions exist, the device activation can be transparent to the end user:
MDM integration is enabled in Cynet 360 Mobile console.
The vendor-specific MDM supports zero-touch activation with Cynet 360 Mobile app.
The MDM is configured to auto-activate the device.
If these conditions are not all met for automatic activation, often the user presses an MDM activation link. In Cynet 360 Mobile console, the administrator creates activation URLs from the Manage page with the Integrations tab. Also, non-enterprise devices can be activated by the user with an activation link. In either type of activation, a device identifier is used to match up with the synchronized device identifier from the MDM. Once a match is found, then that device is associated with the app in the correct console environment.
Administrators need to ensure that variables in activation links are substituted with the actual device identifiers, either manually or through message templates where the variable can be substituted by an MDM or in the console.
Within most MDMs, the administrator modifies email templates that are sent to the end-user to help in device enrollment, and these are used to reference the activation URL. Refer to the specific MDM guides for more information on activation options with each MDM.

title: App Activation without MDM Integration
content:
If there is no MDM integration, the user is typically invited to activate the Cynet 360 Mobile app through an email either generated from the Cynet 360 Mobile console or sent by the administrator for a device group.
After the agreement is accepted, the activation request page displays. The administrator must provide the end-user with the information for activation or request that console send a welcome email.
Once activated, the device is then matched up with the correct environment for the activation link. For more information on activation links, see console documentation.
This figure shows the activation prompt that the user will see.
For devices where you want to use a different activation link on an already activated device, the user must confirm the new activation link. This occurs, for example, on devices that are on loan and rotated to different individuals.
Upon successful installation and activation, the dashboard displays. See Cynet 360 Mobile Overview for additional information.

title: Privacy Information
content:
A console administrator can enable a privacy summary display for all devices. When enabled, a privacy summary screen tells the user what information your organization can and cannot access from the user's device based on privacy policy settings.
The following examples show which information your organization can and cannot see on your device. The information displayed depends on policy settings for the tenant and which permissions are granted based on what is configured in the Privacy Policy in the console. Press the information icon at the bottom of the screen to view the complete End User License Agreement and Privacy Policy.

Press the information icon at the bottom of the screen to view the complete End User License Agreement and Privacy Policy.

title: App Permissions
content:
Runtime Permissions

When you initially log into Cynet 360 mobile application (the app), you are prompted to select the permissions you want to allow. The specific permissions requested depend on the policy configuration in Cynet 360 mobile console and the type of activation chosen.
To modify permissions at any time while using the app, tap the options menu icon and press Settings. Scroll down to the Permissions section and make your changes.
Users receive one or more runtime permission requests for these topics:
Camera access (popup displays if you select a QR code)
Notifications
Local Wi-Fi Network (iOS only)
Allow Location
VPN - Link Verification
Battery Optimization (Android only)
Device Storage (Android only)
Samsung Knox (Android only)
Messages/SMS (Android only)
Bluetooth (Android only)
For a number of dialog messages, the app cannot localize the prompt into another language. The dialog messages are owned by the operating system, and in some instances, these dialogs are always displayed in English. The following sections cover the different types of permissions that are requested. For more information on data privacy, refer to the Related Documentation section.
If during activation, you select to activate the app with a QR code, the app requests camera access. Press OK to scan your QR code for activation to begin.
Administrators can set notifications in the console to automatically alert users in response to certain threats or other activities. In iOS, users toggle the Notifications permission setting on, then press Allow when prompted to let the app send these notifications.
At least one of the threats in the Set User Alerts column in the console (shown in the following image) must be checked for the Notification permissions to display in the app. For more information, see Threat Policy for console documentation.
To protect the device against sophisticated Wi-Fi-based network attacks, the app must have permission to access the local network. This helps protect your device against network attacks and access to private data.
To access Wi-Fi details, the app needs location permissions. The app requires these Wi-Fi details to protect the device and data from sophisticated network attacks.
Location permission must be set to Always for app features to work.
When a security event occurs, the app gathers forensic information. Part of this information includes the location of the device when the event occurred. This is a standard warning for any application that has location service functionality built in. The app does not keep a history of your location information.
There are different flows for setting these permissions in iOS and Android. Each one is described next.
iOS
Follow these steps to set location permissions for iOS:
When you initially launch the app and see the Permissions screen, toggle the Allow Location permission to On. This permissions screen pops up. Be sure to select one of the highlighted options to allow the permissions. Please do not select Don’t Allow.
When you make a selection, this screen displays. Select Change to Always Allow.
The location permission is required to get the SSID and BSSID of the current Wi-Fi connection. The location is not stored in the app.
Android
Follow these steps to set location permissions for Android:
When you initially launch the app and see the Permissions screen, toggle the Allow Location permission to On. This permissions screen pops up. Be sure to select one of the Allow options. Please do not select Don’t Allow.
When you return to the permissions screen, the Allow Location permission will still appear toggled off. This is because it needs to be set to Always, which is described next.
Re-toggle the Allow Location permission on. This time, the following screen displays:
Click Open Settings to display this screen.
Make sure Location is set to Always, as shown in this image.
Now, when you go back to the app, the Allow Location permission is toggled on.
The location permission is required to get the SSID and BSSID of the current Wi-Fi connection. The location is not stored in the app.
With Android 10, location permission is required to detect an unsecured Wi-Fi threat.
An administrator has the option to configure the app to set up a VPN in response to threats. If this is requested, then during the installation of the app, a VPN profile is installed and can be seen in the Settings app of the iOS device. The app requests permissions from the user or, in other configurations, the permissions are automatically granted with the activation. If applicable, the app guides the user when installing the application or when policies change for the device.
This permission is requested when an administrator:
Enables a phishing policy for a local VPN.
Enables the enhanced phishing protection plus web content filtering option.
These VPN Link Verification permission screens do not display if the administrator did not configure a local VPN on the console with phishing or network sinkhole settings.
To allow adding the app to the Android battery optimization exemption list, press Allow to ensure devices are protected while the app is running in the background. This permission prompt is controlled by the administrator’s settings on the console. See the console documentation described in the Related Documentation section for more information.
To allow the app to access your device storage areas to detect and prevent malware from harming your device and data, press Allow to ensure devices can read these storage areas.
To allow the app to use Samsung Knox functionality to protect your device from mobile threats, press Activate to ensure the app has the ability to behave as a device admin app.
Granting this permission can require a user to go to Settings on the device to uninstall the app.
To allow the app to protect you against phishing links contained in your SMS messages, press Allow to enable access to those messages.
To allow the app to detect unknown tag trackers that may be tracking your location without your knowledge, press Allow to enable access to Bluetooth.

sections.heading: Runtime Permissions
sections.text:
When you initially log into Cynet 360 mobile application (the app), you are prompted to select the permissions you want to allow. The specific permissions requested depend on the policy configuration in Cynet 360 mobile console and the type of activation chosen.
To modify permissions at any time while using the app, tap the options menu icon and press Settings. Scroll down to the Permissions section and make your changes.
Users receive one or more runtime permission requests for these topics:
Camera access (popup displays if you select a QR code)
Notifications
Local Wi-Fi Network (iOS only)
Allow Location
VPN - Link Verification
Battery Optimization (Android only)
Device Storage (Android only)
Samsung Knox (Android only)
Messages/SMS (Android only)
Bluetooth (Android only)
For a number of dialog messages, the app cannot localize the prompt into another language. The dialog messages are owned by the operating system, and in some instances, these dialogs are always displayed in English. The following sections cover the different types of permissions that are requested. For more information on data privacy, refer to the Related Documentation section.
If during activation, you select to activate the app with a QR code, the app requests camera access. Press OK to scan your QR code for activation to begin.
Administrators can set notifications in the console to automatically alert users in response to certain threats or other activities. In iOS, users toggle the Notifications permission setting on, then press Allow when prompted to let the app send these notifications.
At least one of the threats in the Set User Alerts column in the console (shown in the following image) must be checked for the Notification permissions to display in the app. For more information, see Threat Policy for console documentation.
To protect the device against sophisticated Wi-Fi-based network attacks, the app must have permission to access the local network. This helps protect your device against network attacks and access to private data.
To access Wi-Fi details, the app needs location permissions. The app requires these Wi-Fi details to protect the device and data from sophisticated network attacks.
Location permission must be set to Always for app features to work.
When a security event occurs, the app gathers forensic information. Part of this information includes the location of the device when the event occurred. This is a standard warning for any application that has location service functionality built in. The app does not keep a history of your location information.
There are different flows for setting these permissions in iOS and Android. Each one is described next.
iOS
Follow these steps to set location permissions for iOS:
When you initially launch the app and see the Permissions screen, toggle the Allow Location permission to On. This permissions screen pops up. Be sure to select one of the highlighted options to allow the permissions. Please do not select Don’t Allow.
When you make a selection, this screen displays. Select Change to Always Allow.
The location permission is required to get the SSID and BSSID of the current Wi-Fi connection. The location is not stored in the app.
Android
Follow these steps to set location permissions for Android:
When you initially launch the app and see the Permissions screen, toggle the Allow Location permission to On. This permissions screen pops up. Be sure to select one of the Allow options. Please do not select Don’t Allow.
When you return to the permissions screen, the Allow Location permission will still appear toggled off. This is because it needs to be set to Always, which is described next.
Re-toggle the Allow Location permission on. This time, the following screen displays:
Click Open Settings to display this screen.
Make sure Location is set to Always, as shown in this image.
Now, when you go back to the app, the Allow Location permission is toggled on.
The location permission is required to get the SSID and BSSID of the current Wi-Fi connection. The location is not stored in the app.
With Android 10, location permission is required to detect an unsecured Wi-Fi threat.
An administrator has the option to configure the app to set up a VPN in response to threats. If this is requested, then during the installation of the app, a VPN profile is installed and can be seen in the Settings app of the iOS device. The app requests permissions from the user or, in other configurations, the permissions are automatically granted with the activation. If applicable, the app guides the user when installing the application or when policies change for the device.
This permission is requested when an administrator:
Enables a phishing policy for a local VPN.
Enables the enhanced phishing protection plus web content filtering option.
These VPN Link Verification permission screens do not display if the administrator did not configure a local VPN on the console with phishing or network sinkhole settings.
To allow adding the app to the Android battery optimization exemption list, press Allow to ensure devices are protected while the app is running in the background. This permission prompt is controlled by the administrator’s settings on the console. See the console documentation described in the Related Documentation section for more information.
To allow the app to access your device storage areas to detect and prevent malware from harming your device and data, press Allow to ensure devices can read these storage areas.
To allow the app to use Samsung Knox functionality to protect your device from mobile threats, press Activate to ensure the app has the ability to behave as a device admin app.
Granting this permission can require a user to go to Settings on the device to uninstall the app.
To allow the app to protect you against phishing links contained in your SMS messages, press Allow to enable access to those messages.
To allow the app to detect unknown tag trackers that may be tracking your location without your knowledge, press Allow to enable access to Bluetooth.

title: Troubleshooting
content:
When you select Troubleshooting from the options menu , this screen initially displays. This is where you can select to have debug logs sent to your email address.

Send Logs for Debugging

Troubleshooting allows the user to view and share the Cynet 360 Mobile application log information. Perform these steps to set this up:
From the Troubleshoot screen, press Send logs for debugging to display this screen.
Enter your email address and press Send Logs . A confirmation message indicates that your logs have been sent.

Advanced Troubleshooting

From the Advanced Troubleshooting screen, you can access advanced details, all logs, and local VPN settings. You can also reset the app or clear all logs.
To display this screen, long-press Troubleshoot in the options menu .
The details of the Advanced Troubleshooting section are subject to change between releases or patch releases.
From the Advanced Troubleshooting screen, press Advanced Details to display the information shown in this figure. This screenshot contains only a portion of the Advanced Details screen; you can scroll through it to view all of the information, which includes:
Bundle ID
App
Build number
App version
SDK build
SDK version
Release version
Embedded license
Device ID
MDM ID
Acceptor URL
From the Advanced Troubleshooting screen, press All Logs to display the options shown in this example.
No personal information is collected or shared through these logs.
From the Advanced Troubleshooting screen, press Local VPN Settings to display the information shown in this figure.
Press this option on the Advanced Troubleshooting screen to clear any login token information and start the app over. This restores the app to its default settings and takes you back to the initial screen where you press Get Started to activate the app.
Press this option on the Advanced Troubleshooting screen to clear all logs. This action may impact your app statistics. When you are prompted, press Clear all logs to continue.

title: Mobile Dashboard
content:
The first screen that is displayed after the activation is the dashboard. The area at the top of the dashboard provides a summary of the status. Each tile is color-coded based on the status of that particular category, as shown in this example.
The status colors are explained below:
Each tile represents a specific category for threat detection:
Apps scans for potentially harmful applications.
Web checks for phishing links and enables and disables VPN depending on the administrator's settings in the console.
Device scans for potential threats or risks to the device, such as outdated OS version or update required.
Network identifies and protects from threats to the networks.
Threat Zones provide information to the user if nearby available networks should be avoided (high-risk networks).
Tutorials contain links to helpful information on various topics.
Tag Tracker (Android only) scans for tag trackers that may be tracking your location without your knowledge.
Threats are defined by the Cynet 360 Mobile console Threat Policy.
You can press any tile to display a list of the active issues detected and view the risks or threats for that category. If, as shown in the prior screenshot, there is an issue to fix, you can press Fix to display the Full Event Log (described below).

Viewing the Activity Report

From the dashboard, press the Report button to display the Activity Report shown here. This report displays statistics for the Device, Network, App, and Web categories, including the number of security scans, threats, and issues resolved or websites blocked during the specified time range shown at the bottom of the screen.
You can tap on a row to display the corresponding log page for that threat category and view the events in more detail as shown in this example.

Notifications

Notifications are part of the threat policy that is pushed to the Cynet 360 Mobile app from the console. For each threat, this defines whether the app notifies the user. Each classification in the threat policy has an option to notify the user and the console administrator configures this as enabled or disabled. When an event is detected, the app communicates with iOS or Android and the notification appears on the device.
There are two types of notifications: banners and popups. Tapping the notification launches the app and provides the user with additional information. It also includes recommended actions for the event.

Options Menu

Tap the icon in the upper-right corner of the dashboard to display these options.
The options that are available depend on the policies and console settings.
Full Event Log (see below): A comprehensive list of the threat log items.
Settings (see below): These are settings for the user's device display, such as the number of days for statistics, and dark or light appearance.
Troubleshoot : Allows you to send log and debugging information to the Customer Support team. You can also access the Advanced Details screen from the Advanced Troubleshooting screen that displays when you long press the Troubleshoot option.
About: Displays information about the current version of Cynet 360 Mobile application, including the privacy policy and the license agreement.

Full Event Log

The Full Event Log is available from the options menu and provides a list of all threats detected on the device. If a risky site is detected, you will see a message that the site is blocked. The event log also displays a description of the threat and the date/time that it was detected. You can scroll through this log.
The active issues display at the top of the threat list. Once a threat is mitigated, it moves to the bottom of the list under Resolved Issues . You can press the trash can icon to remove all the resolved threats.
You can click Fix for any active issue to view details, resolution recommendations, and the URL and category for the site. You can enable the option to Mark this site as “Trusted” (shown in the next image) if you want to skip the alerts. Click OK when you are finished.

User Controlled Settings

If you select Settings from the options menu , this screen displays. Scroll down to see all the information.
You can view and edit new settings for these topics:
Dashboard Stats – Allows you to select the preferred day range to view statistics for the app dashboard.
Appearance – Allows you to select the display for the app, such as light, dark, or the system appearance mode.
Permissions – Allows you to review and reset the permissions that the app requests to provide continuous protection. Permissions include Notifications, Link Verification, Local WiFi Network, and Allow Location.

title: Application Threat Protection
content:
In the dashboard, the Apps tile displays the status with green, yellow, or red indicating if no threats are detected, if risks are detected, or if threats are detected. Tapping on this tile takes the user to the Apps screen.
If a risky app is found, the screen provides the user with information on the application and a recommendation on how to proceed. Categories for risky apps include:
Suspicious apps are installed and are high risk. They have the potential to compromise the device.
Sideloaded apps were installed outside of the Google Play Store or App Store. They have not been officially validated and are considered risky.
Out of Compliance (OOC) apps, which have characteristics that do not comply with the organization’s privacy and security policies.
iOS will only provide access to the apps if Cynet 360 Mobile is used with an MDM.
This figure shows an Android example with a sideloaded threat.
The critical threats are defined by the Threat Policy .

Application Scanning

Cynet 360 mobile application automatically scans the device for risky apps when it is initially installed. Apps are also scanned when they are downloaded and installed.
iOS will only provide access to the apps if Cynet 360 Mobile is used with an MDM.
Here is a screen where a risky app has been detected.
Press the screen to display a list of the apps with issues.
You can press Fix if you want to display the details and recommendations. An example is shown next.

Searching Apps to Determine Risk

When users want to install an app on their device, but want assurance that the app is safe, they can search for the app from a comprehensive database. This displays the summarized privacy and security rating of the app, which helps users determine if it poses a threat to the device. For Android devices, you can also view the app risk report for your currently installed apps. Searches can be performed for non- English languages as well. This feature is not dependent on a license.
Within the Apps tile, you can press the App Look Up link (available if the App Risk Lookup feature is enabled in the console).
Follow these steps to search for an app risk report:
Press App Look Up on the Apps tile.
Search for an app name.
Tap the app name to view the privacy and security risk report for the app.
Follow these steps to view the app risk report for your installed apps (Android only):
Press Installed Apps on the Apps tile. This displays a list of apps installed on the device.
Search for and select an app.
Tap the app name to view the privacy and security risk report for the app.

Difference for Allow-Listed Apps on Cynet 360 Mobile Console

Here is information on how the apps that are allow-listed in the console show up in the App Store Search view.
For apps installed on the device, the app is flagged as “Trusted App” and the message “<App name> has been reviewed by your company and is approved for you to use” is displayed.
For apps from Play Store, MTD does not look for apps that are allowed and displays the app risk along with privacy and security risk levels.
Apps installed on the device cannot be listed/scanned on iOS.
For apps from the App Store, MTD does not look for apps that are allowed and displays the app risk along with privacy and security risk levels.

Uninstalling Apps (iOS only)

To uninstall an app on your iOS device, long press the app icon and select Remove App . Or, you can long press the background and a minus (-) icon displays beside each app. Click that icon to uninstall the app.

title: Web Threat Protection
content:
Cynet 360 Mobile application checks for phishing links and enables and disables VPN connections given the policy settings on zConsole. It uses Web content filtering to warn and protect users from accessing possibly harmful websites and links, such as malware, phishing, botnets, and suspected domains. MTD also provides alternatives for users to be protected from risky phishing links in SMS/MMS protection and the Safari browser on iOS without setting up the VPN.
When the user accesses a communications channel that is not secure, MTD can open a secure VPN for transmitting data. The administrator can also allow the device user to enable or disable the MTD local VPN on their own device.
You can specify a new content filtering category action to block and optionally create an alert. This feature is beneficial in expanding the device detections in filtering and alerting for websites that have specific content. It allows you to block content with or without generating an alert to the end user or a threat to review in the threat log. You may choose not to create an alert, for example, to block SPAM sites without interrupting the end user’s browsing experience.
In the dashboard, the Web tile displays the status of threat detection (red, yellow, or green). Tapping on this tile takes you to the Web screen.
If you press the Report button, a Site Scan report displays. This report shows the number of sites that are blocked and their URLs, along with the number of safe sites and sites scanned.
The following screen displays when a risky site is detected.
When you click on the threat, the following screen displays. If the administrator sets a site to alert the user instead of blocking that site, options to Continue Anyway and Mark this site as “Trusted ” appear for the user to select if they choose to bypass the warnings in the future.

title: Web Threat Protection - Phishing and Content Policy
content:
The phishing and web content filtering policy allows an administrator to define ways to protect users from accessing any harmful websites and links (such as malware, phishing, botnets, and suspected domains) that contain content that might be risky.
You can specify a new content filtering category action to block and optionally create an alert. Actions that can be set include:
Alert the user giving a warning.
Alert the user giving a warning and create a threat.
Block the site.
Block the site and create a threat.
Block the site and do not create a threat.
When a user is alerted about a website or queries a website to see if it is safe, Cynet 360 Mobile application provides the category of that site, such as gambling or illegal drugs, if the administrator enables content filtering. This guidance helps the user determine what action to take next.
In addition to a standard set of categories, a Cynet 360 Mobile console administrator can create custom categorization lists by entering a list of website URLs. In this way it is possible to create alternative actions for a defined set of websites. Custom lists can be related to device groups to tailor the actions per group. For more information on the categories, see console documentation.
An administrator can set up these features on Cynet 360 Mobile console for devices running apps:
Allow the user to control if there is a local VPN or not.
Block the detected phishing URL or web links or not.
See VPN Link Verification Permissions for information about enabling phishing and content filtering.
Due to encryption protocols for HTTPS URLs/domains, the phishing VPN feature may not display the full HTML content, but the alert warning message still appears.
When a specific website category or URL is determined to be harmful, it is blocked. An alert displays when a site is accessed from the browser, which includes the site and category. The app includes a tutorial on how to use the URL sharing feature.
A device cannot have two VPNs running simultaneously. As a result, when you start a corporate VPN, it disables the app VPN that is used for phishing protection. The app prompts you to re-enable phishing protection after the corporate VPN is turned off.

Checking for Phishing Risk

If this feature is enabled, when you press the Web dashboard tile, the screen shows the protection available for insecure (HTTP) connections. To check a link for phishing risk, you can:
Type or paste a copied link.
Press and scan a QR code. You will be prompted to allow the app to use the camera.
This figure shows the Phishing Link Check field, where you can copy and paste a link or scan a QR code.

Additional Phishing Protection with iOS

Cynet 360 Mobile application offers alternative methods for continuously protecting you from malicious and risky links that are either displayed by the Safari browser or contained in SMS/MMS messages.
The app provides the ability to extend the anti-phishing and web content filtering capabilities to the Safari browser on mobile iOS devices. To do this, the app installs a Safari extension (if the administrator enables this feature). You can then manually enable this extension to give the necessary permissions that allow it to run on browser pages. Please refer to the app tutorial for instructions on enabling this extension.
This feature requires the Phishing Protection policy or the Enhanced Phishing Protection plus Web Content Filtering policy to be enabled in the console. The VPN Link Verification permission is not required for this feature. This feature requires app version 4.22.2 or later.
The app allows you to extend the anti-phishing capabilities to the Messages app on mobile iOS devices. This feature provides an alternative method to continuously protect app users from malicious and risky links in SMS and MMS text messages.
The Tutorials dashboard tile contains a tutorial on enabling this iOS SMS Messages feature.
This feature blocks a risky link for SMS messages in the Messages app before a user can click it, and all blocked messages are viewable in a single tab in the Messages app.
This feature requires app version 4.22.2 or later and does not report threats to the console. It only works with SMS and MMS text messages from unknown senders; it does not work with messages from senders in a user’s Contacts list or with iMessages from any source.
The SMS message detection is done only on the device itself, and no content from SMS messages leaves the device. No personal information is collected from the user’s SMS or MMS text messages, and only the links within the text messages are evaluated for risky sites.
This figure shows the setting when this extension is enabled.
After you enable filtering of the SMS messages, the Messages app places the risky SMS messages in a separate folder. This feature allows you to be more protected from risky and malicious links.
This figure shows the filtered text messages in the separate "Junk" folder.

Additional Phishing Protection with Android

The app allows you to extend the anti-phishing capabilities to the Messages app on mobile Android devices. This feature provides a way to continuously protect users from clicking malicious links in SMS and MMS text messages on their device.
This feature must be enabled in the Phishing or Web Content Filtering policy in the console and the user must accept the Messages permission in Android to allow the app to detect risky links in messages.
This feature detects and alerts a user about a risky link in SMS/MMS messages in the Messages app before a user can click it. The threat details include the phone number of the message that contained the risky link.

Enabling Link Verification

If enabled by the administrator, the user is able to toggle Enable Link Verification on and off as shown in this figure.
If the link is risky, the app displays a screen indicating the status, along with details, resolution, site, and category. You can enable the Mark this site as “Trusted” option to bypass this warning and proceed to the site without being alerted again.

VPN Protection

When you enable Link Verification on the device, you must allow the app to add the local VPN to the device. The local VPN is used to detect and block risky links on the device. The local VPN can also be used to tunnel unsecured Wi-Fi traffic if configured in the console policy.
Once the VPN configuration is set up on the device, an alert displays when the device encounters an attempt to access an unsecured Wi-Fi network. The app automatically connects the device to a secured VPN to tunnel the insecure (HTTP) traffic over the unsecured Wi-Fi connection.
An example of an alert is shown here.
If you toggle Enable Link Verification on, this popup displays.
The following message displays as confirmation that link verification is enabled.

sections.text:
The phishing and web content filtering policy allows an administrator to define ways to protect users from accessing any harmful websites and links (such as malware, phishing, botnets, and suspected domains) that contain content that might be risky.
You can specify a new content filtering category action to block and optionally create an alert. Actions that can be set include:
Alert the user giving a warning.
Alert the user giving a warning and create a threat.
Block the site.
Block the site and create a threat.
Block the site and do not create a threat.
When a user is alerted about a website or queries a website to see if it is safe, Cynet 360 Mobile application provides the category of that site, such as gambling or illegal drugs, if the administrator enables content filtering. This guidance helps the user determine what action to take next.
In addition to a standard set of categories, a Cynet 360 Mobile console administrator can create custom categorization lists by entering a list of website URLs. In this way it is possible to create alternative actions for a defined set of websites. Custom lists can be related to device groups to tailor the actions per group. For more information on the categories, see console documentation.
An administrator can set up these features on Cynet 360 Mobile console for devices running apps:
Allow the user to control if there is a local VPN or not.
Block the detected phishing URL or web links or not.
See VPN Link Verification Permissions for information about enabling phishing and content filtering.
Due to encryption protocols for HTTPS URLs/domains, the phishing VPN feature may not display the full HTML content, but the alert warning message still appears.
When a specific website category or URL is determined to be harmful, it is blocked. An alert displays when a site is accessed from the browser, which includes the site and category. The app includes a tutorial on how to use the URL sharing feature.
A device cannot have two VPNs running simultaneously. As a result, when you start a corporate VPN, it disables the app VPN that is used for phishing protection. The app prompts you to re-enable phishing protection after the corporate VPN is turned off.

sections.heading: Checking for Phishing Risk
sections.text:
If this feature is enabled, when you press the Web dashboard tile, the screen shows the protection available for insecure (HTTP) connections. To check a link for phishing risk, you can:
Type or paste a copied link.
Press and scan a QR code. You will be prompted to allow the app to use the camera.
This figure shows the Phishing Link Check field, where you can copy and paste a link or scan a QR code.

sections.heading: Additional Phishing Protection with iOS
sections.text:
Cynet 360 Mobile application offers alternative methods for continuously protecting you from malicious and risky links that are either displayed by the Safari browser or contained in SMS/MMS messages.
The app provides the ability to extend the anti-phishing and web content filtering capabilities to the Safari browser on mobile iOS devices. To do this, the app installs a Safari extension (if the administrator enables this feature). You can then manually enable this extension to give the necessary permissions that allow it to run on browser pages. Please refer to the app tutorial for instructions on enabling this extension.
This feature requires the Phishing Protection policy or the Enhanced Phishing Protection plus Web Content Filtering policy to be enabled in the console. The VPN Link Verification permission is not required for this feature. This feature requires app version 4.22.2 or later.
The app allows you to extend the anti-phishing capabilities to the Messages app on mobile iOS devices. This feature provides an alternative method to continuously protect app users from malicious and risky links in SMS and MMS text messages.
The Tutorials dashboard tile contains a tutorial on enabling this iOS SMS Messages feature.
This feature blocks a risky link for SMS messages in the Messages app before a user can click it, and all blocked messages are viewable in a single tab in the Messages app.
This feature requires app version 4.22.2 or later and does not report threats to the console. It only works with SMS and MMS text messages from unknown senders; it does not work with messages from senders in a user’s Contacts list or with iMessages from any source.
The SMS message detection is done only on the device itself, and no content from SMS messages leaves the device. No personal information is collected from the user’s SMS or MMS text messages, and only the links within the text messages are evaluated for risky sites.
This figure shows the setting when this extension is enabled.
After you enable filtering of the SMS messages, the Messages app places the risky SMS messages in a separate folder. This feature allows you to be more protected from risky and malicious links.
This figure shows the filtered text messages in the separate "Junk" folder.

sections.heading: Additional Phishing Protection with Android
sections.text:
The app allows you to extend the anti-phishing capabilities to the Messages app on mobile Android devices. This feature provides a way to continuously protect users from clicking malicious links in SMS and MMS text messages on their device.
This feature must be enabled in the Phishing or Web Content Filtering policy in the console and the user must accept the Messages permission in Android to allow the app to detect risky links in messages.
This feature detects and alerts a user about a risky link in SMS/MMS messages in the Messages app before a user can click it. The threat details include the phone number of the message that contained the risky link.

sections.heading: Enabling Link Verification
sections.text:
If enabled by the administrator, the user is able to toggle Enable Link Verification on and off as shown in this figure.
If the link is risky, the app displays a screen indicating the status, along with details, resolution, site, and category. You can enable the Mark this site as “Trusted” option to bypass this warning and proceed to the site without being alerted again.

sections.heading: VPN Protection
sections.text:
When you enable Link Verification on the device, you must allow the app to add the local VPN to the device. The local VPN is used to detect and block risky links on the device. The local VPN can also be used to tunnel unsecured Wi-Fi traffic if configured in the console policy.
Once the VPN configuration is set up on the device, an alert displays when the device encounters an attempt to access an unsecured Wi-Fi network. The app automatically connects the device to a secured VPN to tunnel the insecure (HTTP) traffic over the unsecured Wi-Fi connection.
An example of an alert is shown here.
If you toggle Enable Link Verification on, this popup displays.
The following message displays as confirmation that link verification is enabled.

title: Device Threat Protection
content:
In the dashboard, the Device tile displays the current state of the device itself. Examples of threats that show up in this category include:
App Tampering
Device Jailbroken / Rooted
File System Changed
For more information about threats, refer to the console Threat Reference Guide.
Tapping on this category takes the user to the Device page. If there are any critical events or risks detected, the details and Yes/No values show the items that are issues.
Cynet 360 Mobile application color codes the screens to help you quickly identify the severity of issues. If a critical threat is detected as defined by the Threat Policy , the title bar changes to red. A yellow title bar indicates that a risk was detected. Green indicates that no risks or threats were detected.
If any of the detections are set to ‘Yes’, you can tap the down arrow to get recommendations and more information on the item.
Some devices are not able to display the full page due to their size. In this case, the user must scroll down the page to view the remaining items.
These figures show the Device screen with risk detected (yellow) and threat detected (red). If you press Fix, a screen with details and resolution displays.

title: Network Threat Protection
content:
In the dashboard, the Network tile displays the current state of the network or Wi-Fi connection. Examples of threats that show up in this category include:
Compromised Network
Danger Zone Connected
MITM (Man in the Middle)
Rogue Access Point
SSL/TLS Downgrade
Once a critical threat is detected as defined by the Threat Policy, the title bar changes to red. A yellow title bar indicates that a risk has been detected.
Tapping on this category takes you to the Network screen, which displays an overview of the device network configuration, including the device’s IP address along with the currently connected SSID and its BSSID.
If there are any critical events, the title bar is yellow and the display title changes to Risk Detected . Recommendations for the user to stay safe are displayed as appropriate for the critical event detected.
If a device is connected to an unsecured Wi-Fi and Cynet 360 Mobile is installed and activated afterward, sometimes no threat occurs for that existing Wi-Fi connection. If the Wi-Fi connection is dropped and a reconnection is performed, you will be notified of the threat.
Here are some examples of the Network screens: one with threats (yellow) and the other without them (green).
